I signed up for Simple Mobile because they advertise a $40 unlimited talk, text, and "up to 3G" web plan.  After I signed up and paid for one month, I learned that they throttle their data on that plan to 119 kbs.  3G data typically runs between 500 kb/s and 3 gb/s.  2G data, on the other hand, can go up to 150 kb/s.  So, their "up to 3G data" is actually a 2G data plan!When I called their corporate HQ, they admitted that they throttle data, but claimed that 119 kb/s is at the "low end of 3G."  When I asked for a refund, they refused.  I ultimately disputed the charges on my credit card and received a full refund.My advice:  Don't trust Simple Mobile.Update:  Since this review was first published, Simple Mobile was acquired by American Movil, which also operates Tracfone Wireless, Net10, and Straight Talk Wireless.  They've since changed their policies very slightly.  The $40 plan is now throttled to 230 kb/s.

NT63T Stylus Pens (1 Black, 1 White)Both Pens' spring-loaded button atop the stylus to lower the mesh wire tip work very smoothly, and I like that the Mesh tip can be retracted to protect the structural integrity and longevity of the stylus thru repeated use during travel, storage in Briefcases, pad-folios, iPad cases.I have used the stylus's on:1) iPhone 5s, 5 and 4s - iPhones respond very sensitively to the stylus's, with the iPhone 4s having a tech armor screen protector, the iPhone 5 having a Belkin screen protector, and the iPhone 5s having a Tech Armor screen protector AND the NewTrent Rugged iPhone 5 case.Regardless, the screen sensitively to the stylus was very representative of using my finger - Great responsiveness!2) iPad Air, iPad 2nd and 3rd Generation - all iPad's screens also demonstrated superb sensitivity to the NewTrent Stylus, with the iPad 2nd and 3rd Gen having Tech Armor Screen Protectors and Griffin Military Protective Cases (no diminished sensitivity, a positive to both you and Griffin!). The iPad Air has a Tech Armor Protective Screen cover and the NewTrent Rugged Keyboard Case, and was equally responsive to the Stylus. Nice!APPEARANCE AND CONSTRUCTIONBoth the Black and the White stylus are very cleanly and sleekly designed, I was asked while working with it a Starbucks this past weekend who made/designed it and where did I buy it - I know it is only one instance, but you are on the money with the design!Construction appears to be very sturdy, and the spring loaded button atop the stylus feels very stout - great for durability.Overall, I rate the stylus a "5" out of "5". My only constructive criticism is that having a pen function adjoined to the current design would make it for one less need for a pen - though I could argue that by virtue of running a virtually paperless business and of having a stylus (and presumably an iPhone/iPad on me in tandem), I would not have any regular need for a pen! (Note - approx. 2yrs ago, I bought your stylus/Pen combo, and love it too - thought it is noticeably smaller and lighter than this stylus model).

What you need is Pogo Stylus. The Pogo Stylus is an ultra-light aluminum stylus with a soft tip that allows electrical charges to transfer from your hand to the screen. Without cutting your nails or taking off gloves, you can use this stylus to select icons, answer calls, write email and text messages, play games, "draw" on the screen, or surf the web.Personally, I don't have long nails, but I do have trouble hitting the right letters on my iPhone. The Pogo Stylus makes it much easier to get it right the first time. In addition, you don't have to worry about scratches or finger smudges, either.I love mine and it works perfectly. You don't have to SMASH it on the screen to get it to work. (As a matter of fact, pressing too hard will mess up your tip.)  Just tap lightly with the full face of the tip and you'll have no trouble at all.My only wish is that there were a way to attach it to my Mophie Juice Pack AirMophie Juice Pack Air (Red) for iPhone 3G & 3Gs. The clip that comes with the stylus fits over the iPhone alone. There are clips available that fit over a variety of other iPhone cases, but not the Mophie. For now, I have to carry it in my wallet. I paired this headset to my iPhone right out of the box. It comes fully charged, which is easy to appreciate.First I'll speak to the quality. The headset fits very comfortably behind the ear and actually feels pretty good. I've used other Bluetooth earpieces and I've experienced discomfort, pain in the ear, low quality sound and poor signal quality. The Motorola Elite gave me none of those things. I called my wife's phone and spoke to her. She never even knew I was on a Bluetooth. I then walked way across the house, away from my phone and the signal never waivered. The sound quality I received was unexpectedly good; very clear, nice and loud, very good quality.It charges while resting in its case. It fits snugly in the case and easily fits into a pocket until it's ready for use. When turning the unit on, it announces the number of hours of talk time available, which is a nice high number when charged. Press the button on the bottom of the unity and speak the name of the person from your contact list and your phone dials the number. Say "hang up" and the phone hangs up. It's the one hands-free device that's truly hands-free (except for turning it on).All said, this is a high quality headset with advanced features and is user-friendly. Expect big things from this little item.

I did not have much success with this product. The price is certainly good, to the point where I'm not even sure how they can get them to my door for the amount of money paid, much less manufacture and package them. I'm pretty sure these are drop-shipped direct from China, because mine took about four weeks to arrive. (And speaking of dropping, I dropped my phone twice in that span - you might want to order ahead if you know you're going to be getting an iPhone and decide to purchase these covers. But, keep reading.) The instructions for application were cryptic at best, but once I figured out how to remove the plastic sheets from the cover on both sides, I was okay. The lint-free cloth provided, however, did not do a very good job. I screwed up my first cover by pulling off the plastic too soon. The second one had too many dust particles and a bunch of bubbles. The third one was serviceable, but it wasn't completely sticky at the bottom. After about two months, the non-adhesion area grew to about one-third of the surface of the phone. I pulled it off and went withBelkin Clear Screen Protector for Apple iPhone 4S (3 Pack). If you get lucky and get a sticky cover and get the dust off your phone, these will probably work. Still, you'll have to get lucky, so I would say if you're a gambler and don't mind waiting a month for your product to arrive, these are fine or at least, give good cost performance. Otherwise, try the Belkin or something else.

After a week of use:I still really like this phone and stand by my 5 star rating, here's some things I've noticed after a week of use:The Screen- It's 720p but honestly it's a non issue, using larger 1080p screens for the past 2 yrs I don't notice a difference I would think Sony will stay with 720p for the Z2 compact and up it to a 4.5in screen.Battery life: If you have 4.3 out the box I wouldnt recommend updating it to KitKat the battery life just isnt as good it will still last you a day to a day in a half on normal use you should be able to get 2 days with normal use, it really depends on how much you value battery life.Screen Size: This is probably the part I'm most torn about, while I love the size when I turn on the phone I can't help to think the screen is just a tad bit small, I wish the side bezels were thinner something that will most likely be addressed on the Z2c. The issue is the on screen buttons take a good amount of screen space while not an issue on the home screen since they are transparent when you open apps they become black and take up valuable screen space making it smaller. May not be an issue to some but just wanted to point it out, if you're coming from a iphone or something similar it shouldnt be an issue but coming form a G2 with a 5.2in screen there are times where I felt the screen was a bit small.Phone Speaker: It's okay, it sounds a bit muffled to me I wish it was more clear volume is okay but overall it's pretty average.Headphone SQ: Very good not quite as good as the Z2 but you shouldn't have any complaints.Software: While Sonys skin is nice there is a lot of bloat for an unlocked phone, it annoys me that there's so much Sony / Xperia junk on here that you cant take off. I have a sony bluetooth headset and it forces me to download the app in order for me to use it, it also appears in the notification tray even after you disconnect it and turn it off the only way to remove it is to go to app info and click force stop. May not be an issue again but just wanted to point it out.Call Quality: Havent used this phone too much for calls about 2 hrs total? But havent had any complaints, audio quality through the phone is just average could be better could be worse.Overall I'll stay with my 5 star rating since this is the only phone of it's kind in this form factor I'm happy with it 80% of the time and the other 20% the grips bother me. The Z2c should be out soon if you want to wait for that hopefully within the next few months but once again it will probably command a hefty price tag like the Z2 around 650-700 at launch in that case the Z1c will be cheaper and I'd probably still recommend the Z1c in terms of value.  If you can afford it I'd wait for the Z2c and see what they come up with but if you chose the Z1c you will most likely be happy with it.Update: Reordered it New Z1c Impressions:I ordered the Z2 and while it's an awesome phone I felt as if it was too big barely I may get it again when the price goes down but as of now it's too big for me sometimes for everyday use...So I decided to give the Z1c another try and wow a lot of my impressions have changed.I'm pretty sure I got a different region this time as the box was different this one had the Brazil Fifa soccer logo on the back the 1st one didn't as far as I can tell the screen on this one is better, looks less washed out (Sony as well as other manufactures are known to use different LCD panels). The build quality also seems better on this version, the back doesnt feel as plasticy or cheap it feel much more premium. This makes sense since some people experience flash bleed issues when taking pictures due to the plastic backing where as some people don't the only explanation is that sony uses different plastic on some regions phones than others.These were two of my main gripes with the phone and makes the $450 price tag a bit more reasonable, if you're not happy with the version you get I suggest you return it and reorder it you may get a different regions and be happier. The Z1c right now fills niche that no other phone can fill there is no other "compact phone" that has flagship specs. Is the phone perfect no, the Z2 compact will most likely be my perfect phone I cant wait for it to come out...but it will most likely be priced similar the Z2 which is 650-700 initially so it will be a while until the price comes down in which I don't think it's worth the price difference. Overall I am really glad I decided to order the phone again I guess 2nd times the charm!Also side note: I'm not sure if I'm going to upgrade to kitkat some users have reported worse battery life after upgrading to kitkat while some have no once again people think it could be some regions phones. There's a thread on XDA worth checking out Sony is supposed to release an update next month, I also noticed this with the 1st phone I had where I was pretty disappointed with battery life after reading all the glowing reviews (that phone had kitkat preinstalled already)I've been following the Z1 compact for a while and finally decided to give it a shot once amazon had prime for it, I have to say there's a lot that Sony got right with this phone and a few things that they didnt. I've used the Z1 and felt it was too big and am currently using the LG G2, I wanted something smaller and more pocketable so I gave the Z1c a try. I'm sure everyone's watched and read reviews so I'll skip all that stuff and go straight to what I noticed.The screen, while it is improved it still isn't up to par with the G2, HTC One or S4 for that matter for some reason colors look a little washed out to me. While it is a good screen, I would say it's not on the level as the top tier phone (or last years now).The camera, the camera takes good outdoor pictures but just okay indoor ones I used the superior auto mode but honestly was a little disappointed with the camera, I use auto since 99% of the time I dont care to mess around the manual settings.Build Quality- This is probably the biggest disappointment for me, using the Z1 I was impressed by the build quality it felt premium and solid the Z1c on the other hand feels some what cheap (imo). The front feels hollow it's hard to explain but it almost feels plasticy the Z1 and HTC one have a harder screen that feels like glass, if you've used the iPad Air the front feels like that. The back is also really disappointing, Sony opted to use plastic instead of glass not sure why since the Z1 was glass and it was one of the 1st things I noticed. If this is supposed to be a no compromise smaller flagship it wouldv'e been nice to have the same build quality as the Z1, while it does look nice the in hand feel felt a little cheap to me but once again that's just my opinion.Battery life- Coming from the G2 and reading the reviews I expected really good battery life the phone has KitKat now and maybe that may contribute to the battery life not meeting my expectations but I would say it's good but not as good as my G2. I didnt have it on the charger and sleep for about 7 hrs I woke up with it dropping 10%. Hopefully it's in new phone mode right now and the battery improves over time but as of now I would say that battery is good not great, it was at 90% at 9 am now its at 67 at 1 pm I was in an area with bad signal so that has alot to do with it was well but ill update this after more time.SIgnal strength- I'm using Tmobile and while coverage isnt the best there's areas where I have less bars then I would have with my G2, not a major deal breaker but could be an issue depending where you live.Overall I will most likely return it I paid around 460 for it and I don't feel like it's quite worth the money, if it was at a lower price point around 350 then I might keep it but for now there's too many other better options in terms of full size phones I can get for that amount. It's a good phone but I would suggest waiting for the Z2 compact and hope that they address some of the issues.

Short story, i was getting a Samsung S4 and I knew it had bluetooth version 4.0 and decided to try and get a 4.0 bluetooth earpiece. Well that was a short search, I never found one from a reputable manufacturer that I trust so I just transitioned my search for the best one period(regardless of BT version) to date. After owning and losing/breaking over 15 various bluetooth headsets I am quite picky when purchasing one. After a 48 hour exhausting blur of reviews/videos/forum reading I settled on the Jawbone ERA Shadowbox, and couldn't be happier...well unless it had a mute function :-) . This headset might be an all time great in this category, small, light,quick charging,flawless audio,painlessly update-able via the Jawbone My Talk program, Tons of usable innovation that perform flawlessly and incredible noise cancellation with a 5.5 hour talk time and an incredible amount of fitting options included. After updating and pairing it with my S4, I called my wife, and her first words were wow you sound so loud and clear i think you have no need for a headset with this phone. My car has a bluetooth in-car system and my wife feels it is lacking for what the option cost. My wife had no idea i even bought one yet and had no idea i was using it. If after her countless derogatory comments of every other headsets call quality, this in itself proves Jawbones claims of this being it's flagship device. Over two weeks, i have used it everyday(phone calls,Spotify/Slacker streaming,Youtube,Voice SMS and email) without fail,and never have had one issue of any type(a first for me) . Truly an Amazing Device(still wish it had a mute function :-) ), yet it is so rock solid i dropped it on a 100 degree day on a hot asphalt from 6 feet high while putting it in my ear and it never unpaired or even got a nick.  A+++ get it without hesitation..highly recommend!

Where I go my iPad goes  (I sometimes feel it should simply be grafted to my arm)  and because I spend a fair amount of time in the car I often find myself low on battery life at about the same time as the phones also need to be charged.  Over the last several months I've received several external battery chargers to test from  different manufacturers.  TheRAVPower xRobot Premium Li-Polymer External Battery Charger Power Bankis the latest.Before I actually started playing with this I thought that I would quite like this all-in-one design, particularly since you don't need to carry extra cables with you.  This is also the only unit I've tested where I could recharge two devices at the same time.  However, I found some distinct drawbacks.First, the cables are quite short.  The USB Input charging cable is so short that I found positioning the device for recharging problematic.  It doesn't quite rest well when plugged into the USB port on my iMac and when plugged into a wall charger simply hangs in midair, putting stress on the charger.  (I solved that problem by plugging it into a wall outlet that has a heating unit directly below it that that the power bank can rest on.)Similarly, the very short Apple connection (just about 1 inch) makes it almost impossible to actually use the iPad while it is charging.  The cable is too short to bend sufficiently to allow the iPad to be used in a landscape position with the power bank resting on some surface and the power bank is too heavy to simply hang off the back while the iPad is in the portrait position.  I tried to get around this distinct drawback by using my Apple USB cord that came with the iPad, plugging it into the USB output port on theRAVPower xRobot Premium Li-Polymer External Battery Charger Power Bank.  That did not always make a reliable connection.  Sometimes the iPad charged and sometimes it did not.In my testing the unit delivered power to the iPad at a faster rate than other power banks I've tested.  It also took distinctly longer to recharge from empty and became warm while charging the iPad.Grandma's $0.02 - Great for quick recharging on the go, particularly if you need to recharge both a tablet and a phone, but not particularly convenient for continued use of the device while recharging.RecommendedNOTE - I also received the incorrect directions for this device mentioned by another reviewer.  I contacted the company and received the correct insert by email.  They are in the process of correcting the problem.

Use compressed air and tape one of the sides down firmly for perfect alignment without bubbles. I recommend watching some of the highly rated screen protector application videos from youtube. These two steps along with careful and slow application worked well for me.The screen protectors fit well, seem resilient, and don't degrade the screen image.

Screen:This is a great case and a great investment for you iphone!  We use this case to protect our iphone 3gs.  If you have ever put on a screen protector on the screen of a phone or other electronic and you do not get the air bubbles out, that is what the other viewers are describing.  However, after you purchase your case, go to an Apple Store and purchase an, "anti-glare film set for iphone  3G/3GS" screen protector.  The key is "anti-glare"! Ask them to put it on your phone.  The young man took the phone out of the case, sprayed a cleaner on the screen and the otterbox screen.  I still saw the mark so he cleaned it again.  The cloth he used appeared to be the problem so I let him use the cloth that came with my sunglasses and that worked leaving no marks/air bubbles on the screen.  The screen is beautiful.  Do not get confused with the regular screen protectors for the iPhone because we had that on the phone prior to the anti-glare and it did not work because the mark was still on the phone.To sum it up, you will have two screen protectors on your phone, the anti-glare protector as well as the one that is on the otterbox.Model number of sorts is PIA-06E which may be the number for the original iphone as well as the 3g/3gs phone. If you have a 3g or 3gs iphone, make sure the front of the screen protector package reads, 3g/3gs because if you buy it without that specification on the front, then you will probably be buying the screen protector for the original iphone and that protector does not completely cover the 3g phones.You get two protectors that cover the 3gs phone well for 14.95.  It is made by power support.  It is sold on Amazon but as I stated I had great success with the Apple employee making the application.The belt clip/case that comes with the otterbox:In order for the phone to stay in the case, you MUST place the phone in the holder with the screen facing your body and the BACK of the phone facing away from the body.  If I were approaching you, I should be able to see the apple on the back of the phone and not the screen of your phone.  If you do so, there is not a problem with the case and the phone as well as the safety of the phone.Sound:We do not have any problems with hearing the phone ring or any of the sounds that the phone makes.Water protection:I was told from the employee at the apple store that if the phone encased with the otterbox drops in water, if the phone is recovered within 3 seconds, then the odds is that the phone will be okay.  That is, if you have all the openings closed on the phone.  Therefore, it is imperative that the cover openings remain closed on the case at all times.Iphone warranty:If you purchased the extended warranty the warranty extended or not will not cover the phone if the phone is dropped in water, if the screen breaks or if the screen is in a bunch of pieces.  Therefore, purchasing the otterbox is a no brainer!

Yes, that's a bit dense on my part, but someone using this device for the first time, might also be confused.Second, I was hoping to be more impressed with the Jabra headset, because I liked the sleek design so much. The device, however, doesn't fit comfortably around my ear. I'm not sure if it's because my ear is oddly shaped, or if it's related to the device's design. I finally figured out a position to make the device "stay", although the ear piece doesn't fit in my ear canal comfortably.Third, the sound quality is less than desirable. The first two days, my Jabra headpiece had wonderful sound quality, but that soon degenerated into a fuzzy, static speaker quality reminiscent of a 1940s radio broadcast. And this is pretty bad, since my phone doesn't have the best sound quality.Does this headpiece have any pros? Yeah..it looks cool. But, that's about it.I'm really disappointed, because I wanted to like this headpiece. I'll have to search for something else.

I received this unit as a review sample.  I've reviewed Bolse products before and found them to be very high quality.  The first thing I do with any usb charger I get it hook up a variety of devices and test them with a usb current meter.  The high power usb compatibility and output varies widely and without an electric meter to perform actual measurements it's hard to know if you are trickle charging or getting full power.With this model the two iPad/U ports work well with Apple products like you'd expect.  The Samsung/U port works well with Samsung and ASUS smartphones and tables, putting out just over 1W.The reason I'm docking it 3 stars is because the two side ports labeled Universal(U) are terrible.  The label on the device says they should be 1A ports.  I measured around 0.444A with every single device I tried.  For those not familiar with USB 0.5A is the max that a standard USB 2.0 port will put out, and many devices will draw just under that.  It seems to me that in this case they didn't wire up the data pins on these side ports correctly, resulting in all devices failing to sense that they could draw up to 1A and then falling back to the save 0.5A max.  I'm not sure if this is a design flaw with every unit of this model type or if it is a manufacturing defect with my particular unit.  Either way, it's not acceptable.

I just bought two of these batteries last week and had the chance to use them over the weekend. I'm very pleased. They do what they promised. Thanks to PowerIQ, they can charge any device on any port, rather than having to put Apple devices in some ports and Androids in others for best charging efficiency -- I've always felt I was getting only half the use out of those devices. My only wish for these batteries would be for the output to match the number of ports at full speed (2.4 * 3 = 7.2amps out shared among the three ports), as the total amps shared by the three ports is 5.I have an app on my android tablet that tells me what my amps are coming in when I plug the device into a charger. When I've used ports that were designated for Apple, even though the port was rated at 2.1 to 2.4 amps, it would read lower coming in than the Android port that was was listed as 1.0 to 1.2 amps. With these batteries, I get the same amps on every port.I'm also very pleased with the capacity, although I did consider going with a bigger battery. I stayed with these batteries because of their ability to be charged using USB. The larger batteries had to be charged with using direct power. My aim is to be able to use solar panels with USB output to charge the batteries when I travel. It's a lot more versatile to be able to charge the batteries, rather than the devices, using solar panels, with the main point being able to keep my sensitive devices out of the sun when charging. And Anker appears to have the best USB solar panel out there right now -- although it doesn't say it has the PowerIQ ports yet.

I am completely new to the world of Qi wireless charging, and I am so glad that this technology seems to be finally taking off. Juggling various cables and docks for all of my small electronic devices is increasingly becoming a major hassle. As WiFi and Bluetooth are becoming ubiquitous, I am really looking forward to the days when we'll be able to finally sever the last cable that is keeping all of those devices "wired." Qi seems to be the answer to all of those troubles.As far as I know there is only a handful of smartphones that use Qi charging natively. My Samsung Galaxy S3 doesn't have it, but I was able to get a small Qi charging pad that turned it into a Qi enabled device.TechMatte Qi charger is a small device that is very neatly designed. It is very thin, portable, and it uses a micro USB cable for power. The small footprint and the smooth surface of this charger mean that you will have to be very careful when positioning your smartphone directly on top of it for charging. There is not much wiggle room in terms of hitting the charging sweet spot. The little LED light on the side is very helpful, as it tells you when your phone is charging - red is for not charging, and green is for charging.Charging time can take quite a while. This is not a kind of charger that you would want to use in a hurry. However, because of the ease of use of a wireless charger, having your phone charged on an intermittent basis now becomes a very convenient option - just put your phone on top of this charger whenever you are not using it, and you will almost never again have to deal with low battery. That in itself makes this Qi charger a very worthwhile product to have around.

So I bought this back on Jan 6th and got it a few days later. I installed it without issue (second time I've swapped the battery) and it seemed to do ok for about 3 weeks.  After that it started dwindling on its max capacity / charge pretty quickly.  I have my phone jailbroken and I can see the max mAh of the battery.  Anyway, about a week ago it stopped accepting charges from anything more than a 1 watt source - such as my travel charger that's 2100, or my iPad wall charger that puts out 2400.  The phone would start dying around 10-20 % and it wouldn't come back on unless I briefly plugged it in to a charger.  Other times it would go to 1% and last there for hours.With all that said, I finally replaced the battery with a Lenmar battery and so far so good. It's charging as it should and draining at a standard rate.  Also the Lenmar showed 0 cycle counts, this one had like 240 reported when I first put it in the phone.So far it's worth the money and the hassle to get the Lenmar, it was about 20 bucks but hopefully I'll be able to ride it out a year or more with this one.

This particular screen protector is without a doubt the best i have used. I've come across a lot of cases that include free protectors and I always give them a shot but I end up hating them. This particular one works phenomenally!!!Here is the guide to applying the screen protector:1) If you want to put one of these on, the best time is IMMEDIATELY after buying your iPhone. Don't let the salesman/woman take off the plastic covering and skip to STEP 52) If you have already used your phone and now decided that you need a screen protector (as most people are in this boat) gather these supplies:-Rubbing Alcohol (or anything else with a high alcohol content) [simply chemistry, alcohol evaporates quickly]-Lint free cloth (such as silk) [included in this purchase]-Credit Card3) Pour a small amount of your alcohol onto your lint free cloth and liberally clean all surfaces of the phone4) Repeat a few times to make sure all debris is removed from the screen5) Lift the bottom of the film from the backside (side that will be touching your phone) DO NOT TAKE OFF THE WHOLE FILM YET6) Place the bottom part of the cover on your iPhone and align it on the edges of your iPhone7) Use a credit card to smooth out any and all bubbles that may be present8) SLOWLY go back and fourth from using your credit card and removing a SMALL amount of the thin film from the back of the screen protector9) !THIS IS THE STEP MOST PEOPLE MISS!After your installation process is complete, try your best to wait about 30 minutes to an hour to really let to adhesive set. This will prevent any debris from gathering under your screen.If you followed all of those steps, you shouldn't have any problems getting a bubble-free screen protector!What really sets this brand apart from the others is that they don't have that sort of sticky feeling that most screen protectors are plagued with. You know what I'm talking about if you've used your friends iPhone and you have a hard time sliding your finger across the screen. This screen protector does not have that problem at all!Another great feature about this product is that it prevents fingerprints. Most phones come with the coveted olio-phobic coatings that are great for about a day. These protectors, since they are not glass, do not hold fingerprints anywhere near as much as glass does.Also, one thing I have noticed with a lot of screen protectors is that after a few weeks, the edges start to peel off. The screen protector my mom has on her iPhone (something she bought at verizon) gets fingerprints like crazy and puts a nasty hazy glare on her screen and after about 3 weeks, about a third of the protector started to peel away. That alone is proof to me that the quality of a screen protector DOES matter.

The item arrived quickly, was packaged neatly with a carrying case, a USB cable for charging, and instructions.I am using this product paired with an iPhone 4S 16GB phone.PRICE - I paid about $30 for this item, which seemed a bargain compared to the other Bluetooth headset offers on the market.SOUND QUALITY - The headphone sound quality is average or better - good volume, good base, good treble.  These are not Bose or Beats headphones for the premium headphone afficianados, but the sound quality is fine for listening to music / movies on the iPhone, which is what I use it for.  Zero complaints from me, and I would speak up if the sound quality was below average, but it is certainly not.BLUETOOTH - Range is great, quality of connection is great, very very few skips in the audio while connected.BATTERY LIFE - It's hard to say, I fully charged these initially and the battery life hasn't run out yet.  This has to be among the most pleasant surprises for Bluetooth headphones that I paid about $30 for.  So far I've watched a movie, listened to music, and spoken on the phone for a total of 3-5 hours of usage with no need to rechage yet.PHONE QUALITY - Excellent.  I spoke with a family member out of state for about 35 minutes.  I initially told them that I was trying a new headset, and they made a point to tell me that my call was coming through very clearly and it must be a good headset.BUTTON FUNCTIONALITY - Fine.  Buttons are easily identifiable and work - construction and projected durability of the product is average.SUMMARY - Better than average sound, easy pairing / great Bluetooth range / consistent connectivity, quality phone experience, great battery life.  This item has earned a 5 star rating from me and I will likely order one or more additional pairs for spares and for gifts.  Great job Kinivo.

I'm not sure what your daily activities are like, but mine include occasional activities wherein I get a little dirty, or sweat, or ash my hands, or anything that leaves my hands in a state that makes answering the phone or touching a laptop IMPOSSIBLE. As the phone rings on and on, I try to wipe my fingertips on ANYTHING that will dry my fingers so I can answer the phone or move the GPS screen or do anything I need to do NOW. But I can't, and the phone call ends, or we've driven by the turn. Frustration mounts.These capacitive pens can be a savior.Why I'm giving this pen a 5 when it's not especially dazzling or hasn't any secondary function (like a flashlight or a pen cartridge, etc.) is that it sets out to do a job and it does it well. PERIOD.The fiber mesh tip is responsive, seems durable, and the pen is reasonably attractive and fits decently in my hand. What else does it have to do?The tip suited all the kind of writing or tapping that I typically do on a touchscreen, and I tried to mimic other motions others might make if they draw or have different handwriting styles or grips. About the only situation where this pen would have a problem would be if one has a style where their grip puts the pen at an especially acute angle: say, thirty degrees (where the pen gets almost parallel to the page). Other than a really odd angle like that, this pen works great.Anyway, I'm satisfied. And it's attractive enough: one pen is black, the other silver, and both shades are muted and not very glossy, which is fine with me. It even has a little nib with a hole in it at the opposite end that would allow it to be held in a lanyard around your neck (or attached to the device) to enable it to always be on hand.I have a Kindle Fire HD tablet and a Samsung s/5 phone, and have also used these on iPads and an iPhone. All worked equally well.NOTE: I was supplied this product free of charge with no other conditions other than my writing a fair and honest review -- which this is.

--UPDATES: well, these lasted less than two months.  First one of the inserts got lost.  It fell off easily, so it was bound to happen.  Second, the connector sleeve fell apart.  It just came off, and disconnected.  I glued it back, and it worked for a while.  But now, it simply has no sound.  This is terrible quality.  It's not worth 1/10 of the price.Unfortunately, I liked its design a lot.  The following is the original review:The angled design is not a gimmick.  It is an essential feature of these earphones to make them stay securely and comfortably in your ears.The acoustic insulation is modest, which to me is a good thing. Some complain that the phones don't go as deep in the ears as they want for better insulation. But one should be aware that plugging earphones very deep in your ears comes with prices: (1) they harm your hearing if you use them too much, which is physically unhealthy; (2) they make you too much "devoted" to whatever you're listening, a kind of devotion that requires isolation, which is socially unhealthy; and (3) with your ears blocked, they inherently make telephone conversations very uncomfortable because of an unnatural interference between you own voice and the other caller's voice.  All these detriments for better sound.  I say no to that.  So it is a personal choice.  To my own taste, these earphones already stay a little bit too deep in the ear, so I'm glad that they don't go even deeper and tighter.  But I'm glad that they're secure and comfortableThe microphone works, and so far no one has complained when I make calls.  But these "in-ear" phones are a bad choice for making calls, because they all feel very unnatural and uncomfortable when you speak with your ears plugged.  Both you and the other caller would sound strange, and your ears feel uncomfortable (sort of like how you feel if you talk during landing of an airplane). But this problem is not unique to these headphones. They are universal for this type of designs, which are primarily for a listening experience, not for a conversing experience.Cannot comment on its durability.

I was pretty excited to try out this product.  I'm a tech junkie and love trying out new products. The ZOMM seemed like a cool idea - a "leash" for your phone so that when you misplace it, you'll be able to find it.  Couple that with a speakerphone and a panic button, and it seemed like a really good idea.Well, the idea is much better than the actual product. First of all, if you want to use this device, you need to have the bluetooth on your phone turned on at all times. For my phone (Droid Incredible), that cuts the battery life in roughly half. That makes it a major pain to use, as I always need to make sure I have my phone charger with me.The ZOMM works pretty simply - attach it to your keychain, connect it to your phone, and you're set. Walk about 30 feet away from your phone with your ZOMM, and it starts beeping very loudly (and I mean LOUD!). It also functions as a speakerphone. Just press the button on the ZOMM to answer a call and use it as a speakerphone. The quality is nice (great wind reducing technology), but the problem is that it's always connected to my keychain. So when I'm in the car, my keys are obviously in the ignition, which is like 4 feet away from my mouth. So people say they have a hard time hearing me because I'm so far away.It also functions as a panic button.  I tried it once, and it wasn't very loud (the newer versions being shipped now are roughly 4 decibels louder than the original models). It just didn't seem like a very useful feature to me.I know these devices are marketed at busy mothers, which is maybe why I didn't find it to be all that useful. You do need <some> tech savvy to get it working, but not much. I suppose it can be handy for some, but I just found it to be frustrating to use.

No, this is not a lightweight battery in any manner of speaking. Like the Xtreme sports, this is an Xtreme battery. The first thing that came to mind is that this battery is the OtterBox of all battery packs. It's surprisingly tough and if you don't need anything Xtreme, you just might want to take a pass. If you do need it, you definitely have selected the right battery.I put it through the "how rugged are you really?" test. I soaked it and then threw it. No, not a little toss, a throw. It bounced like crazy and rolled like something out of a Nascar accident scene. It hit so hard the door to the ports popped open, but fortunately for me it didn't break off. It is both waterproof and water resistant. Beneath the port door are what I am assuming are silicon inserts that seal the ports when the door is closed.If you are holding the New Trent PowerPak Xtreme in the palm of your hand with the logo to the top right face on the bevel, you will find the power "button" on the lower left side beneath the bevel. The door to the ports is on the center front. The ports are labeled on the port door; the 5V1A is on the right, the 5V2.1V on the left. When you press the button the LED lights go on, but will not remain on. They do not flash, but rather are static.And so, how did my PowerPak Xtreme work after being doused and thrown? Quite well, thank you. I was some impressed. I usually do my informal testing with my iPad, but added an iPhone 4 to see what it could do. A 12000mAh battery pack does have a lot of power and gave an impressive performance. All in all, I was mega-satisfied with this battery pack. Just when you think battery packs can't get any better ... they do.New Trent PowerPak Xtreme performance:I charged both an iPhone 4 and an iPad simultaneously. I didn't charge up the Powerpak, but rather used what charge it came with out of the box. I had four LEDs showing, but that could mean I had anywhere from a 75% to 100% charge.~ iPhone4 charged fully within 90 minutes. It was <10% charged at the start.iPad - 33% charge at start15 minutes - 41% (4 LEDs)30 minutes - 46%45 minutes - 53% (3 LEDs)60 minutes - 58%75 minutes - 64%90 minutes - 69%105 minutes - 74%120 minutes - 80%135 minutes - 85%150 minutes - 89%165 minutes - 93%180 minutes - 97%195 minutes - 100% (3 LEDs)WHAT'S IN THE BOX:~ NT120R PowerPak Xtreme~ Charging cable~ Carbineer with compass~ Velour strap: 6" (15.24 centimeters)~ Quick start guideSPECS:~ Battery type: Li-ion battery~ Life cycle: ~500 cycles~ Input: 5V / 1A~ Output 1: 5V / 1A~ Output 2: 5V / 2.1A~ Capacity: 12000mAh @ 3.7V (44.4Wh)~ Size: 132.6 x 71 x 32.6mm~ Using Temperature: 10 F to 113 F(-10 C to 45 C)~ Charging Time: Approximately 10 hoursDIMENSIONS:5 " L x 2 " W x 1 " D (13.335 x 6.98 x 3.175 centimeters) I am including the top bracket.LED Indicators:~ 1 LED: Up to 25% charged~ 2 LEDs: 25% to 50% charged~ 3 LEDs: 50% to 75% charged~ 4 LEDs: 75% to 100% chargedCOMPATIBILITY:The NT120R is compatible with most Android and iOS devices suc as mobile phones, MP3/MP4s, Smartphones, digital cameras, Bluetooth headsets, tablets, and portable game players.Sample provided for review.

I'm a distance swimmer and my husband is training for a triathlon, and we bring our phones with us when we go to the pool/beach. In addition, we live in Southwest Florida, so we're constantly checking the lightning tracker and weather radars on our phones while we are working out, to keep safe. Until now, there was really no way to guard our phones from the rain, splashes from the pool, or wet hands besides a ziplock bag, which wouldn't offer any sort of protection if there was a sudden downpour or the phone slipped into the water.The UPcase is PERFECT for us- we can slip our phone in, lock the case, and leave it RIGHT at the edge of the pool/water without worrying that it might get wet or fall in. My husband even anchors his UPcase at the end of the lap lane in the pool, and sets his phone not to auto-sleep, so he can watch the clock/radar as he swims each lap. It's perfect for anyone who needs access to their phone while they are in a wet environment. It's even perfect for kids and teenagers- they can use the phone to play games (you can still access the touch interface on smart phones through the plastic) and text while hanging around the pool without worrying that their phone will slip in and get ruined.The only problem we have is that the clips that lock the case closed tend to slip around when the case is opened. That's a small thing, but fussing with them repeatedly is sometimes irritating.As far as being absolute waterproof...First of all, there's really no case can be truly and 100% guaranteed waterproof, except for the ones with interlocking compartments and different rubber seals that lock together. I've been endurance swimming for over 20 years, and as soon as waterproof cases for the walkman came along in 1993, I started buying them, desperate to have something to do while I swam miles and miles and miles. I've destroyed many walkmans, discmans, and a few iPod shuffles in the quest for music in the pool/ocean. The problem is not the cases themselves, but that the water/salt and friction ultimately splits the seams and materials the cases are made of, allowing water to get through and destroy the unit inside.It's only very recently that the cases have NOT failed at some time or another, and those cases come with several different interlocking pieces and required silicone gels to seal the locks, etc. Clearly, this is not a multi-compartment kind of case. What this is, is a splash-proof case for iPhone size electronics and smart phones that offers protection if that case happens to be dropped in the water. Even though this case *is* waterproof, it most likely will not stand up to repeat submersion over time- water gets into pretty much everything.So no, I wouldn't recommend swimming five miles a day with this in a chlorinated pool unless the unit inside in inexpensive. I've seen so many electronics get ruined that even if they did some up with some super fancy waterproof case for my iPhone, I wouldn't use it- eventually water would get in. It's just how it is. But if you just need protection and the case for time-to-time submersion, the UPcase is perfect.

As usual I received this product in exchange for an honest review. Also as usual I give my candid opinions below despite that kindness.My first impression of this unit was that it was incredibly ugly and frankly rather offensive. The unit I received for review is an obnoxious yellow color with a logo and extensive writing printed directly on it asking for positive reviews online. It's also clearly marked as 'Made in China' which is rather perturbing given it's made by a company called '5 Star America'. I certainly have nothing against China but if you're going to wrap yourself in the flag then you're rather obligated to follow through with the patriotism indicated by your name.On the positive side the unit does seem to be relatively effective at its job and the USB cable included is a nice flat one which resists most of the issues USB cables have. The problem comes in when you compare this to the photos here on Amazon. Other reviews have indicated that these review versions are different from the ones you get if you actually make a purchase. The photos show a normal USB cable so I'm rather at a loss as to what to think. If I tried to buy this I just have no idea what I would end up getting.In summary, I'm not entirely sure what to think because I don't know what relationship the product I received has to the one pictured. If they really are completely different as indicated in some other posts then anything I say is somewhat moot. If the unit is as featured in the photos then that's got pluses and minuses. All that having been said, I'd be really cautious spending money with this company. They seek reviews using one product and hope to apply it to an apparently different product. This makes me wary and given the number of other choices out there it's just not worth the risk.

Just got the product (Astro 5600mAh).  So far, it works perfectly.  I mainly purchased this because I no longer drive and my Droid 3 is sort of my electronic Swiss Army knife of devices.  I walk an hour to work each way and even with battery saver, by the end of a full shift, I'm nearly out of battery power. I have an old Sony Reader which I will try with it, but I don't use it much anymore.It came with 3 bars still available and is charging my Droid as I type.  It works well with the included cables (micro-USB) and the generic micro-USB cable as well.  I plan to use it while at work with the Astro in my back pocket charging the phone in the front.  I will also be able to test it in a few weeks with my sister's Blackberry, a Nokia Windows phone of her husband's, and my Mother's iPad mini.  I'll update when I get a chance to test them out.For now, I am very pleased with the product and its workmanship which is very high-quality.  I can see from other feedback that Anker makes customer service a very high priority.  All I can say is I'm impressed both by the product and by a company that evidently really cares about their customers.  A lot of the bigger fish out there could take a lesson from Anker.6/27/13 Update:  After more than a month of usage, I have to say this still is working perfectly.  I have been regularly charging my Motorola Droid 3, Plantronics Bluetooth, and Galaxy Tab 2 7.0.  The only issue I've noted is that when I get down to 2 bars, it will be near the end of its ability to charge.  This just implies that the bars are more exponential rather than linear in nature.  Regardless it still keeps me in charge most of the week before I need to recharge the Anker itself.  I do wish the micro-USB adapter included would seat more securely, but that seems to be more a product of the design of the micro-USB size than anything else.  The cable connection used on the Tab 2 and iDevices now makes a lot of sense.  That connection won't jimmy loose while moving.1/13/2014 Update: Am using this nearly everyday and after 6+ months, it is still holding a full charge. I can use it for charging my phone all week and only recharge it at that time.  This is the best portable charger I have ever used.

I recently purchased an iPhone 5, and prefer a lighter and less bulky case. To provide the phone with a degree of protection I bought this case at the same time just to have something on it, and I must say that I really like the feel and weight of it. The rubberized edges prevent slipping but don't grab aggressively like some other rubberized cases I have seen. This certainly isn't a ruggedized case, but for light use seemed fine for an interim solution. Now for the less than ideal: the case does seem to get debris and dust under it to an unexpected degree (and thanks to the clear back it's quite visible,) It can also trap moisture under its back, which was when I started investigating it a bit more. I had never intended it to be a long term solution, but I looked for another case when I noticed the entrapment issues under the clear back plastic (which is relatively easy to scratch, by the way.) I have read about others having issues with the case harming the paint on their iPhone 5s, but fortunately mine didn't experience that in the limited time I had it on my phone.I think this case has a good design concept, but the execution is less than desirable. As an aside, I never exposed the phone to any adverse conditions: it stays in my pocket most of the day and I do not work in an environment that is exposed to the elements. The moisture issue was by far my biggest concern, and was the ultimate reason I returned the case. Mine spent about two weeks on my iPhone 5, and I am glad I paid attention to its status before any real damage was done by dust or moisture entrapment.

One of the quality products that Anker has been known to be associated with. The total 5A current allows for simultaneous charging of 2 x iPad, 1 x Android Tablet, 2 x iPhone or Android Phone at the same time. Note that as the total current is 5A you will notice that the charging time will be about 20-25% longer than using the original charger due to the current spread.Nevertheless, this product gets our thumbs up for 2013! compared to other brands due to its sleek size and build quality.An interesting point to note for the techie who spends quite a fair bit of time on the road in a vehicle, this product had been connected to an 120V / 240V cigarette lighter inverter and had proven to work to charge all 5 devices in the car. The inverters that was used were the Belkin 240V Car Inverter (200W) and Black & Decker 120V Car Inverter (200W).

I got this for a relative. I set it up and tested it and in the process became familiar with its capabilities.My relative was at home alone, fell and could not get up. It may sound trite and melodramatic, but if you're the person who has fallen, if is frightening - as it is to a relative many miles away.The first thing I checked about 5Star is which cellular network they use.I asked on their Facebook page and had an answer within two hours. Verizon. Had they used Sprint, I would have moved on.Sprint currently has the worst network of all the carriers - and I'm a 12 year customer of Sprint. Sprint is awful.I ordered the unit and tried it out. It works flawlessly. Follow the graphic instructions and you are up and running within a few minutes. The online registration is simple and gives you the opportunity to provide special instructions, such as the location of keys so emergency service responders don't have to break down doors and such.5Star technical support which I called primarily to test it was readily available and well schooled, not only on the product, but network operation as well. 5Star customer support was quick to answer as well. Fortunately we have not had to use the 5Star in an emergency situation - and I frankly hope that remains the case.The GPS is phenomenally accurate right down to the street address. I suspect they use nt only GPS but cell tower triangulation as well.5Star recommends that the battery be charged every night and I agree. You never want to have a dead battery on this kind of device.By the way, you can use this unit not only for summoning emergency help, but for less critical situations as well.Overall, I am please with unit and am very glad my relative has it. Makes me feel that he can summon help when he needs it, no matter where he is. Well worth the modest purchase price and monthly service fee.Jerry

First, let me say I am a gizmo freak, I love electronic toys.  Home automation off my Apple toys are my addiction, so I am always trying new stuff, always searching for new stuff.  These are a brilliant idea. It seems like a good concept.  I tried to like these, but, dang!!  I have six of the stickr trackRs and two wallet trackRs.  First thing is the distance.  I leave these in my bedroom and move the iphone 5s into the livingroom and all of them go off...over and over and over, even though they are turned off inside the app. The next issue is how they kill the battery.  Generally my iphone runs 6-8 hours texting and calling with no problem.  Since I installed these, in three hours my phones battery is at 35%.  This has been a daily occurrence since I installed them.  Even with them supposedly turned off in the app, and my Bluetooth on the phone turned off, they constantly show that they are "connecting".  So the app is running in the background HARD, trying to connect.  The connection range is a real problem with these.  I can leave my phone in my bedroom and move all through my house and still talk clearly with my jabra headset, so I know I have plenty of Bluetooth signal inside my house.  The app says you can connect TEN of these, this is a real problem if you aren't carrying all ten items with you.  If you put one in your wallet and one on your keys and the others in various other items, and leave the house with two of them, the others you left at the house will constantly search for a signal...eating up your battery life.  Also, as soon as you are out of range, they alert, the things themselves beep, incessantly.  Even with all their alerts off.  Which upsets the dog, and irritates everyone still in the house.  None of the eight will sound an alarm on the phone, all it get is a volume icon and a "trackR separated" notification.  I can press the button on the things and nothing sounds on my fone.  I tried a line of sight test at my house and the best straight line I can get is about 25 to 35 feet before they start beeping.  The wallet trackR gives the better signal, probably because they are bigger and may have a better antenna?  The strings that hold the sticker trackRs onto key rings etc (actually good old fashioned phone charm holders available right here on amazon) fit into little notches on the rim of the trackR.  If they get hung up on anything, like inside your pocket,they pull loose.  Great.  Finally had to put a drop of super glue on them to keep the trackR from falling off and wandering off (at least they are easy to find when you press the alert in the app).  Their delivery stinks.  I order two, took them 3 weeks to send me a tracking/shipping number.  I was ok with that, it was in the order instructions.  What got me was they didn't actually make it to the post office until SIX days later...and they showed up at my house another six days later (California to Florida).  Three weeks to ship and two weeks to deliver??  When I wrote the company and voiced my complaints, I got an email back about returning them for an EXCHANGE.  Exchange?  Wow, thanks.  They seem to spend more time marketing than making a product that actually works.  They don't seem to have a lot of focus in what they are doing.  Since they came out with their upcoming BRAVO model I have been inundated with a never ending onslaught of confused offers, counter offers, buy one get one free, buy one, like us here or there, get another free, on and on.  When they announced them, I immediately ordered two.  That was before I found all these problems with this batch.  Of all the iphone add on devices I have tried, I HATE these.  I really wish I had not gotten involved with them.  Usually I buy something, I try it, if I don't like it, ok, I tried it, it didn't work for me, or, I write the company and they work with me to make it work.  Honestly, since I gave up blackberry for apple, I haven't been disappointed in anything, or any company I dealt with.  These things, right from their marketing, design and customer service are horrible, and so easy to hate everything about them, and pretty much everyone associated with them. I have to go to the store, I am deleting the app from my fone and pulling the batteries out of all eight of these stupid things so I can leave peaceably and quietly and not upset the dog. Phone Halo ~ I hate you.

This is a slick, nicely designed (except for the back of the battery) and built battery pack. It is a dead ringer for an iPhone 3, the face is the same size as an iPhone 3 or 4 and it is about the same thickness, the back edges are rounded just like the 3. It can't be beat putting it in my pocket while charging my phone - it mates perfectly with my iPhone 4s.There are 4 pale blue LED's that indicate charge level. They are slightly visible through the face and a bit brighter looking straight on toward the USB port. The box includes a very nice looking microUSB cable to charge the battery.I was able to charge my iPhone 4s from about 20% to full 100% with 2 lights left on. Meaning, I can get almost another half a charge on my iPhone. It does charge an iPad at full high speed (the battery does output 2 Amps). This isn't something you'll want to do regularly; it only boosts the iPad Air about 40%.The battery looks great from the front; it has a great form factor, and does a good job charging. All isn't so wonderful with this battery though. There is this massive logo on the back of the battery that cannot be removed (I guess you could cover it with a SkinIt cover for an iPhone 3). The whole thing is butt ugly - a great big stylized American flag circle, directions in big white type, and 10 white circle logos for cautions. All that would be kind of OK in a roundabout way. But wait, the thing is "Designed for America" but made in China. There is just something wrong with all this flag waving and making things in China. Sorry, the irony is just too great for me. I do understand that every lithium ion battery is made in China; there is no way around that fact. But don't wave the US flag in my face while you do that.There is another small problem, because there is no power switch or test switch on the battery, the only way to tell how much charge is on the battery is to plug it into a device or start charging it with a microUSB cable. Virtually every battery I've seen lately has a button to show the charge level. If I'm grabbing this battery for the day, I darn well want to be certain it is fully charged. Can't do that easily with this battery.In the end, if you don't mind the American flag waving and using a product made in China (every portable battery is made in China), and you don't mind the inconvenience of figuring out the charge level of the battery; this is an excellent battery for charging cell phones. The price honestly can't be beat. When I consider price in the equation this is a much better battery.I was provided a review sample.

.USB battery packs keep your power-crazed smartphone satisfied while on the go.Many brands to choose from. Prices are bouncy and coming down.This Motorola P4000 (4000 mAh) is about perfect if the price drops below $30. Until then, best to check out thePowerGen 6000mAh, or theAnker 4500mAh.I'll update this review if the price gets friendly. (I've set a price alert at camelcamelcamel. Google it and set your own email alerts for any amazon product.)The imbedded Micro-USB cable is a big plus. Save space. No tangle, mess, or leaving behind. I use it with my iPhone, thanks to aLenmar Micro-USB-to-Apple-30-pin Converter Tip.PROs:* Thin and light (4.25 oz, 0.40" thick)* Imbedded cable eliminates multiple hassles- Fit and finish: Good. Looks and feels like quality.- Power-in: Standard Micro-USB connector - 42" cable includedOKs:- 4000 mAh capacity is good for about 1-1/2 iPhone charges (see Note 1)- The imbedded cable is heavy duty. Requires some effort to press into its notch.- 1.5-amp output: Charges some tablets faster than competing models- Textured surfaces are neither glossy nor rubberizedCONs:* Poor value when priced over $30- Listing copy is misleading in places (see Note 2)- - - Notes - - -Note 1: Motorola claims two 90% charges of a "1450 mAh average smartphone battery." That's 1.8 full charges, but my test gave only 1.53 full charges of my iPhone 4S battery (1432 mAh). It charged from 13.8% --> 100% + 29.3% --> 96.0%.Note 2: "Charges the average smartphone battery up to 2 times and powerful enough to charge your tablet" actually means (according to the footnote on the box) it will provide two 91% charges of a typical (1432 mAh) iPhone battery. Most current Android phones have larger batteries. It will charge a Samsung Galaxy S3 (2100 mAh battery) less than 1-1/4 times. It will boost an iPad 3 battery about 25% of one full charge.~~~ Comments & questions welcome ~~~

(which I'm confident that it would last longer than that-I don't plan on toying with the clip all day everyday).****UPDATE: 10/3/2012******It looks like an update of my review is called for. I went to take my phone off my belt so I could prop it up on the desk and the clip broke off :( I contacted the seller (BuyWorld) and they promptly offered me a replacement or refund. (Kudos for the good customer service). I know these things can happen and you'll always have a defect of some sort in mass production, but I can't risk this mortgage payment falling off of my hip while I'm running to catch the train on a snowy winter morning in Chicago, so I'm opting for a refund. When the iPhone 5S came out, there were lines around the block and a long wait, especially for the gold one. My daughter's is gold, and with a traditional case, all that showed of it was the gold ring around the buttons. If you pay for a nice looking phone and want people to see it, this is the case for you.The case is well made and does a good job protecting the phone. The material is flexible enough that it doesn't interfere with pressing buttons at all. The sides are slightly thinner than other cases I have. All similar cases will allow a certain amount of movement if you touch the side portions near the screen, and this allows slightly more. That's neither a positive nor a negative per se, and has nothing to do with the quality of the case. But it did mean that I had to remedy a badly aligned screen protector. The overall thickness is perfect, and will keep the phone from sliding away.The bottom of the case has less open space than some others I have, offering slightly more protection, but potentially less compatibility. This case works fine with standard accessories such as authorized iPhone cables. There are some docking stations that won't work with any case, and this is no exception. But there are some accessories that are in between, such as aftermarket cables. This all boils down to other components not being compatible with Apple specs rather than the case being at fault, but it's something to keep in mind when you buy other accessories. Genuine ones work best.  The AmazonBasics lightning compatible cable's description gives a warning that it might not be compatible with certain cases, and with this one you have to be extra careful to get it to make contact, but you won't have to remove this case to do so. I used it with aZeetron Light Up Lightning USB Cableand had to be extra careful to get things to seat, but the affect was amazing. Since the cable tip lights up, the case transmitted the light much the way a fiber optic cable does, and gave a pleasant red glow while the the phone was charging, which switched to a turquoise glow when the phone was charged. Other people in my daughter's dorm thought that made the case the coolest thing ever.What was more surprising is the reaction it got. I didn't expect that a clear minimalist case would garner many comments, but it proved to be very popular. That was especially true among male college students.Note: I was sent a sample of this case, but no review was promised in exchange. Opinions are my own, but were influenced by the positive reaction this case got from others.

I really wanted to have Bluetooth connectivity in my car without have to buy and install the factory kit. Well, luckily my car has a 3.5 mm audio jack in it, and within a few minutes of searching I came across this beauty. The unit is comprised of 3 parts that are all connected together by (what seems like) miles of wire. 1st there is the 3.5 mm audio cord to plug into your stereo, which is connected to #2 the power adapter, which plugs into you accessory outlet (or cigarette lighter) and has a USB port on it to charge a phone at 1A output (works on my Nexus 4 real well). This then connects, via cable, to the Bluetooth receiver; and this is where the magic happens. The receiver is top notch in my opinion. You stick it to your dash, using either of the 2 round double-sided sticky pads that are included, and then it's time for setup. Just make sure it's all appropriately plugged in, your on the right input for your stereo, and turn on your car. The device enters pairing mode by holding the center button in until the led flashes blue and red. Search for the device with your phone and your done. It reconnects to my phone every time I get into the car (takes maybe 3-5 seconds after powering on the car).The button can be used to play/pause music on your phone, answer/hang up on phone calls, and if you hold it in it will go to google now and listen for your command (no clue if it goes to Siri on iOS). On top of the device there are a forward and back button that will skip songs for you; there is a slight 1 or 2 second delay upon skipping the song until it actually does skip, but it gives you an audible beep that you pressed any of the buttons so that is reassuring that your inputs are being acknowledged.The mic is also attached to main device housing the bluetooth receiver and it is fantastic. I have had very few people say that they are unable to understand what I am saying and there hasn't been any complaints of feedback due to them coming through my car speakers. The only complaints I would get it if I had the windows open and they said that it was windy.Overall, the only complaint that I have for the device is that the cord is quite long (for me anyway), but that can't really be that big of a deal and is a much better problem to have than too short a cord. This is a lovely device that will work for most anybody that has the right setup.

Please people this is No where close to any S3 in your wildest dreams especially after looking at the benchmarks. Marketing Always!It actually performs WORSE than the Samsung Victory and is WAY underpowered even by the S2.I always look at benchmarks first to see what your getting for your money and this one is poor.If you like it fine but don't be fooled by the name.Using Performance Benchmarks lets you see EXACTLY where these cells fit in the power and performance range. You can download the Passmark from Google Play test yourself and run several for an average but make sure all open Apps are closed and reboot once. Then about 15 to 100 people submit these Benchmarks for every phone around and the guys Average them out so they are right on. No Mobile Hotspot:I spent $80 more to get the Samsung Transform Ultra instead of theSamsung Galaxy Prevail Android Smartphone (Boost Mobile)for one main reason: Mobile Hot-Spot. Everything I read talked about the Ultra's "Hotspot" capability. It's actually built-in to Android 2.3, which the Ultra uses. No one seemed to state whether or not Boost had disabled that capability in the phone. Maybe I should have asked for a demo at the store... Alas, the option is not there in settings and if you use a third-party settings manager, the phone says "Hotspot is not supported."I was able to root the phone and installed Barnacle which gives me wifi-tethering in ad-hoc mode. But I paid extra money to get the full hotspot capability. I may have to play with replacing the Boost-crippled Android OS...Touchy touch-screen:Other than the mobile hotspot, the touch screen is way, way too sensitive. The phone tries to make calls when I slip it in my pocket unless I disable the "Emergency Call" button. The phone tries to change modes when my ear brushes the top bar when I'm taking a call.Battery life:The battery life is abysmal. For instance, I took it off the charger about 90 minutes ago. I checked to see if it was my turn to play in Scrabble (it wasn't) and watched about 10 minutes of video on Netflix. This was on Wifi in my house. Other than that usage, the phone has been idle. The battery is now at 65%.When I'm out and about, I am constantly turning on and off wifi, GPS and bluetooth depending on what I need to do at the moment. If I am sitting still for any amount of time, I seek out a wall outlet. You have to use a car charger if you expect to use this as a GPS in the car.Now for some good points:Boost rocks if you are in an area with good coverage. It's cheap and you never have to worry about using too much data. I love it!Android smart phones do so much... Look at my complaints: I was watching video on Netflix, playing Scrabble, using it as a GPS... This is a very functional device. Of course, the Prevail does all this too for quite a bit less money.

I've had about 10 different headsets.  My favorite was a motorola h720, but I cant listen to my ipod on that, no A2DP.  My only real reason for liking that one so much was its awesome volume level.  I work outside and its the only one loud enough to hear in anything but an office environment.  The Voyager Pro+ is a very close second.  And the people on the other end of the call like this headset a LOT better.  The noise canceling is like magic.  I'm a lineman and work on the side of the road.  People on the other end of the call often cant even hear traffic.  One more than one occasion they have asked me why I am yelling into the phone.  I say so you can hear me and they say I hear you just fine.  I've asked - cant you hear the city bus that just drove by and they almost always say no.  And the speaker in the earpiece gets loud enough to hear it.  Its actually loud enough that when inside you have to turn the volume down or it way too loud.  This is honestly the most important thing to me, if its too quiet and I cant hear it what is the difference how good it sounds.  I cant say enough about how great it sounds on the phone.That said I am dissapointed in the A2DP, but I think it may be a limitation of the standard.  Ive had 2 ipod touches and an HTC EVO that I tested and all 3 of them had to be within about 2 feet to work for streaming audio.  This is also a big deal for me, I listen to music all day to keep from going crazy working by myself.  Typically im listening to podcasts, and when they are breaking up I cant tell what's being talked about and it drives me crazy.  However this problem has been there with 4 different A2DP enabled headsets, including 2 voyager pro+'s, and all 3 "phones."  So I have to keep the ipod in my shirt pocket and the headset on my left ear for it to work properly.  I'm 6'2 and my jeans pocket is too far away to work!  Something needs to be done to increase the transmission power on these devices, seriously, 2 feet is not enough range.I've gotten used to this one limitation, and the rest of the features of the device more than make up for it.  One thing that surprises me - I often forget where my phone is in my truck.  When I get out and the phone rings the caller ID comes up over the headset.  Hit the button and you are talking, hit it again and it hangs up.  It also pauses and resumes the ipod, which is paired seperatly from the phone (crappy work phone) without a hitch so the show im listening to waits for the call to end and starts back up.  But the phone portion of the headset works without breaking up from at least 25 feet away.  So if I get out of my truck and go up in the boom (its a bucket truck) I can still answer the phone while it is sitting on the dash of the truck.  So i guess I REALLY dont understand the range ristriction of the A2DP stereaming considering the range of the headset profile.If you are looking for a comfortable, clear, loud, and feature packed headset this is the one.  You just have to get over the look of the boom mic, but if you are concerned about fashion, a bluetooth headset is not really for you in the first place...

The Trident Kraken AMS hard plastic case is very high quality in design and function and it feels that way in the hand. The metal kickstand is the icing on the cake and has come in handy many times.I have a Zagg Invisible Shield screen protector on my Google GSM Galaxy Nexus and it fits perfectly around it. No bunching up of pealing issues because the case is a two-piece top half and bottom half design.The silicon sleeve between the phone and the hard plastic shell serves as a layer of insulation and protects the phone from scuffs and scratches. No part of the plastic shell actually makes contact with the phone (other than the buttons)The volume and power buttons on the phone are replaced by the hard plastic buttons on the case and they feel firm and solid.There are flip out rubber plugs for the earphone and charger ports. Again high quality in design and function.The really good news is unlike most hard cases, the Trident Kraken case is one of the few hard cases that fit the Google GSM Galaxy Nexus with the optional Samsung 2000mah extended battery and it's slightly larger back cover (this does not include the Verizon version with its 2100mah battery).Although the kickstand is metal, it rattles a little in the holder when retracted in the case. This is a common complaint in forums/reviews. It feels like it might rattle out of its retracted position and pop out but it hasn't (yet). If the stand ever does break or wear out, I'll just pry it out of the case altogether. The case itself is so good, the kickstand is just a little bonus.UPDATE 9/10/12: The kickstand still works but no longer stays in the holder after just a few months.  The little contacts that hold it in just aren't thick enough and have worn down. THIS IS AN UNACCEPTABLE DESIGN FLAW! Now the kickstand doesn't stay in the case body. It sticks out from the case and I have to hold it in by hand. I see there are replacement kickstands online BUT they are half the price of the case. I'm not going to buy a new kickstand every 2-3 months.

Everything is relative. I recently evaluated the OXA Juice Box S2 10000mAh Safe External Battery, and loved it with some small issues. The one thing I really liked is that it has a digital readout as to how much juice was left etc., but the more I used it, the less I was interested in the readout: I just looked to see when the device(s) were charged.The S1 has the same quality batteries and though it is 7800mAh rather than 10000mAh, after charging and powering everything from an MP3 player to a bluetooth speaker and Kindle Fire, I must say that I prefer the size of the S1. It is smaller, has rounded edges, and easily fits into a pocket. (It is difficult to explain, but on top of other pluses, it is just more comfortable.) The 4 LEDs on the top readily tells how much power is left in the charger, and it's blinking tells you that it is charging the other unit. Further, unlike the S2 which has the outputs are stamped in black on black, the S1 makes it easy to see which is A1 and which is A2.Remarkably, I used this on a Bluetooth speaker. It powered the speaker for more than 12 hours AND kept the speaker fully charge when I finally gave up my "evaluation." I also charged a Kindle Fire - which I use constantly - in less time than the AC adapter that came with the KF does, and had an MP3 player fully charged from 0 to full charge in less than 90 minutes. These are MY needs, minor though they may seem, and the S1 satisfies them fully.ew
