I recently ordered one of these from Amazon to use in my F150 with my OneX.As always, the service was awesome and shipping too, it even showed up a day early! (Thanks Amazon!)Now for the product itself. It seems quite intuitively designed. The bottom "foot" piece can be shifted up and down about half an inch and from side to side about a quarter inch. This allows for you to get it just right so it wont block your ports. The main joint seems quite sturdy and once tightened, it is snug. The ball joint seems sturdy enough too, but I havent been able to get the tightness of it just right. It seems in either I get it a little too lose and it vibrates a bit on some roads (plus it is mounted in a truck, not a smooth riding Cadi) or I get it a little to stiff so it makes it a little more "involved" to rotate it on its side while driving down the highway. But that isn't a BIG deal because to don't rotate it much.The "one touch" mechanism works as advertised. Plop your phone in the cradle, give it a nudge and the little arms snap in place and hold your phone. The one hand release works nice too, just pinch the side buttons and the arms pop open again.So, why only 4 stars?Although the engineering of his product seems quite solid, the material and build quality seems less so. Its not cheap junk, but the release buttons feel cheap and the action of the gripping mechanism as it pinches and releases has me wondering how long it is going to last. The suction cup is strong, REALLY strong. This is a good, and bad thing. When stuck to my window it is INSANELY strong, It feels like I could hang off of it. However, if you want to remove it, even using the release latch and tab, it takes some doing to get that sucker free. They advertise it as being able to grip those textures panels in your car. I was really looking forward to this as I have the perfect spot on my dash I wanted to put it and I am not too keen on sticking it on the window. I could only get it to stick to the dash for 20 minutes or so before "clunk" the whole thing is on the floor. Bummer. They also advertise this as fitting a OneX, which it does, barely. I do have a thin rubber case on it, but that adds nearly no bulk. The grabbing arms have just enough movement for the lock to engage once the phone is mounted. Also this means that the release buttons are harder to get to so you have to reach under, over, or around the back of your phone to reach them.However, if you want a simple mount that works quite well that IS easy and convenient to use, this is a great choice for the money. Time will tell how long it hold up.

This very attractive Bluetooth Speaker paired and connected very quickly to my Android phone and tablet in seconds.PROS:* It has THREE functions on the bottom of the speaker: Off, MP3 and Bluetooth.***Note that YES you can plug in the attached audio cable into your CD player and it will work to play the CD Player, radio IF you do these two things first:1) Make sure you have NO SD card inserted into this speaker. (Only place the Micro SD card into this speaker when you plan on NOT using the audio cable as it the audio in and out cord will not work when a SD card is plugged in. The MP3 mode works for BOTH the MP3 (SD card) and the AUDIO, but not at the same time.2) Plug in the audio cord, then you turn on the MP3 mode (the MP3 Mode works for both playing the MP3 songs (if you have the SD card (not included with this) plugged in and it works if you just have your audio plug plugged into the speaker and into your CD Player, Radio etc).Note that this speaker has Bluetooth capabilities as well as audio in out (with a cable included) so that you can plug it into your radio, CD player etc when you don’t want to use Bluetooth and just need the speaker part.In addition, it also has a slot for a MICRO SD card (no SD card is included, just the slot for it). Since the manual is small, I am giving you helps here on how to use this should you buy this speaker (see Pros)…In addition, this box contains: In and Out Male to Male audio cable, USB cable for charging (in your computer), speaker and tiny manual. Since the instructions are not that detailed, I included my personal instructions on how to use this with my review (it took me 2 hours to take notes and then write my own, so hopefully this will help you save time and get this going faster).+ The other Pro is that the sound is OK, could be louder. So if you want to increase the sound, press and hold down (for a long time) the + button on the bottom front of the speaker. To lower the sound, press and hold for a long time the – button on the bottom front of the speaker.And I found the loudness of the speaker is MUCH louder in the MP3 mode and not near as loud in the Bluetooth mode or the Audio in out mode (when I use the audio cord to play my CD Player).+ Line In and Out/Portability  – This speaker will work on a CD player, Radio or a laptop (see my above instructions on how to do this). This speaker is small and so easy to travel with.+ Charging - This Bluetooth speaker came with USB Charger cable and charged very quickly (less than 2 hours for a full charge).Charging suggestion – This unit comes with USB charger that you can plug in to a computer to charge the unit. However, since I always have mini USB chargers lying around the house, I used one of my micro chargers and plugged it into the wall to charge. It charged much faster that way. Also, most any brand of micro USB charger should work fine on this unit and it charges very quickly+ Pairing/Connecting – The speaker very quickly paired with my tablet, phone and MP3 player in just seconds!CONS:- Needs to have a separate Audio in and out mode so I don’t have to remove the SD card to plug in and use the audio cord.- Needs to have a higher volume on this for the Bluetooth and audio parts. The volume on the MP3 feature is very loud and clear however.- Has a mini USB charger port instead of the popular macro port that most devices use these days. (But they include the mini USB cable for this).Therefore, my true rating is 4.6 stars as I took off some because of the fact that I have to remove my SD card when I want to use the audio cord and the fact that on Bluetooth and Audio modes, the sound is not as loud as when on the MP3 model.Recommend this because: For the low cost of this speaker, it has many features and it is portable and it has in and out cord, and accepts a micro SD card so you can use this as a music player!

This is probably the third or fourth iPhone screen protector I've used, and I found it to be just as easy to install as any of the others.  Make sure the screen is wiped down REALLY well before you try to apply it.  These screen protectors come with a cloth for that.  There can't be ANY dust on the screen, otherwise you will definitely get a bubble.  The screen protector has 2 pieces of film covering the 2 sides, so you just peel off the first one, line it up with the edge of your iPhone screen, and start applying it slowly from one side of the phone to the other.  The screen protector is cut to EXACTLY the dimensions of the iPhone screen, so there is no room for error when you are lining it up.  Applied carefully enough, there is actually no need for the little mini-squeegee tool that it comes with.The screen protector is made really well, and I have no complaints about the quality.  The anti-glare feature does a great job of reducing glare, even from the sun.  However, this "anti-glare" feature means that the surface is more opaque than the standard screen protectors, and it really messes with the screen clarity.  Imagine if you sprayed a light mist of water on your screen, how it would distort the color of each little pixel on the screen - that is kind of the same effect that this screen protector has, except to a slightly lesser extent.They do a fantastic job of protecting the iPhone screen and reducing glare, but the pixel distortion is just not worth it to me, so I will likely be replacing these with a non-anti-glare version soon.

I don't why it's so difficult to get a car mount for these smart phones, but I thought I'd found the answer here. Unfortunately, I should have seen Room112's video review first, as he quickly nails all the problems.There are a number of major issues with this equipment:1. Unlike the product picture, which leads you to believe the mount is separate from a cord to the cigarette lighter, it's all one solid piece. This means that if your cigarette lighter is in the middle near the stick shift, it's too low to view the phone safely. Alternatively, if the cigarette lighter has an inset mount (like on a Honda CR-V), the phone has to lean forward making it near-impossible to view.2. Because the cigarette lighter provides the support for the entire unit and your phone, it doesn't sit in the socket snugly in either case, wobbles around constantly and falls out, or disconnects without you noticing.3. The flexible neck is too flexible for the weight of the phone (HTC Hero), so it slowly collapses in either position. Given the weight of the Hero, I'm not sure any phone would be light enough to stop this from happening.Conversely, the FM-broadcast feature of this mount works just fine though, as an aside for the HTC Hero, you can't charge and listen to music through the audio jack at the same time. This isn't a fault of this mount, but there's not much point in buying an FM-broadcast mount when you can't charge simultaneously, so make sure your phone has both a USB and audio jack port available.So overall it's pretty disappointing - if anyone else has found a good mount for the Hero, please leave me a comment!

The labeling of the ports could be a little more obvious, but the bottom port is capable of a full 2.1A output. The top (middle) one is limited to 1A, so make sure to plug in your power hungry devices to the one closest to the edge.Overall this worked flawlessly and it is great to have the dual ports for multi-device charging. Another excellent feature is that the plug folds into the device which makes it MUCH easier for transport while traveling and not resulting in the tines getting bent or poking other things. There is a blue LED on the top of the charger as well that provides an indicator of power which is a nice touch.It would be nice if either port would do the high capacity charging, or if the device supported the 2.1A on both ports simultaneously as devices are moving more and more towards this power hungry nature. It's the glass, glue, a handful of tools and that's it. That's all you need. Instructions (and really, really good ones) can be found on YouTube. Better to watch a video than read a set of lazily written sentences on a sheet of paper.Take your time in a few spots - First, taking the glass out. When I repaired a 3G I got impatient trying to remove the glass and pried under the bezel to get it out. That broke the back cover and made the bezel no longer fit. On this 3GS repair, I used the suction cup and that was it. Worked perfectly. Remove the cables in order (not that I know doing it out of order would cause any harm). Take your time using a hair dryer (no need to purchase a heat gun, but if you have one already it would help). This is going to be the most time consuming part, getting the glue loose enough to come off. If you are impatient, you will bend or break the plastic housing that holds the glass to the digitizer. Be sure the three connections are secure before you close the phone up, and you shouldn't have any problems. If your camera, speaker, dock or home button (battery, too) are acting up, now is the time to take care of that as well. The less you play with the connections the better chance of them seating properly.This will take about an hour with a hair dryer, with removing the glue taking by far the most amount of time. Heat gun will be much faster, but be careful not to melt anything.4 stars just because I'm not sure about the long-term durability and quality of the replacement glass.EDIT: Two weeks later and the glue and home button failed. Home button cable completely tore off. Will be attempting a refund.

Not sure which thin case this is compatible with, likely a Belkin, but not my Griffin Survivor!The unit, as it states, uses your Lightning cord.  One simply inserts the cord, pulls the plug end down tight, routes the cord under the base and attaches the magetic silver base.  From there, however your Lightning cable is plugged in via outlet or USB, sitting your phone on the dock starts the charging process. The built in headphone plug, seen in the picture, slips into the headphone jack on the phone and one plugs their headphones into the jack on the back of the dock.  The phone does rest on the lightning cable and the headphone jack, but it also rests on the back of the dock somewhat as it's angled back slightly. Now, to be fair to Belkin the plastic ring on the headphone plug and the lightning plug contact the phone body or the case so the weight isn't directly on the jacks in the phone, but it is defintely not ideal and could be a concern down the line. Good engineering would have recessed the plugs in way that the phone body sat on the dock when the plugs are inserted. So, this is truly a design flaw.Charging, syncing, and playing music through the dock's headphone jack have been flawless for about 10 trial uses. I am able to text, take speaker or bluetooth calls and listen to music while the unit is docked and charging. The cord has never once pulled through when removing the phone from the dock and I am able to dock and un-dock the phone with one hand.  That said, I would not want to place my $800 dollar phone on this dock, byond the testing I didn, given the lack of flush mounting of the plugs. The lack of case compatibility with my particular case shouldn't count against it due to me needing a case with lots of heft and protection.  So, not having the cables pull through, one hand usability, and the general look and build quality of what is shipped gives it one star, rather than zero for the poor engineering of the cable/phone interface resting the phone's weight on the plastic rings around the plugs (a slight amount of the weight is negated by the slight rearward angle).I would not recommend this product for your iPhone 5.

I had never used an Otterbox before, but they came highly recommended, so I ordered them sight unseen.  I am pleased with my purchase.For those of you who have never seen one, the case consists of two layers.  The first is a two piece hard shell that snaps around the Ipod.  Be sure to get it to snap down on all sides.  The top has a built in screen protector,  I was worried about how this would affect the feel of the Ipod but it does not seem to be an issue for my kids.  It does have just a tiny bit more "drag"  (the coefficient of sliding friction is a little higher for my fellow geeks) than the glass surface of the Ipod, but they quickly got used to that difference.The second part is a silicone skin that fits around the hard shell.  If the skin does not appear to be fitting correctly then you do not have the hard shell snapped down fully.  The two flaps on the bottom fit into the jack and the charging slot, these are the weakest part of the design as far as I am concerned.  It takes a little finesse to get them to stay correctly as the silicone "plug" part that fits in needs to be put in at sort of an angle.The silicone skin actually makes operating the volume and on/off buttons easier, as they stick up more, although slightly more force is needed to operate them. The case defiantly adds a little bulk to the Ipod, but this is not a bad thing as it is much easier to hold.  The extra width though, means that a lot of docking products will not work with the case on.  You can, however, plug in the charging cable that comes with the Ipod without removing the case.From a physics point of view the case design makes sense.  The silicone cover will increase the time of impact (even doubling the time in fractions of a second will halve the force) when dropped and the hard shell will provide further protection to the edges of the Ipod by breaking before the Ipod edges are impacted.  That said, I am not willing to drop it to test it!  I wish that Otterbox would conduct drop tests on their products and publish them though.

*Update at the end of the review!*Allow me to start off by saying that I was looking for a cheap bluetooth headset here on Amazon. Notice the word "cheap". This wasn't almost a hundred bucks like some available on Amazon, it wasn't even $50. This is a low-cost bluetooth, and your expectations shouldn't be too high. That said, I'm relatively pleased!PROS- Plays nicely with the iPhone- Handsfree calling- Cableless music playback- Comes with headphones*- Very intuitive to set-up and use**- Sound quality is definitely manageable- Clip is strong, holds even while jogging- Can be used with any 3.5mm headphones (the reason I bought it!)CONS- * Headphones are pretty bad. Kind of uncomfortable and the sound is only manageable for an hour at most.- **Counter-intuitive volume/track controls. Volume up is on the same side as previous track.- Proprietary charger. It's 2012, nobody wants to carry around 15 different cables all the time.- My battery life sits at around 3 hours. Not bad for working out, taking longer walks and long telephone conversations. Audio books - not so much.I love being able to store my iPhone in my backpack while I jog with just this little receiver clipped on to one of the straps. It makes everything SO much easier. As I mentioned, the headphones are really not ideal (you will want different ones), and the receiver itself has good sound quality when played through more expensive audio solutions. I'm not sure what the range is like on this thing - I almost always have my iPhone within a foot or two - but I have dealt with any loss of transmission, and people have told me that the mic quality is clear if not on par with the iPhone.The cons aren't enough to make me drop this down to 3-stars, and I would still recommend it, but I would definitely advise the company who created this to get with the times. Apple gets to use proprietary chargers because they sold 200 Million iDevices in 2011 - the rest of you however should be using standard USB protocols (and I would love Apple to switch too).OVERALLIt's a very decent little bluetooth receiver for a fabulous price. Once you get used to the issues mentioned, it's not difficult to use and so far hasn't failed. I will update the review for longevity, but for now this is definitely recommended!!!*UPDATE*After quite a few months of near daily use, I am still enjoying my purchase! It works very well with my Macbook and Mac Pro, so I can use this for Skype while I work with clients. I ended up cutting down the cable length on my Apple headphones and re-soldering the 3.5mm jack so that they are shorter. The short headphones that came with the bluetooth just become more unusable with time. Not for everyone, but a very easy way to get decent sound!

Most folks get frustrated with inexpensive screen protectors like these. Toward the end of this review I have a tip that my be useful. What you like in screen protector will depend on your preference, so I wrote about my experience.The protector itself offers a clean image without a matte and/or darkened finish, and for me, does not interfere with the touch-screen interface. The anti-glare aspect gives it a glistening or crystalized effect at times, but not to where it blocks visibility. Oil and residue tend to show fairly easily, but it also wipes off easily. The fit around home button, camera, etc is about 95% accurate, but this usually the case with generic protectors. Applying them can be frustrating, but I don't necessarily think it is a product defect, just the nature of living in a world with dust.Tip: I have a method that works for me, but not claiming that it trumps others. First, make sure fans, a/c, etc are all off. When wiping the bare screen before application, I do it slowly b/c I've noticed rapid or intense wiping builds up static electricity. After that I get a handheld vacuum and place it near the phone. When I'm ready to apply the protector, I turn the vacuum on and keep it running until I am satisfied the protector placement. I still get a bubble or two, but they are usually unobtrusive and away from the screen.

I'm a long-time Virgin Mobile customer, and have always elected to own very spartan phones.  My work and social life didn't require anything fancier, and the simpler phones generally held up better than my colleagues smart phones.  However, after expanding my business, it's become necessary to own an Android smartphone that enables me to conduct my everyday duties.  As well, it would be nice to own a fun handset.I did a lot of research, comparing cellular carriers in my area (particularly the Chicagoland suburbs), and it turned out Virgin Mobile - with the LG Optimus V smartphone - was just perfect for me.  Virgin Mobile, using Sprint's network as its backbone, has always provided great coverage in and around the cities and states I work in.  For service, I signed up for the $25/month service.  300 anytime talk minutes and unlimited data and text suits me just fine.  I've never gotten close to my limit.  Virgin Mobile throttles 3G data after a 2GB cap, but I've never gotten close to that (I rely on encrypted wi-fi in most of my offices).  As a Virgin Mobile customer for years, and I've always been pleased with their cellular service, and their customer service.  I'm paying less than most of my friends, however I feel I get comparable service - sometimes even better service (especially the poor souls with AT&T;, who can't get a dependable signal in the Chicago Loop area).  The 3G download and upload speeds are comparable to other 3G service in my area.  I average 2000 kb/s, at home, and get even faster speeds in downtown Chicago.  An added bonus with Virgin Mobile's no-contract service, is that I'm not locked into some draconian service plan.  Nice!My roommate and several of my pals have the T-Mobile G2X (a.k.a., the Optimus 2X), and the Optimus V compares quite favorably.  While the more-expensive G2X has a nicer camera, LED flash bulb, a slightly nicer screen, more internal memory and a faster CPU...  Truthfully, I think the Optimus V is only just slightly less awesome than the G2X.  And, in the areas where the Optimus V are weaker, they really don't faze me.  I don't need a powerhouse phone - but even when I'm playing games or editing photos, I feel like the Qualcomm 600 MHz CPU holds up just fine.  It's still the most powerful phone I've owned, and is no slouch against other same-generation smartphones I've used.The Optimus V and very open Android Froyo 2.2.1 OS, really does suit my needs quite perfectly.  I can run the latest builds of Opera Mobile, TeamViewer (remote control software), Square with the little credit card reader, Hulu Plus, Netflix movies, Avast Antivirus, MyBackup Pro, Facebook, Cerberus (tracking software), PDF and e-book reader, Springpad, RadioReference Scanner Radio, Groundspeak geocaching app, MortPlayer multi-format music player, Zedge, multi-format QQPlayer for videos, WiGLE war driving, and many other apps - including the occasional game and augmented reality apps.  Additional perks are Google Suite integration, Gmail, Google Contact, Google Calendar, YouTube videos, Google Maps, and Google GPS Navigation.  The Android operating systems makes it super-easy to customize.  Custom wallpapers, ringtones, alarm clocks, animated screen savers, advanced launchers, custom security lockout screens and more are available online.  There really isn't any part of this phone I can't customize to my liking, WITHOUT having to root it (which I hear is fairly easy).It's a fine form factor - 113.5 mm (4.47 in) tall, 59 mm (2.3 in) wide, and 13.3 mm (0.52 in) thick.  With the stock 1500 mAh battery, it weighs a comfortable 129 g (4.6 oz).  The battery life is a little better than the G2X under similar usage, and there are higher-capacity batteries available.  As well, there are a ton of accessories available for the Optimus V.  With a little shopping around, I found numerous cases, screen protection, charges, data cables - all for super cheap.  I added in a 32GB micro SDHC card, and it instantly recognized it.  Connected to my computer, via USB micro cable, it was instantly recognized and mounted on Windows XP, Vista, and Win7.  After months of daily usage, after loading a myriad of apps, I haven't had any major crashes.  The touchscreen is very precise and sensitive, allowing for complex actions and quick typing (I've since started using Swype).  The speakers and mic are pretty good, with no complaints about call quality.  The voice recognition works really well, either with the built in microphone or using a headset.  The external jack works fine, though I only use it for the Square card reader.  For hands free, I use a Bluetooth headset - all of which, I was able to easily pair up.  All of the buttons are sturdy, and offer a satisfying click when pressed.  After months of use, the buttons still feel good as new.  The camera isn't too shabby in adequate light, either, with automatic geotagging.  Video recording is also quite good, for a phone.  A feature that comes in real handy, is that you can use the phone as a mobile hotspot, with the use of a free app.  This really is a well-designed handset, and never seems to freeze or crash, after months of extensive use.Am I the envy of owners of Apple iHype products or state-of-the-art Android handsets?  Doubt it.  Virgin Mobile may not be the sexiest of service providers, and the LG Optimus V isn't the most vogue handset.  Do I care?  Heck no!  Honestly, I'm PROUD to own a phone that is reasonably powerful, stable, energy efficient, and bargain priced.  I feel the Virgin Mobile Optimus V is the killer bargain of its generation, and will serve most folks quite well - without costing them a fortune.

I have tried several of these blue tooth hands free car kits/speaker kits (under $50) by several different manufacturers and all of them have been disappointing.  THIS ONE IS NO EXCEPTION. The clip on case used for a visor has a small clip and would not clip onto the visor of my 09 Tacoma.  After prying it open and praying it wouldn't snap off the plastic, it awkwardly sits on the visor, BUT the position of the clip does not allow you to flip the visor down and let the sun charge the unit unless you take it out and flip it over and re-insert it into the holder so the panels are out.  You then have to remember to re-insert face up before use, because the buttons and speaker would be up against the visor.  THE BIGGEST PROBLEM is the most important.  The speaker, LIKE ALL THE OTHERS sucks!  The volume is way too low.  I have tried it on the windshield and on the visor and my callers are a whisper at 65 MPH on the freeway, windows up, AC fan off, and no radio on.  City streets are no better.  This is with both units in both a pick-up truck and a small SUV.  When speaking to people, the complaint is that you sound far away and "tinny", the volume is TOO LOW on both ends with the volume on the unit at full.  This was tested with a blackberry and a motorola cell phone on BOTH speakers I bought.  Placing the unit on the visor and flipping the visor down so that it's almost at mouth level makes it a little better, but then your vision is blocked by the visor.  Completely disappointed.  I am giving up on these type of systems, they just don't have the volume and speaker capability we need.  I am assuming the only true option is a built-in vehicle system.  Maybe my next car.... I use this device to transmit a DirecTV satellite receiver output to my earbuds (plugged into a cheap $24.00 Bluetooth audio adapater from Wally World), about 15' across the room. The manual says to keep the transmitter within 5 feet of the receiving device, which, if that were the case, I would have returned it. At 15' there is no breakup or background noise. The audio quality is far superior to what I had been using: an FM transmitter (common for car stereo setups) to a Sony Walkman am/fm radio on FM. While the latter setup works, there is a lot of background noise and cross/same channel interference because the Sony FM portable receives so many FM stations that there is always interference. Also, moving around (like rolling over), causes the signal to come and go on FM, hence my desire to try a Bluetooth solution)This is where Bluetooth comes in. No interference whatsoever, No problem pairing this transmitter with the el cheapo Wally World Bluetooth receive adapter (which also works very, very well). I am taking the line level out of the DirecTV receiver and using the supplied adapter cable that came with the transmitter to get the audio from the sat receivre to the transmit adapter. I leave the transmit adapter plugged in all the time to the provided charging cable (a simple USB wall charger). On the receive side, the Wally World receive adapter is also powered all the time. Both devices appear to have an internal Li-ion battery, but they can both be left on charge power during normal use without risk to the batteries.The transmit adapter is about 1" square and 1/8" thick, weighs next to nothing. The cheap Wally World receive adapter is very similar in size and weight, and it has the standard 1/8" (3.5 mm) stereo jack output)If you want to duplicate this setup, keep in mind we are using "line level" audio, so there is no volume control. As it turns out, the audio level is perfect for night time listening with just a common set of "Frogz" ear buds. If I felt I needed volume control, I could find a set a ear buds with built in volume control, but so far, there is no need. The line levels are quiet enough to not "keep me awake", but certainly loud enough for clearly understandable  dialog.N.B. Concerning the "delay" referenced in other reviews: Using it on the DirecTV sat receiver, there is a perceptible delay, but it is very, very slight. If I look really hard to spot this "issue", I can see it now and then...and to be honest, that problem manifests all on its own from DirecTV without any bluetooth transmitter. Nevertheless, there is "some" delay, however slight, but not enough to confuse or even mildly annoy me. Further, I got it to "fall asleep" to, so most of the time I have the TV off when listening to this setup. I did make a point, however, of evaluating the delay carefully before writing this review. It is possible that each device may show differing delays, but I don't see the kind of delays that some have written  about. Maybe I will in the future, but so far, there just isn't any issue. Maybe the pairing of this transmitter with the el cheapo Wally World receiver adapter is just a happy marriage. Who knows?Highly recommended (both transmitter and receiver).

I am not even exaggerating when I tell you that the Anker external battery series are some of the best external batteries you'll find anywhere. If you're buying an external battery, chances are its not to show off to your friends, it's to keep with you and juice up your devices when they die. And this thing is perfect for that.Pros:I actually really like the design of this. It's just straight black and rectangular (cue Apple patent jokes here). It's got a little round button exactly where an iPhone home button would be, which is flanked by little blue circular LEDs which indicate the battery level. Very clean, very nice to look at and to hold. I don't doubt that some people might actually mistake this for a phone, which would be an interesting discussion. Size-wise, this thing is about a half-centimeter taller and wider than my HTC One, and probably about twice as thick.The battery charges itself up to full capacity in a couple of hours, and holds onto it for much much longer than thatCons:None major, save for the fact that the included adaptable cable (which comes with like 4 or 5 tips) is a little outdated now. It came with an old iPhone 30-pin adapter, a mini (NOT micro) USB adapter, and some DC ones or something. Either way, I don't use it, because I have my own USB cables from all of my devices, which are mostly micro-USB now.Devices I've tried:Nexus 4, Nexus 7, HTC One, iPhone 4 - All phones charge exactly as you'd hope, very quickly, and without any issues. I don't know what the difference is between the Apple and Android ports, but I've stuck with them just in case they are optimized for each device. But I dunno, maybe it's all psychological. Either way, both charge very quicklyNintendo DS, Sony PSP - Both portables also charge without any problems.I doubt you'd find any success trying to charge an iPad or laptop with one of these, but if you're trying to do that, you've probably got some other options before you. This is definitely meant to be super portable, and it certainly is. Dead devices no more!

PROS:I like the retro look of the boombox.  It feels solid and hefty to me (11.8 pounds) but not too heavy to carry around.  The handle is solid heavy duty plastic screwed into place on each side by two screws and fits my hand nicely.The wireless works fine but I find the sound better using the wired connection to my MP3 better. The wireless cuts out periodically.I love the sound on this boombox.  It is clear and crisp even when I cranked the thing WAYYYY up.  It has five speakers and sounds phenomenal for a stereo of its size.The remote comes with a CR2025 lithium coin battery included.I like that the unit comes with a rechargeable battery but do wish that the charge lasted longer than six hours.  Of course, I can always keep it plugged in, which actually is probably what I will do most of the time.  The rechargeable battery IS replaceable once it quits charging but the manual says it needs to be bought from TDK Life on Record. I tried to find one on their website but was unable to (for price).I like that there is a USB for charging.  I do wish there was a USB port for input (flash drives, laptop, etc).I like having FM radio on the boombox and having four presets on the remote control.The unit comes with separate operator's manuals in different languages - English, Spanish, German, French I'm pretty sure about.  But it also comes with what I think are Japanese, Chinese, Korean, Russian and maybe more?CONS:The remote for this unit is small and thin.  I am quite sure I will be misplacing it soon unless I do something to prevent it.  I think I'll put a velcro tab on the back of the boombox and one on the remote so that I can keep them together.I wish the cord/power brick wasn't so cumbersome for the boombox.  It would have been nice to just have the power cord for this but maybe not enough room because of the five speakers.  Or maybe it has something to do with the power for all those speakers (I am not an electrician).The handle is solid and does not move.  Because of this, the dials and readouts underneath it are a little hard to get to/read.The knobs for power/volume control and for source/select control are a little stiff.  I don't know whether they will loosen up after using a while but right now it's a little difficult to fine tune volume/station selection/etc. because of the stiffness.It takes a while to manually check out the radio stations using the select control dial.  There is a seek button, however, on the remote, which goes quicker.The equalizer works OKAY but is rather weak on the Treble.  Bass is a little better - can be upped some but not a huge amount.It is great having five speakers on this boombox but the two 5.25" passive radiators are not covered, which seems very risky to me.  Maybe it's for the effect since you can see them move when playing music.I wish there was an AM radio option on the unit.  FM is included but not AM.  I wouldn't think that would have been that much of an expense to add.Also, the boombox comes with a 3.5mm aux input so I can have a wired connection to my MP3 player, which I love.  But just how much would it have cost to include aCables Unlimited AUD-1100-06 6 feet 3.5 MM Male to Male Stereo Cable?  They are cheap and luckily I had one laying around.  It would have made the boombox ready-to-use in all ways.MY RECOMMENDATIONS/COMMENTS:I don't think I would take this out in the woods camping or to a rowdy party.  But if you want a super sounding stereo in your house/apartment that stays in one spot most of the time, this is the stereo for you.  It looks good, sounds better and the price isn't outlandish for the sound you get.

I needed an option to connect my phone to my car stereo.  I have an older car without an aux in plug nor does my car stereo have a bluetooth feature.I purchased this to compare it to the Motorola Roadster 2 bluetooth and FM features.  My review will be related to the FM feature as that is what I purchased the product for.Review:I really wanted to like this product.  It is substantially larger than the Motorola Roadster 2 but seemed like it would be louder (using the built in speakers) as they were much bigger.  I was very impressed with the look of this compared to the Motorola Roadster 2.  When used without the FM feature this was louder than the Motorola Roadster 2 although I still could not hear everything that was being said all the time and with the windows open you can forget about using it at all (as I get older I do notice I have some hearing loss).FM Transmitter: My location Rocklin/Roseville CAThis feature was abysmal.  In both of my cars (2001 Acura TL and 2005 GMC Yukon XL) the FM transmitter could barely transmit to an empty station.  In my Acura there was quite a bit of static.  When I played music through my phone I could barely hear it.  In my GMC the music couldn't even cut through the static to be heard.  I tried to move the Jabra to different locations closer to my car stereo and it still wouldn't transmit the signal clearly.For me this was a deal breaker.  I sent the product back and stuck with the Motorola Roadster 2.Pros:-Nice design-Multiple speakers that sound much better than other products-Could realistically be used as a portable speakerCons:-FM Transmitter weaker (MUCH MUCH) than other competing products-Much larger than other products like the Motorola Roadster 2

My battery life was so poor on my Droid Charge that I wanted to try the extended battery I saw on Amazon, sold for $12.99 from Hyperion.  But I bought one of these belt-clip holsters from this vendor and absolutely love it.  I was distressed to think I'd not be able to find a belt-clip holster that would fit with the extended battery.  But I was so desperate for a battery that would get me past noon that I bought the extended battery.  I was so excited to find that the back case it comes with fits inside this belt-clip holster!!!  The wonderful part with the kickstand that protects the back when you use a standard battery won't fit over it of course, nor will probably any case cover if you want to use the belt-clip holster, but for me, that's not as important.  If I drop my phone while it's in its holster, it'll likely land on the battery bulge, or on the holster side.  Either way, it may crack what it lands on, but I won't end up with a broken (function lost) phone.  So I recommend anyone who's looking for a belt clip holster that will work with an extended battery case on their Droid Charge to get this clip!  How can you lose?  It's inexpensive, and works great!

I don't write too many reviews but felt compelled to write this one because this case is so good.  I have had this case on my phone for 3 months now.  I love my Nokia E71 and want to keep it for a long time.  I tried a "leather" case that had clear plastic over the screen, and it ended up scratching my screen protector (glad I had that installed!).  I searched for other cases and almost bought a standard silicon case but just didn't like how "sticky" they feel when trying to put in one's pocket and how they don't always fit tightly.This case solves both problems.  The silicone protects the phone nicely, while the plastic "shell" provides reinforcement increasing the protection and giving it a very solid feel.  With the plastic shell, the silicone fits tightly, and one can insert the phone in one's pocket without much problem.  None of the keys on the keyboard are obstructed, all of the buttons and ports work just fine with this case on.  I happened to drop the phone for the first time this week on a concrete basement floor, and the case did it's job!  Phone is working just fine with no damage/scratches.The only issues I have with this case are:- I would like a little more of the silicon to be revealed on the back of the case to help prevent sliding off of tables, desks, etc.- The area along the upper-left side near the memory card slot/USB port and the screen is a little bit too thin and makes me wonder if this will rip off eventually.  I think there is plenty of room to increase the material here while sill allowing for access to these ports.

I have been through a few cases for my phone and this is my favorite so far.  It isn't perfect, but it certainly does a very good job for the price.Pros:- Rubberized texture is good for hands but doesn't stick to everything and collect lint like some other cases- All buttons and ports remain accessible whether in the case or not- Headphone jack is usable with 90 degree headphone plug (last case needed straight, slim plug)- Kickstand is surprisingly rugged and does a good job.- Case doesn't add much thickness or weight to the phone.- Low profile edges allow for easy scrolling and do not inhibit any touch screen usage.- Belt holder part of the case is very convenientCons:- Phone can get easily knocked out of the belt loop part of the case, especially when worn sidways, I stopped wearing it sideways.- Phone case plastic is starting to crack around edges after only a few gentle removals.- Case does not protect top and bottom edges of screen from damage.- The camera is always facing outward.  If you are a conspiracy theorist, you might not like this.  Nothing electrical tape can't fix.This case does a very good job at ergonomics without infringing on the use of the phone.  It is very convenient and even looks pretty sleek too.  The kickstand is wonderful since the Samsung phones don't have them built in.  I thought the sliding case might scratch the screen or at the very least peel up the edges of my screen protector.  I was pleasantly surprised to see that it is designed very well such that the screen is given adequate clearance even when sliding it in and out of the belt loop holder.  It is also nice that I can access the volume controls even while it is in the case, for when I am riding my bike or motorcycle.The biggest flaw with this case is that the phone can be very easily pushed out of the belt holder (especially when worn horizontally) even just by a shoulder bag, or a hand when carrying things.  I have often found the phone half out of the holder when I wore it horizontally or even at a 45 degree upward angle.  One time I had a shoulder bag which knocked my phone out onto the sidewalk.  It also allows it to hit the ground at the perfect angle, right on the corner which is unprotected.  As such, even with the case, it still cracked my screen and I had to get it replaced.  I have since started carrying it directly up and down and it seems to have fixed that problem.I still highly recommend this case as long as you can handle it being straight up on your belt.UPDATE:  After wearing the case vertically on my belt now, I have not had any problems with the phone getting knocked out of it anymore.  The only down side is that it is slightly more awkward getting the phone out of the holster vertically as opposed to horizontally.

This case (cover) was exactly what I wanted for my iPhone 5.  It is super thin.  It is non-slip.  It snaps on and fits perfectly.  It does not block access to any of the buttons or ports.  It may not provide the utmost in protection from drops or immersion in liquids, but that isn't what I wanted.  I wanted a cover that would not detract from the simple elegance of the iPhone 5, but would provide some cushioning and a less slippery surface.  This case does exactly that.  As soon as I ordered my iPhone 5 (on the first day, it is my fourth iPhone), I started looking for cases.  I decided to order that super-protective case that virtually encases the iPhone in armor (you know which one I mean), but I used it for a day and decided it was too cumbersome, too bulky, too ugly, and just too, too, much.  I immediately removed it and went without a case.  But the phone was just too slippery,  It is so slender that it feels frail, even though I know it is not.  So I went looking for other cases.  I settled on one on Amazon that seemed like it would fit the bill.  I know I can't mention other products, but the one I settled on cost double what this one costs.  It was better than the armor, but I still wasn't happy with it.  Just a bit too much.  Plus, the cutout for the cable was too tight.  The 30 pin to lightning adapter would not plug in without pulling the case half way off the phone.  Even the standard lightning cable required some effort to plug in and obtain contact every time.  The lock and volume buttons were covered and required considerable effort to push and activate.  This cover solves all those problems.  No covers on the buttons.  The bottom ports and speakers are completely exposed.  The phone mounts easily on the adapters without forcing or removal.  You can barely tell there is a cover on the phone at all, but the phone is no longer slippery, and I feel more confident putting it in my shirt pocket (even though my wife hates that) and not having it slide out if I bend forward.  It also does not easily slide off smooth surfaces like the car console or dash.  This is the perfect case for my purposes.  If there are any drawbacks at all, I would prefer that the name NOT be on the back and that there be a cutout for the Apple logo, but that is being really picky.  For ten bucks, this cover can't be beat!  It's perfect!  If anybody wants a full suit of armor or sort-of protective cover for any iPhone 5, let me know and you can have them for the cost of shipping, otherwise they are just going to collect dust until I decide to throw them away.

First, the goods:- stereo sound is very good (for a wireless device);- comfort is good, except there are no alternate size speaker cups in the bulk package. So if these are too large for you - you are out of luck, because it is almost impossible to get a replacement elsewhere.  In terms of comfort, these would be perfect for a gym, except...The bads:- the set looses a connection to the BT source at a mere turn of your head!This flakiness is more than an inconvenience - for me it rendered this otherwise almost perfect device absolutely useless, so I end up returning it.  I tried it with two sources: my Blackberry, and a laptop with a BT transmitter.  The laptop transmitter is pretty powerful: with my other headphone (also by Motorola, by the way, HT820) I can walk around the house, even go on a top floor without loosing the connection.But with this set, I lost the connection every time I turned my head or raised my arm.  And what's the deal with the replacement cups?  There is a link to an accessory site in the manual.  The site is useless - nothing at all!About the bulk packaging: it's fine, except you don't really know if you are getting a new item or a return (I suspect the latter). Also, the retail package comes with an alternate size set of speaker cups, bulk package doesn't have them.

Comfort: My main problem with this headset was that it is not very comfortable on my ear.  When I first got it, I couldn't wear it for more than 5-10 minutes.  Now that my ear has become accustomed to it, I can wear it for an hour or so.  It just takes getting used to.  However, I wish I could rotate the headset without rotating the earhook like some bluetooth headsets.  In order to have the headset have a tight grip on my ear (and not fall off), I have to rotate the headset so that it points upwards towards my eyebrows instead of downwards towards my mouth.  It's not really an inconvenience per se, but it looks pretty uncool.  Perhaps I have misshapen ears.Audio: The sound is fantastic.  Only sometimes does it get staticky in my pocket, and not often enough to notice, at least on my end.Ease-of-Use:  This headset was easy to pair with my Motorola KRZR and the main things I do (hanging up, answering calls, and adjusting volume) are simple and intuitive.  Only problem is that if you don't hold buttons for the correct length of time, it can perform the wrong function.  For example, pressing the call button once activates voice dialing, for 1 second redials your last call and 3 seconds turns it off.  I've done my share of accidentally redialing.Battery: I was disappointed when I learned the battery wasn't rechargeable and it instead uses a AAA battery, but the lifetime is very long.  It's supposedly 30 hours or so.  I can't verify that, I just know I've been using it for two months now without replacing it, and it's just a regular Energizer battery.Durability: I carry this around in my jacket pocket most of the time and it holds up well.  I do find myself accidentally turning it on when I lean against something, but I just need to be more careful.
It's way too big for a woman's head.  It feels bulky, and not comfortable at all.  I wouldn't recommend this to anyone.  There are others out there that are much more comfortable and less bulky.

This is a review of the New Trent 'iZen', full name: iZen Power Folio. There are lots of extended battery cases for the iPhone, but this is now a battery powered case for your iPad. Because the iPad has a bigger built-in battery than the iPhone, you need a more powerful external battery as well. A lot of batteries I see on the market are 5,000, 7,000 or 10,000 MAh, but this one is 14,000 MAh, which is the biggest battery I've seen. But New Trent has made this battery flat and wide so that it doesn't add much thickness to the iPad.This case is for the traveller with multiple USB-powered devices who wants to make sure they always have power without bringing a separate battery for each device. Or, if you have an all-day presentation to make on your iPad, using this case will guarantee you don't have any embarrassing blank screens as your iPad powers down in the middle of your marathon session. It does add a little bit of weight, but if you're on an international flight and want to make sure you can finish all three of your movies that you brought with, this way you don't have to deal with half-bright screens just so you can eke out enough power to last until you get to the hotel.First, you have to install the battery in the case by sliding it in along the grooved rails. What's nice is that once you've slid it in all the way, you know you're making a good connection because the little ring light on the end lights up momentarily to let you know you have contact. Then its just a matter of snapping the other end into the plastic shell and you're all done - there's no loose feeling or worries about the battery falling out.Like all New Trent iPad cases that I've tried, the iPad also fits very securely in the case. There's a satisfying 'click' when you snap the corners in, and then you know you're in tight. Just like the battery, there's no worries about anything falling off.The battery conects to what i'm calling the 'power bar' which serves a couple different purposes. First, the power bar has a small minijack plug in it that you connect to either the USB Battery-Charging dongle, or to the USB Cable for charging your device. Both cables are very small, and easy to slip into whatever you're carrying your iPad in, or even your jacket pocket if you travel light.Operation is pretty straight forward - when you plug in the iZen to charge, the light indicates when its done charging, and while charging a device, the light changes to a different color to let you know its drawing power. The most important part of a battery case is of course the battery, and I was able to successfully charge my iPad off the battery overnight, then let the iZen charge back up during the day, then charged my wife's drained iPad the next overnight. You use your own USB charging cables, so youo can charge anything that uses a USB cable - iDevices, smartphones, dumbphones, USB helicopter, anything.The case is functional as well. The power bar is also used to hold the iZen in one of two different 'display' positions, using the built-in notches on the front of the case. The front of the case also has magnets for activating the built in wake/sleep functions of the iPad. The magnets aren't strong enough to hold the case on if you hold it upside down, but they were sufficient to hold the cover closed while holding the ipad walking up the stairs or carrying it around.The magnets are strong enough to activate the wake/sleep sensors when the lid is folded to the BACK of the case, but you get used to that pretty quickly and your pretty soon your natural instinct is to fold the case back in such a way that this doesn't happen.The case is noticeably heavier that just the iPad by itself but that's to be expected when you're packing this much juice. This case is pretty specific-use, however, so if you're looking for a case with a battery in it, you're obviously not looking for the svelte 'barely there' case, because if you had that, you'd have to bring with a big honkin' battery too.This case is for someone who wants to minimize the # of different accessories they take with them on a trip or someone who needs a battery for all-day, full brightness presentations (or marathon game playing sessions).

TheSPIGEN SGP SGP10044 Linear Metal Crystal Case - 1 Pack - Retail Packaging - Metal Slatefor the iPhone 5 is a well-made basic case.  It allows you to see the attractive back of your iPhone, which a lot of cases cover up.  It also allows you to slightly "customize" your case, with a gold accented base plate and multicolored home button stickers. It includes a clear screen protector, which is a nice bonus.  It provides decent protection, and it allows you to use all your phone's features.But the description is misleading: this is a plastic case.  The frame portion does look a bit like metal, but that's about it.  It doesn't provide the same level of protection that metal would, and the design is such that it may come apart when dropped.  Add to that the premium cost, and this is a poor value given the materials.PROS:++ Decent protection for your phone++ Clear back plate is thicker than other clear backed cases++ Clear back shows off attractive back of the iPhone 5++ Case adds minimal thickness to your phone++ Allows complete access to the phone's touch screen++ Fringe of the case allows you to place your phone face down without the screen touching the surface it's on++ Installation is easy, though it does take some time++ Good protection against scratches++ Matte frame is smudge and fingerprint resistant++ Interchangeable base plate and home buttons add color and visual interest++ Clear screen protector is a nice extra++ Custom designed for the Apple iPhone 5++ Includes a cleaning cloth++ Very slick packaging, almost better designed than the case itselfCONS:-- Description is misleading, implying metal construction-- Case frame is constructed of less durable plastic, though it appears like matte metal-- Smooth surfaces don't provide any extra grip for holding onto your phone-- Interchangeable gold toned base plate is not very attractive-- The buttons built into the frame are too firm, requiring too much pressure to activate the buttons on the phone-- Provides very minimal cushioning for protection against drops-- Base plate connects via clips, and may come apart when dropped-- Premium expense for such a basic case design given the materials-- It is unclear what, if any, warranty is available on this productCONCLUSIONThis case provides basic protection with no loss of functionality.  It also offers the promise of creating a customized look.  You could even buy several cases with different colored frames and mix and match the pieces.  But it comes at a premium given the basic design and materials.  And it doesn't provide enough protection given the intended use.  Depending on your needs, the alternatives may offer better protection, better value or both.

I have always been a proponent of Bluetooth headset use, especially when talking on the cellphone and driving at the same time. I think it is unfortunate that certain people consider using bluetooth to be uncool and somehow associated with a rather hateful stereotypical image of a Hummer-driving yuppie. If you're driving and talking, GET a Bluetooth headset! I would rather see people driving around on the road like they are telephone tech supports than blind and distracted zombies. That ends my rant on why I believe everyone should be using a wireless headset. Now let's talk about this Plantronics Discovery 925 Bluetooth.I've gone through a few Bluetooth headsets in the past few years - mostly Motorola and Jarba models priced around 20 bucks. I had absolutely no complaints regarding these economy models. When I received this 150 dollars headset(!) in the mail, I was seriously expecting to be awed and shocked with a level of performance, feature, and luxury I had previous unexperienced.The unboxing and setup process was pretty straight forward. One fancy feature that is indeed very useful is that a leather carrying pouch was included, and it isn't just any plain leather pouch either. The carrying pouch, in fact, has a built-in battery. When the headset is stored inside the pouch, you can plug in the charging cable and charge both the headset and the charging pouch. So later on, when you're on the road, you in fact have two batteries ready to be used which greatly extends the hours of use between charging. Unfortunately, the charging carrying case is the only positive thing I can say about this headset.According to the manual and the packaging, the headset supposed to have some fancy audio-processing logic which will make the phone conversation crystal clear. Alas, it was not the case. Once I got the headset bonded with my Verizon KRZR phone and made a test call to my wife, the voice sounded rather dull, down-sampled, and somewhat metallic. This is unacceptable for a 150 BT headset! I can honestly say, in term of voice clarity, all my previous 20-buck Motorola/Jarba outperformed this ridiculously overpriced headset by far. I have not tried the headset with any other cell phones. But a Motorola KRZR is a rather common handset, and Plantronics 925 gave me quite a let-down.In conclusion, I will not recommend this headset to anyone at its current price. If it was around $30 - $40 (currently Amazon is already offering at 50% off the original listing price), and only if looks/design is more important to you than voice clarity, perhaps only then I will recommend it to you.Sorry Plantronics, I honestly, genuinely wanted to love this headset. But for the time being, I'm going back to my 20 dollar Jarba.

I am as huge of a WP fan as you will find.  I am a great cheerleader for the OS.  It is the best of both worlds:  You can customize, remove, and adjust your homescreen, shortcuts, and app selection as you want.  It is a fantastic user experience- seamless, smooth, and a fun time.  I love the Office tools, the calander is one of the most useful there is, it incorporates your email and social sites so amazingly well, and is a great media/internet experience.That is why the Titan is such a let down.  Call quality is as poor as you can imagine.  There is echoes, garbled reception, and callers on the other end almost can not understand the speech at all.  We have had 4 Titans and this has been true of all of them.  2 of them did not load sites correctly, one had a screen that stopped working, and another would not allow email updates.We purchased the device in November of 2011 so this is a long term problem.  I can not recommend it.  We got the HTC Titan 2, and the call quality was much better, though still not a strength.  We have had no problems with it.  Please avoid the Titan for your own sanity.  If you are in love with the 4.7" screen and like HTC, the T2 is a much better option.

Test of PowerPak Ultra 14,000 NT140R-BSummary:The New Trent PowerPak Ultra is an excellent portable battery. It delivers a greater percentage of its rated capacity to recharge devices than any other portable power supply I have tested.The form factor is very good, but not great. The thickness interferes with ultimate portability, and the weatherproofing, while arguably useful, adds bulk and weight for, in my opinion, a poor tradeoff. There is no doubt that the packaging is rugged and durable, but the device must be protected like any other while it is in use. For some, this will have value, but I would rather have less weight and smaller size.In all, this is one of the very best portable power solutions available on the market at this time. It delivers on its promise of capacity, ruggedness and weatherproofing, which is a breath of fresh air in a market that overpromises and underdelivers. The value for your money is extremely high. I cannot recommend the New Trent PowerPak Ultra highly enough to those who require extra durability and environmental resistance. It has little or no real competition in its price class or level of quality.My final rating of the New Trent PowerPak Ultra NT140R-B is an easy 5 stars out of 5.Form Factor:New Trent have created the maximum protection in the minimum space and weight. Corners are nicely rounded, and the rubbery sides afford a very good grip. Without adding features that are unnecessary, New Trent have added exactly what is needed, and nothing more, to create ruggedness and waterproofness. I have sealed the port covers on this as directed and submerged the PowerPak Ultra in 12" of water for 2 hours, and there was no detectable leakage. This battery is great for people that are in wet and/or muddy environments, that will be able to use it for charging in a more controlled environment. Remember that when you open a port cover to charge a device, the water resistance is gone. I travel a lot in my job, on foot and by public transport. My batteries stay either in my ScottEvest or my waterproof backpack, so my personal perspective may be different from yours. Nevertheless, I still feel that it is a bit large and a bit heavy. I use my ScottEvest jackets all the time, and this power supply does not fit well into the space I have.Measurements:Weight: Reported weight is 13.00 ounces, and I have measured it at 12.95 ounces.Capacity:The rated capacity is 14,000 mAh. I always approach rated capacities with great skepticism. When these types of devices first came out, in the '90's, I found the actual delivered capacity very close to the rated capacity. In recent years, there are very many of these portable battery devices available, and I have found that almost all the ones I have tested deliver only 50% to 75% of their rated capacity. It is not that difficult to test the battery that you manufacture and give it a true rating, instead of giving an inflated rating and then talking about power losses, etc. Just give the battery the proper rating of what the customer can expect from it! That said, New Trent have produced a battery in the PowerPak Ultra, that comes closer to delivering what it is rated to deliver than any other battery I have tested, except for AA batteries such as the Eneloops, etc. I have used three devices to test the charge delivered: Galaxy Note 10.1 2014 Edition, Galaxy S4 (T919) and an Anker cell phone battery charger. Following is a table of three sets of charges that I have compiled. The devices were turned off during charging so the full charge can be measured, not inclusive of running the device. The second table shows another feature of the New Trent PowerPak Ultra that I greatly appreciate: the LED charge indicator lights actually represent, pretty closely, the actual remaining charge. It can be seen from the table that, although not exact, the LED indicators are closer to true than any other battery I have tested. This is a big help, to not have to guess the remaining charge, as you have to with other units. It is wasted cost to include battery level indicators that are as hopelessly inaccurate as others I have tested. Here again New Trent has added real value by making the basic functions work properly, instead of being poorly designed addons.Charge Delivery Test Table 1Charge Device #  Charge Delivered  % of Rated Capacity  Recharge Time1  13069.8 mAh  93.4%  7.5 hours2  12934.5 mAh  92.4%  8.2 hours3  12915.9 mAh  92.3%  7.6 hoursLED Charge Remaining Indicator Test Table 2LED:  4  3  2  1% Remaining:  100%  81.6%  56.2%  22.5%

I'm sure it seemed like a good idea to some engineer--- let people control their music in their car by hand motion. And so the engineers at Monster created this bad boy, which lets you control your Apple device by waving your hand.First, what I liked about it, and why it's still in my car: It is sweet that I am direct connecting to my iPhone, so I can charge and be hooked to my car stereo with just one plug. I do think the output fidelity is a little better, and I don't have to worry about adjusting the volume on the iPhone. It also works great with Pandora, which is a plus. Having a cord stash built into the charger also earns kudos, making my dash look neater. Win. It has found a permanent home on my dash.But... the hand motion thing just didn't work for me. My charger port is tucked in behind the gear shift on my 2012 Civic, and so I have to really focus on my hand to not bump the gear shift and to get the wave motion right and not accidentally trigger it when I'm reaching for something else. I found myself having to look down to make sure where my hand was, and then continually getting frustrated when the motion wasn't read right and did the wrong thing. Major fail. I've actually put electrical tape over the sensor to disable it--- that's what a pain it is for me. It would have actually worked much better (and been much cheaper!) just to use plain manual push buttons that I could tactically feel and that wouldn't have been so easy to accidentally trigger-- but that wouldn't have been sexy enough, now would it Monster? And by the way, did you really have to make a big oversized connector, big enough to put MONSTER on it, but too big to use through most cases & covers that are available for the iPod and iPhone?Just like most Monster products, this is a monstrously over-priced disaster of style over substance.

Great little Bluetooth Earbuds! Well made with attention to detail. The sound is pretty darned solid for earbuds. I don't find the common complaint read about here, concerning bass response, unless I fail to get the earbuds solidly seated within the ear canals. Sure, if you don't, the device doesn't have the "acoustic cushion" to create bass. Remember, these buds are having to create bass with 6mm drivers. That takes some tech magic to acomplish. It's not as easy as when phones are working with 20-40mm drivers, but if you demand tiny, in-ear phones, that's a necessary trade-off.The relatively short run time is a bit of an aggravation, but again, the name of the game here is tiny, small and light. This means tiny, small and light battery. Pairing is pretty seamless, although sometimes when switching back and forth between my iPod and iPhone, I have to re-pair every time by having them "Forget Device" and then re-pair. Once I've done that, all is well.The little charger/case is rather cutsy, and actually pretty practical. It can give the BackBeats a life-saving transfusion if they bow out in use right when you need them.One thing I don't like with these buds though, is that if they're properly inserted in your ears for music and proper bass response, and a phone call comes in, the sound of your own voice is horrible. Like you're under water or in a barrel. I have to "break the seals" in each ear in order to talk. But he person I'm talking to, always seems to say that the audio is perfectably fine on their end.Overall, these buds are winners in my book!

UPDATE 3/2/14 (~3 months after purchase)tl;dr, I'm updating my review because this product is absolutely fantastic. I am very pleased with it's performance. If you're looking for a great external battery with tons of power, this is the one to get.I'm not exactly sure when I bought this product, but it has single-handedly changed the way I use my mobile devices. It's 12000 mAh capacity is perfect for those, like me, who are always connected on at least one device.I bought this product primarily because I always find myself with a low battery and no outlet in sight. I travel on public transportation and I don't know the systems so I use Google maps to get around. I also use my phone to tether when my school's wifi quits.Exterior:No buttons and a beautiful LED ring to let you know how much juice you have left. Each ring is approximately 10%, so you're not left to guess with 4 or 5 LEDs. The shake to wake feature is very sensitive but I keep the battery in my bag most of the time and I haven't noticed any loss of power from it. The soft touch material on the exterior is very elegant and I appreciate the design.Performance:I'm no battery tester but I can tell this product is efficient. It's charged my iPhone 5s 6 times from absolutely dead -with more juice left- on just one charge. It charges my phone rapidly, maybe two hours tops in the smart port. It also charged my phone, kindle, and beats at the same time. I am very pleased.Amenities:I only got the battery, a case (which is not at all cheap), and a short (6in is my best guess) micro-USB to USB cable for charging. You will need your own USB to wall charger, but if you have any devices to use the battery with you'll have something laying around. I didn't receive a longer cord or any iPhone wire when I purchased, contrary to other reviews.All in all, this is the perfect device for the tech-obsessed person or the unfortunate souls who can never seem to find an outlet. I recommend this product.Please note that I did not review this product for any gain, I did not receive any product for free or discount nor any financial incentive. These are my opinions and my opinions only.PREVIOUS REVIEW: The Anker Astro3 is a great great product and has helped me, as a student, remain efficient.The Astro3 is beautiful. It's smooth, doesn't have obnoxious branding, and feels great to hold. It is not that heavy, and fits great into my bag.The large battery (12000 mAh) charges all my devices (AT THE SAME TIME, TOO!) andI have a smaller Anker battery that looks like lipstick, and I gave that to my mom when I got this product. Don't get me wrong, that works great too, but I realized that I'd rather a larger battery.I have noticed that the "shake-to-activate" is very sensitive, but if it's set off and not connected to any devices it doesn't stay on and serves just as a charge percentage indicator.I love that Anker used 10 LED's because it makes it easy to recognize the charge. 3 lights? 30%! Easy and well designed. I also really like the case they gave me! My Astro rarely leaves its little pocket, and if you put it in with the ports up you won't ever have to take it out!I cannot attest to the actual mAh in the battery, but neither can most people. It charges my phone tons of times, and I've even had it in my bed plugged into my phone overnight. It charged my iPhone up to 100%, and since I bounced around all night it kept it there. However, I didn't notice any significant battery drain, and the Astro3 charged up my beats, kindle, and iPad before dying. I'm very satisfied and I would DEFINITELY recommend this product!Edit: Forgot to add that I use this product almost solely with the AmazonBasics 4 inch Lightning cable, and it works like a charm! It keeps my bag from becoming cluttered. I'm very very happy with it.

The pros: protects very well, at least until it's completely worn, which might be within a few months.The cons: had to watch a YouTube video to make any sense of the assembly instructions provided in the packaging.Durability: it's lacking. This case had three layers: a nice neoprene kind of padding next to the phone itself, a hard PVC kind of shell that encompasses the entire phone, and then a soft, tacky rubber cover that fits tightly around the hard shell. Fits tightly for a month or two anyway. Then after a little use it starts to fatigue, then stretch, become misshapen and in some spots may tear away entirely, especially the dust plug that covers the power/sync connector.For a case that protects against nearly everything, it has a surprisingly short life expectancy, especially the black version. If you're set on buying this, avoid the black version because it appears to wear twice as fast as the 3 other colors I've purchased and used.All-in-all I still wouldn't recommend any of them, having gone through 3 in 18 months.

I found that annoying for the most frequently used button on the phone.Secondly both the power button cutouts and the volume cutouts have a depth problem. If you look at the pictures of the side of the case the cutouts for the buttons actually wrap around the back of the phone slightly. This leads to what I call a "staircase issue". The staircase issue is created by the lowest step in the staircase being created by the case, the next step is the body of the phone itself and then another step above that the outer edge of the TPU case itself. This creates a series of ridges on the side of the phone that you can feel, then somewhere in those ridges is another ridge which is the button you are looking for.Not an ergonomic design at all.

I bought this for my wife's phone because she had dropped her previous phone and shattered the screen. She was sold on the Otterbox name because her friends had them, but she wanted something sleek, so I chose this one. Although it was a 2 piece case split in the middle, it seemed to fit securely with no obvious seam or gap. And indeed, it only accidently came apart 2 or 3 times in the first 6 months that we had it. But the little rubber trim pieces on the front of the phone started peeling off, starting at the edge of the 2 halves. I don't see any way to effectively glue it back in, and the 2 halves are starting to come apart now without depressing the little latch, too, so it's pretty much done. My friend had one that he got rid of because the 2 halves wouldn't stay together after a few months, too.I've purchased LOTS of cases for phones (literally dozens) and I can say this is clearly not worth the money - there are other cases half the price or less that have similar quality and technically a better design.Skip this case unless the phone doesn't get moved much and you must have the Otterbox name.

Although the product deserves one star, I am giving it two because the company's product support responded quickly to my complaints . . . unfortunately they couldn't resolve the problems and provided untested suggestions as you'll see below.  I have a 2014 Moto Droid Maxx with the very latest release of Android.  It has no problem linking with bluetooth to several different devices including my car.Followed the installation instructions and it linked briefly, long enough to determine that it's bluetooth range is very limited.  However, I couldn't do a full test because the next time I tried to link it didn't.  It had lost the pairing and I could not get it to pair again.  I tried re-installing from scratch, followed all of the FAQ suggestions, nothing.In fairness I contacted the company.  They had a new release of the Android software coming out, so I waited until it released and re-installed from scratch.  No device found.  Contacted them again and they said the firmware in the TrackR needed to be updated and provided instructions.  Following is my reply to them after trying to follow the instructions:"Your instructions for updating the TrackR do not work.  Step one for Android says to go to [...] which doesn't exist.  As my phone says, "Chrome was unable to resolve the server's DNS address."  Even if it resolved, the instructions would be a challenge for average users.  I am technically proficient, but I look at products like this as something the technically challenged should be able to use.  Sending a user to a non-existent site is a sure way to get the product returned immediately.I'm sorry but I am going to give up on your product.  I am writing a review for Amazon and I just can't spend any more time on it."

Whenever a new smart phone comes out there is a huge rush to supply new accessories for it, and usually the result is ill-fitting cases that work like junk. This case, despite being one of the first out of the gate for the Sensation, is really, really nice. Made of firm but slightly flexible plastic, it fits the unit like a glove and provides substantial protection for the smartphone. The buttons on the side end up a bit recessed, but this is a good thing because it means they will not get damaged if you accidentally drop the phone. A slight inconvenience in hitting the buttons is worth the extra protection in my opinion. The color is very nice, and matches the slate gray / purple gray of the phone. All the cutouts for the camera, mini-usb port, etc., are perfectly aligned. There are no rough edges (caused by a poor process in production--this is the bane of crappy cell phone cases). In short: this is a near perfect skin for the HTC Sensation. I'd recommend it to anyone.

I live in a state where hand-held calls while driving is now an invitation to have a conversation with Mr. State Trooper. Even if your normal driving environment is someplace where the law hasn't caught up with technology, a Bluetooth-enabled hands free solution is just a good way to keep your focus where it belongs...on the road.The Roadster 2 paired easily with two separateHTC Evo phones. Incoming call quality through the integrated speaker was satisfactory, and callers reported good quality via the Roadster 2.If you get tired of losing aux audio input cords (or drive a car without one), you can use the Roadster 2 to stream audio from your phone's apps through an integrated FM transmitter to your car's radio. This wasn't always a strong or steady signal (and I'm not sure if it's because of a crowded FM spectrum where I live or signal strength through the cars root to the antenna above my rear windshield). You can also stream music through the integrated speaker in the Roadster 2, but I'm not sure why you'd want to (what's fine for a phone call doesn't do justice to music you like...).This device recognizes when you enter or leave the car (perhaps by the noise of the car door closing??, and battery life is quite long. No AC adapter is included, but a 12v DC adapter for the car is). A very detailed user guide is included.We've been using theearlier versionof this model (the Roadster) for 16 months, and it has worked very well. Assuming the changes to the Roadster 2 are actual improvements, we expect to get long and useful service from it.
