When I received this LyveHome Photo and Video Manager, it was just in time for me to test out from a distance - I had a Florida vacation coming up that would let me see just how well it would work locally, as well as via the LyveHome app, uploading photos from many states away.This being the first Digital Picture Frame-type device that I've owned, I wasn't entirely sure what to expect outside of the typical method of displaying a scrolling slideshow of images, but with the size of this drive, and its features, it's about the best one could hope for as far as digital picture frames go.2 TB will go a long way towards storing, I believe, an entire family's memories. Being able to access those photos, and this drive, in so many ways and from so many devices, will make it valuable for years to come, and I look forward to seeing how it'll hold up over time. And if there are any photos you DON'T want to come up on the clear, crisp (albeit not terribly large) screen, it's easy enough to adjust the settings of the LyveHome so that it doesn't automatically upload EVERYTHING on the connected/linked device. I have to say, though (as others have said) that it really IS nice to have those long-forgotten images taken so many months/years ago, in plain sight, where I can enjoy them anytime I'd like. :)Setting the LyveHome Photo and Video Manager up wasn't terribly difficult, but I did experience a few glitches. I uploaded about 450 photos from my Galaxy Note 3 with little overall problem, aside from the issue of having the drive not recognize (and consequently upload) about 80 or so of those photos. For some odd reason, when the only thing that was accessing those images was the LyveHome app, it skipped those images, claiming that they were being accessed from something else during the upload. They weren't, but since they remain on my smartphone, I just deleted those images from the LyveHome app's upload list, and kept it moving to the other images that I was trying to upload. Since I'm still on vacation in Florida as I type this, I've also uploaded the images I've taken here, to my LyveHome device at home, with no problem other than slow upload times, which I attribute to wonky WiFi in my current location (and it IS wonky where I'm staying, so that's the most likely cause for the slow image transferral).Overall, I'm happy with my experiences with my LyveHome Manager, and think that it's worth the investment if the price is right for you. I can especially see this being helpful for those that take LOTS of pictures, be they professional photographers, aspiring pros, or just those that enjoy taking lots of photos on a daily basis, or while traveling. It's nice to have a device with plenty of space, a small footprint, and many outlets for loading up those images stashed here and there and everywhere, so that all of your treasured shots are kept safe and sound in one location. And knowing that you can access those consolidated images from just about anywhere? Well worth the price, just for the peace of mind!

I'll try to make this thorough, to answer as many questions as possible if you're trying to decide whether or not you should spend this much money for the SMH-10 headsets.The short answer is absolutely: Absolutely, yes!My girlfriend and I were interested in finding a good pair of headsets, so I started researching the topic, and it wasn't long before I came across the name Sena. I saw lots of great reviews here on Amazon, and several other professional reviews of this product, so I finally decided to give in and buy the dual set, so she and I could communicate with each other.At first I hesitated to spend 300 dollars. But then I reasoned that it's only 150 per pair, and that didn't seem too bad if they turned out to be as great as everyone said. I assure you it was definitely money well spent.When they arrived, the units were partially charged, so there was enough juice in them to test them when mounting the speakers in the helmets. That was nice that I didn't have to wait on charging before I could test them.Here's how we did the installs... Note: Taking the time to mount the speakers perfectly makes all the difference.The first thing we did was to check the mounting piece that attaches to the helmet. It fit in perfectly on both our helmets. We didn't mount it in place yet, but just wanted to be sure they would fit.Next, we put one speaker in place. They stick naturally to the cloth in our helmet linings since they have some velcro on the back of the speakers. So, you just put it in place and it stays very well.Once we placed the speaker where we thought it should go, we put the helmet on and felt to see if the placement was right over the ear. Move it around until the speaker is right over the ear. Then, we plugged in an mp3 player, and checked the sound. If it's in the right place, the volume on high should be very intense!Then, we put the other speaker in, and tested the position again. Take the time to move them around until you're sure they are right over the ears. Once we found the perfect position for each, we ran the wires under the lining, and firmly tucked the speakers into place.When I put on my helmet, my ears can feel the speakers a little as the helmet slides into place, but when its fully on I can't feel them at all. But, they are right over my ears, and when the volume is on high, it is intensely loud. You should not have any problems with it being loud enough.Then, we secured the mount in place, and positioned the microphone so it would be directly in front of our mouths. Mine is directly in front of my lips, close enough that if I pucker slightly I can feel it. That is perfect! (Make sure the mic is oriented the right way, with the triangular guide toward you)Then, once the hardware is in place on your helmet, all you have to do it attach the control unit, which easily and firmly clicks into place. Very solid and safe. Then you are ready to pair with your phones or devices.First, we paired the helmets to each other so we could use the intercom feature. Then we each paired our helmets with our phones. Super easy! Super fast! Couldn't have been more simple. Then we tested the intercom and the phone features...There are two ways to use the intercom. Manual and VOX. By default VOX is disabled, so you have to initiate an intercom connection manually. You simply tap the large easy to find and handle jog dial with your left hand. Even with gloves on, this is super easy to use. Once one person has tapped the jog dial, the intercom will connect. The connection process takes a couple seconds. Then, it will stay on until one of you taps the jog dial again. Feel free to chat away... (When VOX is activated, you simply start talking, which opens the intercom connection. But, it will close when one person stops speaking for 20 seconds. I much prefer the manual always-on)The quality of the intercom is amazing! I was totally impressed... It was considerably better than I was expecting! And there is plenty of volume! No worries there!The manual says the intercom distance is up to around 900 meters. That's around a half mile. I found that be more or less accurate. But, line of sight makes a big difference. When they start to get too far apart, you can still hear each other, but you will get static. I feel the distance of the intercom is more than adequate! So count on at least a quarter mile, and up to half mile under perfect conditions.When we're on the intercom, she tells me she hears me perfectly. No road or wind noise at all, whether my visor is up or down. Sometimes I can hear a tiny bit of wind noise from her if her visor is up, but I think that's because of the mics position in her helmet. When the visor is closed, it's perfect. The clarity of the intercom is amazing!The phone feature is equally amazing. If your phone supports handsfree dialing over bluetooth, (any modern smart phone) then you can just use your headset to initiate a call. For example, touch the phone button and say: Call Roger.When a call comes in to your phone, you hear a series of beeps that sounds like the Twilight Zone theme. Just touch the phone button, and you're connected. The phone quality is what really amazes me!You can turn on voice activated answering as well, but I prefer manually touching the button to answer.Every call I've tried with my friends (before they knew I had this headset) could not tell I was in motion at all. Even when I was doing 75 mph down the Interstate. They said I sounded perfect. No road or wind noise at all. Even when I raise my visor, only a couple times was I told they could hear a little bit of wind, but not much, and that I still sounded perfectly clear. I am totally impressed with this! So much better than I expected. My girlfriends experience with the phone quality is the same... Amazing!Also, if your phone supports audio streaming via bluetooth, you can listen to music from your phone or mp3 player wirelessly. Very nice. You can also use the included wire if you need to. Personally, I won't use this feature... Just the intercom with my girlfriend, and phone calling.By the way, if you're using the intercom and a call comes in, you will hear it. The headsets priority by default is Phone, Intercom, Music etc...As for battery life... Well, after one full charge, we still haven't used up the juice. We've been on one long trip of about 3 to 4 hours with constantly connected intercom and some phone calls, and other shorter trips around the area. All in all maybe 7 to 8 hours of heavy usage, and still haven't needed to recharge. Very impressive!As for weather... During our long ride, we hit rain. We live in Oregon, and it was a fair to medium rain. Enough that we had all our gear on and I had to keep wiping my visor. Didn't affect the units at all. Probably about an hour and a half of the rain. They have rubber plugs over the connectors, so no harm at all. Very nice.So, with that said... I promise you, you won't be sorry. These things really are as great as everyone is saying. Take the time to position the speakers optimally, and once you're up and running you'll be totally impressed. I would recommend these to anyone!To anyone in other reviews who say they've had an issue with being able to hear the volume, or with wind noise... I can almost guarantee it's because they didn't take the time to place the speakers carefully and test them to be sure they are directly over the ears. And that their microphones are not right in front of their mouths, oriented the correct way.I hope this was helpful, and I hope you will enjoy yours as much as we are. Thanks for reading!*****Update: Sena has a newer updated model called the SMH-10B which is version 2.0 of this headset. They are identical except the new ones have a micro USB port for charging, instead of the old charging port. Plus, with the USB connection and cord, you can flash upgrade the software if and when they have updates available.Honestly, this is not a big deal. Nice, but not a huge requirement. So, if you don't see the ad description specifically mention SMH-10B (with a B) then you are getting the prior version. Sena no longer sells the older series.At the time of this review, Amazon does not currently have the new version. It just came out. But, you can order them from several other places at the exact same 299 price, with free shipping. For example, check out RevZilla. Same price, free shipping.

I have written reviews and tested numerous other phone covers. This was the most disappointing so far.The packaging:The box was large for what was included but served the purpose.  About 80% of the width is wasted air space to make it look good on shelvesThe contents:There was three clear (no anti-glare or matte finish so we have no confusion), a cleaning cloth and a rigid, credit card sized cardboard for smoothing the screenThe screen:Definitely not the right size for the 4G HTC EVO.  Way too small to cover the entire screen height so I went with covering the main area and the buttons only. The width was just a bit short of covering the entire phone.The sides are labeled front and back with tabs that peel off the thin layer which made it easy to see how to place it.The application:This was frustrating and I tried different ways that I have done other screens to compare.1) I carefully cleaned the phone with the cleaning cloth. I carefully peeled the backing off and before I could move it drew tiny particles from the air that stuck to it.  First time I had that happen.  I cleaned those off and applied it and smoothed it out.  For some reason it would no go on right nor smooth.2) This time I did what the others normally do (see theZAGG FFHTCEVO4GS invisibleSHIELD for HTC EVO 4G LTE - 1 Pack - Retail Packaging - Clear)and I used a spray found with it and sprayed the phone and screen.  No dirt appeared and it was so easy to apply. I could easily move it in place and used the tool to smooth out the bubbles.Now in saying that peeling off the front tab both times caused it to peel the new screen guard back off the phone and leave bubbles and the edges would never go back.  So I pulled the front tab corner loose before starting the third time and used choice two above of wetting it one more time and was successful.So I was very disappointed in the packaging and the sizing of the screen guards. It specifically says HTC EVO 4G and these screen are very short and cut wrong.  The top of the phone screen is squared corners and the bottom is rounded.  The screen guard is all rounded edges besides not covering it.

There was a lot to like about this headset.  I bought it primarily for use at the gym.  The best feature of this headset is that it's got a great clip that expands easily.  I used this for music at the gym with an iPhone 4.  Pairing was not a problem.  Charging was initially not a problem.  The music quality was spot on and not a problem.  I will confess that I didn't use their ear buds but went with a pair of Sennheiser earbuds that wrap onto my ears.  (no slippage during a run)My troubles started with intermittent failures to charge.  First the unit wouldn't turn on.  So I'd charge it again. Belkin 2.1A / 10W chargerI've used numerous Belkin chargers in the past and they all had one thing in common. They have problems fully charging my Sony AS-10 Action camera, and they will not charge my BlackBerry Bold 9900 or 9930.This particular model would not charge the BlackBerry Bold 9900/9930, the phone would give me an error message saying there's insufficient power from the adapter. My Motorola Droid2 charger (0.8A) and my Samsung Galaxy S3 charger (1A) will charge the Bold 9900 without any problems.This Belkin would not fully charge the Sony AS-10, and only provides 2h 15min of recording time while my Droid2/S3 chargers will charge the AS-10 to provide 2h 32min of recording time.However, this Belkin charger will charge up my Kindle Fire HD 7" without any problems. But it does produce a high pitched tune when charging the Sony AS-10 camera.The one thing that really bugged me was that the AC prongs are not rotated 90 degrees, so this Belkin takes up valuable space on a standard power strip or surge protector.This charger also did not come with a USB cable possibly for cost savings.Using the Kill A Watt meter, the Belkin 2.1A consumes 9.6W of power when charging the Kindle Fire HD 7". This is just about 1 Watt shy of the full 2.1A rating, but that said, it is providing more current than the typical smartphone charger when connected to the KFHD7".Overall: 4/5 stars, nothing really exceptional about this charger other than providing a slightly higher current to charge devices like the Kindle Fire HD 7". It won't charge my BlackBerry Bold 9900, it makes a high frequency pitch when charging the Sony AS10.

My sister in law received one of these as a gift and she was elated.  She works long hours and is the go to answer person in her large real estate title company.  Shortly after she got it she called me and asked me to look at it and tell her what was wrong.  She had used the power pack several times and she could only charge her iPhone 4 about 50%.  She had borrowed one of my wife's larger power packs that could charge her phone 3  times.  She wanted to know why hers didn't work as well.This unit, while cute, is absolutely underpowered.  It claims to have 1900 mAh capacity but in reality that is not the real charging capacity of the device when you consider how it works.  The battery pack has internal electronics that consumes power, the connector creates a contact that inserts some resistance and the iPhone has internal electronics in the charging circuit that consumes power and also drops the voltage from 5 VDC to 3.7 VDC to charge the internal battery of the iPhone. (more power lost)  The final efficiency of the charging is somewhere around 75% of the rated capacity.  This means that 75% EQUALS ONLY 1,425 mAh capacity to charge an iPhone 4 that has a 1,420 mAh Lithium Polymer battery cell.  That means that the likely hood of getting a full charge for your iPhone is about zero.Her experience was only a little over 50% and she returned it to the store it was purchased from.  I think you can see from the number of poor reviews and the new selling price that this product has some serious performance issues.  My recommendation is to buy a better power pack!

The port identification markings are somewhat difficult to read by being the same color as the background but the LCD screen identifies what port is being used. I was looking for a clear case for my new iPhone 5S and after reading the positive reviews for this case, I decided to buy it. I was really looking forward to trying this case on my new iPhone 5S. I understand that clear plastic cases will eventually scratch but being scratched out of the box before even using it is totally unacceptable.I contacted Rearth's customer service to let them know that the case came defective and that I wasn't satisfied. Well, guess what? They haven't responded yet. I contacted them 4 days ago and they have yet to acknowledge my issue. Does Rearth have a customer service department? I don't know yet, because they haven't answered my call. This is one of the worst customer service departments I know. I have bought several cases from other big brand company's like Speck, Otterbox, Griffin and Spigen and they all have customer service departments that actually care about customer satisfaction. How can you run a company that needs sales revenue to be in business and yet you don't care if a customer is unsatisfied with your product? I would have been a satisfied customer if my issue was acknowledged promptly and a replacement case issued, because this might have been an isolated incident.I am very unhappy with my purchase of this case and it's been the only negative experience I've had on Amazon. I purchased this Samsung charger through this listing from "Clearance Masters--Ships from Hong Kong".  The charger delivered is a counterfeit knockoff of the true OEM Samsung charger. Its housing and external markings are perfectly forged -- they deceptively mimic the Samsung logo and certification agency marks. I measured the output of the charger and compared its actual capability against its stated specification. The charger should provide a well regulated 5V at up to 1 Amp.  Instead, it provides a poorly regulated 5.26 V open circuit, decreasing steadily with current to 4.1V at 410 mA, then to about 3.5V at 430 mA. Attempts to draw more current result in further rapid voltage drop. That's quite a deviation from the 5V 1A marked on the back of the enclosure. The too great voltage when unloaded is a hazard to some devices, and the drop in voltage at moderate current draw will cause unpredictable results when charging many cell phones. The charger will not rapid-charge phones expecting a true 1A capability.  I've posted a picture of the counterfeit's innards.If you device doesn't require much power, it may appear to you that this charger works adequately.  However, beware: its internal circuitry is inherently unreliable and may pose a fire or shock hazard.Since writing this review I've stumbled across a comprehensive test and comparison of true chargers vs counterfeit chargers, which you may find helpful: [...] (Amazon doesn't seem to be allowing me to post the link, but you can find it by searching for a-dozen-usb-chargers-in-lab-apple-is)

The 2-material (strong + fluffy) AmazonBasics PC/Silicon Case for iPhone 5 has not only all the benefits of the concept, but also all the disadvantages of the innovative, though flawed design, that exceed the benefits.  The polycarbonate (PC) shell swaddles and provides strength to the fluffy and shock absorbing silicon core, but the thick case sticks up too high next to the screen edge that makes it very difficult, if not impossible for some, touching the screen next to the edge using the thumb.  In cases where only silicon turns up and is close the the screen edges the pressing thumbs can compress the soft silicon and this difficulty is not as pronounced.  However, AmazonBasics has also the hard PC shell turned up that prevents squeezing the silicon.  If the hard PC shell was turned up along the shorter sides of the case, instead of the longer, that problem would not exist.Otherwise, the case is good.  It is light, rounded as without edges, fits perfectly, not slippery, does not cover anything requiring direct access including controls and dock connector allowing charging while in case, has very functional protrusions covering the buttons and allowing to feel and press them as without the cover. The surface is smooth and does not attract grime, though it can be easily washed in the unlikely event.  It seems inferior to more expensive luxurious Spigen cases and similarly priced Belkin Shield cases, which also have simplistic cutouts for the buttons instead of the covering protrusions covering the buttons and allowing to feel and press them as without the cover.

2/27/2014 Update:So disappointed this little guy quit working about a month ago. It won't recharge and the phone will not recognize that it is connected. It just blinks the charging LED's and does nothing else.  So I guess it's useless now, as I have tried letting the battery delete and then re-charge - but nope, still fails to charge my Galaxy S3 and will mostly likely have to be thrown out at a battery recycle depot.  Since I bought it in June or July last year, that gives me about 6 or 7 months use from brand new.  At $25 or so, that calculates to cost about $4 a month for 6 months.  To be honest it was sort of worth it as I always used it on my Galaxy S3 at the end of the day (very handy to have vs. your phone dying).  If this thing lasted a couple years like my laptop batteries - it would have been the champ I'd hoped it would be.  But 7 months?  That's just disappointing. I'll get another external battery pack, but it will be another brand.11/18/13 Update:I've owned this now for a few months.  Realistically it seems to fully charge my Galaxy S3 full once and leaves about 15% power left until empty. This is fine for my S3 but NOT a good choice for more power hungry devices like Galaxy Note, or any Tablet.  For those devices you'll get a partial charge - but not multiple charges by any means.  I tend to use my Limeade a lot when I can't use a charge cord.  Like at the end of the day when I'm down to 15% power on the phone and just want to keep checking emails and reading news without having to resign to plug it in to its overnight charger.  The limeade will keep me going for hours longer without much to hang on to (due to its smaller size).  In summary after about 4 months use I still like it and find it very useful.>>>>>>Original review circa July 2013The Limeade Burst L50X2M unit is approximately 5 inches in length, by 2 and-a-half inches wide, by one-half inch thick - and comes with a full size, built-in USB cord (plugs into your computer or laptop to charge the unit itself) and a SECOND built-in "Micro-USB" charger used to charge your phone or tablet.  The built-in cord feature is a BIG plus, because - if you're like me - you're going to end up misplacing the external plug-in cords as you move your charger around.The Limeade Burst L50X2M is a slim - but powerful portable cell phone charger.  "5000mAh" (milliampere-hour) is more powerful than slightly, lighter and slimmer, 3000mAh portable units I've had in the past - this translates to a quicker charge, and the ability to charge bigger items, or charge your phone more often (before you have to eventually recharge the Limeade unit).  When you buy a unit over 5000mAh, you are going to get a bulkier and/or heavier unit, thus reducing some portability or "slip-in-your-pocket - forget about it" convenience.  The Limeade unit seems to be the perfect match of "slim size" and "decent recharging power".  Note that at 5000mAh, this unit may be best suited for partially (or fully) charging smart phones (not tablets).  If you need to charge a tablet you will want to purchase a 10,000mAh, or higher powered external battery charger.  For cell phones, the Limeade Burst L50X2M does not provide a quick charge - it takes about 30 mins to an hour to charge my Samsung Galaxy S3.Included in the packaging was a black "velvet" bag to carry the Limeade in, as well as one full-size USB (computer size) external charging cord with adapters for Mini-USB, Micro USB, and what looks like an iPhone adapter.  On the unit itself there is a power button (with auto shut off), four small green LED power level indicators (4 indicate full charge, 3 indicate 50-75% charge, etc.) and TWO full sized USB slots on the unit (one of which is 2.1A which is an even faster charging slot), means you could (theoretically and mostly likely only partially) charge up to three phones (if you had a third external connector).  All of that is probably more than you will ever need, and at $25 for all this, seems like a real deal ...providing it lasts.  I will update in 6 months or so.I guess my only tiny complaint is the outer surface is an almost imperceptibly tacky or "rougher" matte black, and tends to show marks from your sweaty fingers. I think I would have preferred a smooth black surface.

I think this product is like other similar products – though I have to admit that this was my first experience with such product that has sticky surface for a phone holder. As expected, the sticky surface does catch dust and need to be washed/cleaned more often.As a general-purpose phone holder (i.e. with arms), I would give it 3 stars. As a phone holder with sticky surface (without using the arms), it is 1.5 stars. I’ll explain why:I have a Nokia Lumia 920 which is somewhat heavier than the iPhones and the Androids. So I knew that it would be a real test for this holder to see how well it can hold the phone. The problem is that although it can hold the phone, you have to make efforts to make sure the phone is fully stuck to it and won’t fall down. I have had mine fall down at least 3 time. That is when I decided to use the arms it helps to keep your phone in place when the sticky surface fails.The NFC tag works but can be annoying – depending on you use it. I have mine set up to bring up Bluetooth menu but the problem is that there is no option (at least in Lumia 920) to enable or disable Bluetooth with NFC scan. I could bring up the Bluetooth menu and then turn it on/off from there but it’s that’s an extra step – not worth the effort. Also, when the phone is unlocked, it scan NFC again and keep bringing up the Bluetooth menu. I was there was a way to disable NFC on the holder instead of turning it off from the phone.My experience with this product has not been positive. If you are buying this for NFC, I highly recommend that you test your phone with any other custom NFC tag to see how you would like to use it with this holder. If you don’t need the added NFC, I recommend getting a different phone holder that does not include it.

This is my first Bluetooth headset. I got it when I was on the verge of upgrading to an Android smartphone, and wanted to have a hands-free headset for the car. I decided to wait on the phone upgrade, so I have not had a chance to try it with a phone yet.I have tried the device with two netbooks, both from Dell, both running Ubuntu. on the A90 Vostro, the headset found sync and worked really well very quickly. On the older Dell Mini 9, I have yet to get it to work. I am waiting on a Bluetooth USB dongle for further testing on various computers.The headset uses an internal rechargeable battery which charges in just an hour or two. When that dies, you're out of luck until you can recharge it again - the downside of rechargeable.The build quality and sound quality are good for this kind of headset, and I had some fun recording into Audacity and playing it back. I think this would be a good headset for web conferencing, and I will test it in that arena.I will update when I have had a chance to test it with more devices.UPDATE: The Bluetooth dongle arrived (iogear Bluetooth 2.1 USB Micro Adapter) and that seems to work with my Linux machines, however this headset will not link with the dongle in 2 different machines. That means that this headset is only able to link with 1 out of 3 devices I have tried - not a good sign. It might very well be the best screen protector for this type of screen but they made it more difficult to install by fitting it to the (almost) entire phone.  Rather then make it fit the rectangular shape of the screen itself they have made it to fit directly around and underneath the earpiece and light sensor and then down past the end of the screen to the hard button on the phone.  So you pratically have to be an draftsman or mechanical engineer to set this hard to see thin film between these two points and precisely along the outer edges of the screen's width.  Its crazy, all they had to do was cover the screen which is a simple rectangle and leave the rest of the phone which isn't much uncovered.  The good thing is they give you two because unless you are one of the above you are probably going to screw up the first one.Update: Within one day of writing this review I received a phone call from Charlie @ Rearth, he and I had a very nice conversation about my review and he went on to explain that his company's market research indicates that most customers want a protector that covers the entire phone and because of that, they created this product.I was amazed at the speed of this company's response.  I didn't write the review with the expectation of a call back or an email for that matter.  I just wanted to let people know that this product, while a great protector was difficult to install.Charlie offered and I accepted his offer of another package of screen protectors. Although my first thought was this time I am going to cut the bottom off before installation, I have rethought that process and think that perhaps I will give rEarth a call to see if there is anything I can do better to get this on correctly.  I have the last screen protector on the phone it is just a hair off center putting the cutout at the bottom just a 1/32" over the hard button which leaves the screen protector with a small lifted opening but not on the screen. Other then that this protector looks and feels beautiful.  I am torn now as to whether I should leave it alone or try again with the new package, because my first attempt was a disaster.  Any suggestions?

A surprisingly good fit, that gives my Galaxy Nexus new-found endurance.I really like my Galaxy Nexus and use it heavily, but between steady usage and sometimes poor Sprint coverage, I was charging or topping-off the factory-original battery 1-2, even 3 times a day, and it was making my phone much less useful-- running low or dying outright (usually at inconvenient times).I didn't expect much of this third-party battery, and figured it would be an awkward fit, but it turns out to fit the Nexus very snugly and precisely-- it would be a struggle to get the replacement cover off the phone now (it was pretty easy to get into place). The new back is rubbery instead of plasticy like the original.  It doesn't look elegant, but it works fine, and after two months of constant use hasn't show any signs of wear.And the battery life... Oh the battery life!  I got 3 days, 4 hours on battery the very first time I had this ZeroLemon installed-- and it wasn't even completely drained at the end of that. Typically I charge the battery every 2 days now, just to keep a full charge.  Quite the improvement!Do follow the vendor's recommended charging procedures, but even without doing it rigorously, the results are excellent (my first-time results were from charging a few hours less than recommended).  So charging directions are very particular, and are certainly going to give optimal results, but don't sweat it if you don't follow them exactly.Also, the ZeroLemon does still support NFC, so you don't lose that function by going with an aftermarket battery.  It really Just Works, which is a great thing (and sometimes unusual in the aftermarket product sector).As far as caveats and concerns: The side buttons take a slightly firmer press now (through the rubber shell), but still work fine. The speakerphone (which I rarely use) is a bit muffled by being recessed more deeply than before.  If charging while powered off, the battery symbol shows full soon enough, but you shouldn't detach it yet (booted up, you'll see it's still charging). Since the battery capacity now shows red/critically low for many hours (up to a day in my use) before failing, you may also find Android stops polling for updates before it needed to (that's adjustable, but the default settings turn off syncing/updating when battery is critical).The large battery also takes a while to charge, so you can't just throw it on the charger for a few minutes to top-up (but neither do you have to top-up often), instead I usually plan when I'll need to charge the phone and do so overnight so it's fully charged and ready to go when I leave in the morning. Exchanging the old constant charging and being tethered to USB power, for occasionally having to plan ahead when to charge is a decent trade-off in my book.The other big adjustment I had to make was the increased heft-- the phone is now much bigger and much heavier than before, although it still fits well in my front jeans pocket. Holding the phone up for a while (like lying in bed) definitely reminds my wrist that the phone weighs more now.  The grip still feels natural in my hand.[ I was given this product for the purposes of review. I received no other compensation, and my review is my honest opinion of the product. ]I'd wish for better accuracy in how Android perceives its battery capacity-- the extended "critical" period makes it hard to predict how much juice you really have left. The brand name might be on the unusual side, but the extended run-time and easy integration make this ZeroLemon high-capacity battery a winner. Highly Recommended.

DESIGNThis is a very interesting design.  All they did was took a regular iPhone holder and added a "cone" or outlet under the speaker and direct the sound out the right side out of a much larger plastic opening.  Others have done it with docks and such, so Kensington did it in the car.  The microphone also is "coned" and it goes through it windscreen and out the bottom.  The arms holding the phone are spring loaded and open wide.COMPATIBILITYIt works well with my iPhone 4 with mySwitchEasy TRIM Hybrid Case for iPhone 4 (Black  Fits AT&T; iPhone)and it could hold bigger cases, too.  Since my case isn't rounded in the back (like the iPhone 3GS), it is tough to get into the holder with one hand. Since I don't want to remove my phone from the case, there is a gap between the speaker itself and the "cone," so it won't be able to capture the sound as well and amplify as loudly.It holds an iPhone 3GS in aSwitchEasyRebel Case For iPhone 3G (Skeleton)just fine, BUT the speaker is on the left of the 3GS, so that is why Kensington has this product marked as compatible with the iPhone 4 only. With the rounded case, it slides into the holder with a slight push.It has a nice, large suction cup with flexible arm for windshield use and a vent attachment. While my Garmin suction cup falls of with temperature changes, this suction cup hasn't come off yet (I've had it for about a year).  The arm is 7 inches long, which makes it very nice to put the phone exactly where you want it when using the suction cup.  In my 2002 Grand Am or 2004 Jeep Liberty, the vent holder wouldn't work.  It looks like you need to have vents that are not too deep and with decent spacing in between the fins to fit this. With the 7 inch arm, it can be nice to get it closer to you, but it also tends to be a little bit wobbly.All of my charging cables are able to be put into the bottom connector of the phone while in the mount.SOUNDIt does help amplify the sound some, but it seems to me that it only really helps much if the output of the sound it facing you.  They designed it with the sound going out the right side (the speaker is on the right side of the phone), so if you are the driver and this is in the middle, the sound will be going away from you.  If you have it sideways, you have a choice to point the sound up or down.  It sounds the best to me with the sound directed upwards as the driver, which is not how I use the phone.One thing I had to be careful with is the position of the phone in the holder.  The arms allow is to move left-to-right, which can make the speaker move off of the "cone" gathering the sound.Overall, this is a really great holder if you are not expecting a high fidelity powered amplifier.

Edited 10/26/2013-> Added negative information about built-in screen protectorI always take care of my smart phones.  They usually look new when I am done with them.  My last phone was a Motorola Droid, which I housed in a TPU case.  It looked nice and protected well for scratches. However, when taking one of my walks I tripped. The phone did not fall far - but it was far enough.  Devastation!My new phone is a Samsung S4.  I love the phone - but it does not seem as sturdy as my old Droid. And that Droid shattered. So I need to protect my S4.  I purchased two top rated cases to see what is best for my needs: the OtterBox Defender and the Trident AMS Kraken.Summary:Both the Kraken and the OtterBox Defender were real close for my needs.  The one that I ended up using is the Trident AMS Kraken.My final choice was because I felt the Kraken to be somewhat better than the OtterBox.  I got quite frustrated by the proximity sensor not working correctly with the OtterBox.  This prevented me from accessing the phone after the screen turned off during calls.  I also felt that the body of the Kraken would protect the phone better.If interested in my reviews, I have another write up for the OtterBox case.Detailed review of the Trident AMS Kraken case:Appearance: Looks great.  I like the appearance of the Trident AMS Kraken protective case for the Samsung Galaxy S4.  My case is the red one.  The mix of the red polycarbonate against the black silicon looks great.  The svelte S4 now looks like a tank.  Oh well!  Far more important to protect the thing.  So I guess the way to really define how it looks is that it looks like a great tank.Physical characteristics:The inner silicon liner is soft and pliable.  I think that it should do a good job to shield the unit from shock and vibration.  I like the idea of a soft cushioning shell surrounding the phone itself.  The silicon is not as thick as used with other cases, but seems to be sufficient to provide a good amount of shock absorption.The hardened polycarbonate plastic shell fits well, firmly holding the phone in place.  The shell has some integrated silicon to further protect the case, including molded bumpers that protrude to protect each corner, soft runners around the perimeter of the case, and covers for switches and connectors.  Since much screen damage occurs when a phone has impact on the corners, the bumpers are a great idea.The silicon case extends to protect the volume rocker and the power switch. These are lightweight and do not make the switches hard to use.  Some cases that provide this protection use silicon that is so thick and sturdy (or stiff) that it makes the switch hard to use.  Only negative is that since this works so well, that the power switch might activate while in your pocket.  This is really a tradeoff.  For the Kraken, I feel that the switch is not as easily able to be pressed accidently as it would with an unprotected S4, but it is not as stiff as it can be with other protective cases.  The on/off and volume control switches work easily through the protective cover.Protective covers are provided for the headphone jack and the USB connection.  The covers fit nice and work great.  I feel that the material is a little flimsy - but I really don't care.  I doubt that these will wear too much.  I particularly like the cover over the USB connection.  With some other cases the covers make it more difficult to use the USB connection. With the Kraken the USB connection works effortlessly.The Kraken case has several slots cut out of the polycarbonate on the back that look like they are designed to help with ventilation.  These phones get hot - and with these secure cases there can be quite a bit of heat buildup.  I think that is a good idea - plus it looks good.The polycarbonate front bevel extends too much over the phones screen.  This makes it hard to catch the notification bar. The two finger swipe to pull down the quick settings menu is particularly hard.  Some apps with side swipe are difficult to use.  Since there is less space at the bottom of the screen, you can accidently hit an icon at the bottom of the screen when you press the home, menu or back buttons.The hard plastic case is somewhat slippery on flat surfaces. The case fully protects the front of the phone if you place it face down on a surface.  The case is little slippery, but the protuberances designed into the case help make it feel that it will not slip out of one's hand.  Easily slides into and out of your pocket.The silicon liner picks up an incredible amount of pet hair.  Be careful to clean it well before applying the outer shell.Other factors:Sound quality - phone calls:  No degradation of sound quality for either party.  Microphone and ear positioned speaker work fine.Sound quality - phone speaker for music: no degradation of sound quality.  Not that the S4 speaker is that great in the first place .. but the case does not make it any worse.Sensor access: The S4 proximity sensor is intermittent with this case.  It usually works - but sometimes remains stuck.  Why is this important?  One use of the proximity sensor is to determine when to turn off the screen.  If you get a phone call and you put the phone to your ear, the proximity sensor will turn off the screen. Access to the touch screen is prevented so that you cannot accidentally press buttons. The problem is that the sensor may not detect when the phone is moved away from the ear.  When you move the screen away from your ear, the screen should become visible and you should be able to use the touch screen. With the proximity sensor not working, the screen does not free up until the other party hangs up from your call.  If the other person does not hang up you cannot use your screen again! During the call you cannot look up contacts, or do any of the other multi-tasking that your phone is designed for.  On some S4 forums, I read that black cases do not seem to have as much of this problem.  I also read that some people claim use of a black magic marker around the opening where the sensor is located helps.  I tried using a black magic marker and the sensor works most of the time.  Carefully applied you can hardly see the black. A suggestion to Trident is to make the polycarbonate slightly thinner in this area and surround the area with black silicon.Screen protector: the top portion of the polycarbonate shell has an integrated screen protector.  Unlike the poorly written instruction manual that indicates you need to apply the screen protector yourself, the protector is already installed. The material is a lighter material than used in other protective cases. You do not notice it at all when using the phone.  However, it picks up fingerprints, lint and pet hair easily.  The fingerprints do not readily come off with my usual eyeglass lens cleaning cloth. I used eScreen water based polymer coating screen cleaner - that worked great. That also helped to reduce finger prints for a very short time. There seems to be an oily film under the protector when you view the protector with the screen off.  However, it does not seem to affect any quality of viewing when the screen is on.Screen protector update (10/26/2013): Screen protector scratches easily.  Fingerprints are impossible.  By the end of a day I cannot stand the fingerprints.  Dirt and dust easily get under the screen protector.  The screen protector also is not as responsive as the original 'gorilla' glass.  My solution: I purchased a Neo HD Crystal JL31.6 screen protector.  I took off my case, peeled off the built-in protector.  I added the Neo screen protector and put the case back together.  The Neo protector ended up messed up in a couple of places around the edges where the case exerts pressure. However, even with this problem this screen protector works so much better than the original Trident supplied plastic.Assembly: first couple of times it was difficult to get the case on and off.  After doing this a few times, it now is pretty easy.  Instructions are poor.Kickstand: neat idea, not quite implemented as well as it could be.  When closed, the kickstand is not flush or below the level of the polycarbonate shell.  It actually sticks out a little bit.  When the phone is put face up on a flat surface, the phone rests on the kickstand rather than the case.  This makes the phone tippy, and could potentially harm some surfaces.  What if you remove the kickstand so the phone will lay flat?  Without the kickstand, the area where it connects to the back can catch on things in one's pocket, has sharp edges.  Also, the case does not look as nice.  When attached and extended, the kickstand is not terribly stable.  The instructions are very poor in explaining how to remove the kickstand.  Here is how you do it: push the metal from left to right until it clicks.  Then you can lift it off of the case.Belt clip: two problems.  First - it does not seem to be secure enough when clipped.  Second - since it clips to the front of the phone you cannot use the phone until you remove the clip. You will not have time to answer a call if you have to remove the case.  However, if you have a Bluetooth headset this can work.  I will not bother with the clip.

I have an iPhone 4s and had been using an Otterbox Defender case, which I was extremely pleased with. However, I noticed that I could easily run my battery down to nothing on days when I needed to talk and use the internet heavily, especially when traveling. I had been looking for some kind of battery back up system when I got the opportunity to review the Spyder Battery Case.The battery case itself is surprisingly sleek - not really any bulkier than the Otterbox Defender I had been using. However, if you cherish the ultra-slim nature of your iPhone 4s, you may not like the bulk this battery case adds. It is heavier than a regular case because it has a battery incorporated into it. It's made of a rigid, satin finish, rubberized polycarbonate and fits my 4s perfectly. The cut outs for buttons, camera lens and speakers align exactly. The case has metal contacts in the bottom for using with the charge/sync dock and a mini-usb port for charging without the dock. There is also an on/off button on the back and 4, blue LEDs which indicate charging status and level of remaining charge.If you wish to set up the dock as a charging station without connecting to your computer, you will need to purchase a usb power adapter (the usb cable is included). However, you can simply plug the charging/sync dock into a usb port on your computer and eliminate the need to buy a usb power adapter. I LOVE that my phone inside the case sits securely in the dock for charging. Previously, I have been plugging a charging cable directly into the proprietary Apple charging port every night, but was worried as I have heard that it is easy to damage the port. This is so much better!So how well does the battery work? It more than doubles your iPhone's battery capacity!!! The iPhone 4s has an internal, 1432 mAh battery. The Spyder battery case adds another 2000 mAh of battery power to your phone. Fantastic! It is an A-grade, lithium-ion polymer cell battery for larger capacity and more charge cycles.Spyder recommends that you let your iPhone drain to 20% capacity or less before you turn on the Spyder case. My phone was up to 100% (from less than 20%) in a little over an hour and there was still juice to spare in the case!I am absolutely thrilled with this case and dock. It meets all my needs and more and keeps me from having to even think about my phone battery, no matter how much I use it in a day.The only negative I have is that I wish the case had an extended lip around the screen of the phone and was maybe a little less sleek and a little more protective. I am used to a Fort Knox level of protection with the Otterbox Defender. The Spyder battery case sits flush with the screen, so if I drop it on gravel or other rough surfaces, there is no protection for the front of the phone at all. Hence, I had to buy a screen protector. (My Otterbox case had a screen protector built-in.) But for more than doubling my phone's battery life and the convenience of a charging dock I can just sit my phone in, I'm thrilled and I am more than willing to be a little more careful with my phone.I HIGHLY recommend this item.

The cable that Apple supplies is too short. These cables are a good length and good for either home use or travel. I like it for travel, as many hotels and ships have outlets that are not close to where you may want to put your phone or iPad.

The iPad has a pretty high current requirement so it's important to get a charger with a 2.1 amp output or it won't charge. This Belkin charger has that capability and has no problem charging my iPad2. The plug folds down for better portability and pivots 90. The charger appears to be very well-made, small, and light. The charging cord is well-made, too, but it is a sync cord that is limited to 4 ft. Sad, but true, but I rarely sync the iPad with a cord now that iCloud is set up, so what I need most is a long charging cord. Since this one is short, I use a 6 ft extension to allow me to charge the iPad and still lay down on the couch and use it. But even though this cord is heavy-duty, it has one major drawback: it does not have positive latches to hold the connector to the iPad, so the slightest movement causes it to pop out. Still, a functional replacement cord is easy to obtain and cheap, so I won't rate this down much, but I will take one star since the cord is part of the price. For anyone looking for a quality, 2.1 amp USB charger, this charger should fit the bill. If Belkin made this without a cord and charged less, I'd give it 5 stars.

For me, this product didn't charge well, if it charged at all. I don't think my unit is defective. With a little jiggling around I can get my Samsung Charging pad's LED to turn steady green (indicating the phone battery is supposed to be charging). However, a full 9 hour overnight charge left my battery indicator showing essentially the same percentage charge in the AM that the Note 3 had when I started to charge it the night before. Not acceptable.  A little Scotch tape on the edges of the receiver card to hold it down tighter to the battery didn't help. In contrast, with the Samsung 2 amp wired travel charger that came with the phone, the phone charges to 100% in just a few hours. I do like the fact that Fonesaleman includes two small plugs (one black, one white) to cover the USB 3 connector so that it doesn't get dirty.I guess this type of Qi receiver is a hit or miss proposition as many have reported good results. I now have a genuine Samsung Wireless charging back S cover on backorder at Amazon.Edit 02/05/14: The Samsung charging back S cover arrived and works flawlessly. A note about wireless charging - it takes at least 2x longer to charge a phone with any wireless "Qi type" charger as opposed to the more powerful portable charger that comes with the Note 3 phone. This, I learned, is normal. It still doesn't change my opinion of the "Fonesalesman" product.

Updated review:  Had the phone for three months, and then it just died without warning. We ordered from Techgiant, a third-party merchant here on Amazon, and they informed us that there was nothing they would do to remedy this (nice, no?). Contacted Samsung, who informed us that since the phone was manufactured in Vietnam, they are not responsible for any warranty within the U.S.  We took it to a local cell phone repair shop, and apparently the main board fried....no idea how or why....to the point where they said repairing it would be cost prohibitive. Contacted Amazon, and God bless them, they gave us a partial refund as an effort to lessen our heartache.My wife still loves the phone, so we're going to get an unlocked version through Staples, and we will buy a warranty through them as well. We would buy directly from Amazon instead, but they only sell this through third-party merchants. We will pay about $100 more that we would online just for the phone, but the peace of mind we'll have getting it through a reputable company makes it worth the extra cost.If you read all of the reviews, you'll see our experience is not unique. So, purchasing this phone online, through a third-party vendor, is a gamble.  If you choose to roll the dice, I hope you have better luck than we did.  I felt obliged to update this review to let you know of our very bad experience so maybe you can avoid having to go through something similar.Original review:My wife was tethered to T-Horrible by a contract for several years, which we finally broke away from....with a penalty, of course.  It was worth it.  They treat their customers like dirt.We then switched to Virgin Mobile, which didn't work out so well either due to bad connectivity and bad customer service (but not as bad as T-Horrible, by any means...the main issue was being on hold for an hour to speak to someone in India, and usually subsequently getting disconnected).  Plus, at least with the Virgin plan, we were not bound to a contract....something we vowed to not repeat if possible.We bought this Samsung phone and switched over to Straight Talk, and couldn't be happier.  Connectivity is great, as is their customer service (so far).We ordered a micro SIM card with the AT&T; format.  Even going with their higher priced unlimited $45 per month no-contract plan, I figure it will take about a year in savings as compared to a contract plan to break even on what I paid for the phone.We contacted Straight Talk and made it clear that she wanted to keep her old number.  We were told it could take a day or two to process everything and make the switch.  Well, that was wrong....it took about an hour!  We ordered the 30-day unlimited plan and the SIM card online from their website, and it arrived the next day (the had a special offer for free overnight shipping).My wife's only complaint about the phone itself is how complex it is; it takes some time to learn everything it can do and how to make adjustments (e.g., why is it making that noise every now and then?).  It really is an amazing device....the screen clarity and all the options it offers will blow you away!If things change, I will update the review, but right now we are very happy with our decision to go with this phone and with Straight Talk as a service provider.

I always hate the thought of covering the iPhones' typically beautiful design with an obtrusive case, but I'm very pleased with this one... the frost clear version of this case looks absolutely wonderful on the white iPhone 5, complimenting it nicely and preserving the look of the device.  I tried a similar case from Best Buy, and although it was ok, it had a mildly annoying "bubble" effect on the rear of the device (where the smooth case contacted the smooth surface of the top and bottom sections of the back).  The Kay's case does not suffer from this problem, presumably due to the frosted surface.I do, however, expect it to eventually discolor.  The case I used with my iPhone 4 was the Belkin Grip Vue, which was similar to the Kay's case.  After 6 - 9 months or so, it started to yellow, presumably due to being handled all the time.  Perhaps the Kay's case will not do this, but if it does, my assumption is that it's simply the nature of this kind of material, and will replace it as needed.A small mold mark is visible on the edge... noticeable, but not horrendously so.The screen protector was included, but I typically don't use these, so I can't comment on its quality.Edit 11/7/12: Dropped to 4 stars for the fact that cases sold now have that hideously ugly "Kay's Case" branding.  I don't accept the seller's argument that they had to do this to "protect the customers".  Ironically, I suspect this could have the opposite of the intended effect (which I presume was a desire to increase sales by combatting knockoffs).  It cuts into the appeal of the product, and I personally am going to look elsewhere to see if I can find a similar case with no prominent branding like this, even if it means buying one of the Kay's Case "knockoffs".Remove the branding, and I will immediately order 2 more to keep as spares to use throughout the service life of my iPhone 5.  As a last resort, if I'm not able to find a suitable replacement (if/when that time comes), I will reluctantly buy another Kay's Case and just try not to ever look at the back of the phone.

Finding a case for your iPhone can be a daunting task. There are hundreds (thousands maybe) of cases out there. I'm also incredibly picky. I figured I would buy a few cases and see which one worked best. I wanted to find a case that provided protection, but didn't hide the beauty of my new iPhone 4S. I decided to give this case a chance based on the positive reviews. Unfortunately, it didn't meet my expectations.While it does provide good protection for the iPhone on the sides, back, and even face, it is a very bulky case. It adds quite a bit of thickness to the iPhone. Also, it had surprisingly sharp edges. They started to wear down some, but they are still there. The back design is kinda ugly, but I could live with that. What was the deal breaker for me was that this thing is a lint magnet. The top has a lip that goes over the front of the screen. This is a good thing because if you drop it face up, the screen has some protection. Also, if you lay it flat on a hard surface, face up, the screen doesn't touch the surface. The problem with that is, when you put the phone in your pocket, this lip accumulates all kinds of lint and pushes it to the edge of the screen. You end up with a layer of lint and dust around the edges and under the lip. There's no easy way to clean that other than taking the whole case off. It's maddening. That's not something I can live with. If that's not a concern for you, and you want good looking, solid protection, then this case might be the way to go. After all, the price is certainly right.

If we're just talking about 'style', then this unit is pretty awesome. It's sleek, small but heavy (I like my tech products to feel heavy... weight typically suggests 'quality'), and the soft touch finish makes it comfortable to hold.HOWEVER, there are a number of oversights or stylistic choices which really keep this device from being a useful addition to your home/office:1) No chargers for Apple or Droid (micro-USB) devices. Why do we need a docking station that won't charge our phones?2) Also -- if you do plug in a charger, the phone won't stand upright... you have to turn it onto its side. A  minor annoyance, but an annoyance nonetheless.3) The handset is groovy, but... my phone is already a handset in and of itself. The 3.5mm jack is cool, I mean it fits in the phone... but it means I can't use my 2.5mm accessories, nor my normal 'analog' phone headset, nor my Bluetooth... so it's really just a redundant handset.BOTTOM LINE: to be honest, if I wasn't a Vine reviewer, I'd never have used this product. It strikes me as a superfluous and redundant accessory by its very design. For the money, I think people would be better off going with a nice Bluetooth or 3.5mm hands-free headset or headset / charger dock combo. Let's get one thing out of the way: you have to have patience with this product, both for installation and for it to set.If you can handle that, let's move on.  Almost everything that I am about to say reiterates the instructions, which are detailed and provide optimum results if followed.- Read all of the instructions that come with it before you begin the process.- Thoroughly clean the screen.  Ideally you want to wipe it down with a lens cleaning cloth and then give it a quick shot with compressed air- Keep your fingers and the skin wet.- Use the install card to push the excess air and solution out as you go along (more on this later)- Yes, the bubbles go away.  However, the more you have, the longer it takes.  Expect 2-4 days.  In the first 24 hours, you will notice that the bubbles have shrunk.  They will continue to do so until they have disappeared completely.  A majority of the bubbles that I had on mine were only visible when the screen was off, so it did not affect the screen clarity.A few side notes:- The reason why the instructions tell you to keep your phone off for 12-24 hours is because liquids (i.e. the solution) and electronics don't mix.  It is to allow time to dry in the event that some of the solution gets inside any of the openings in your handset.- I thought that my install card was missing. I found it after I already installed the skin when I took the box completely apart.  What I didn't think of at the time is that a credit card would have been a good substitute.  That being said, I didn't use anything to push the excess air and solution out as I went along and I still had good results.- What the heck is a microbubble?  The best way that I can describe them is that they are tiny spheres that don't hinder the feel of the screen as opposed to air pockets or "blisters" that noticeably raise the skin off of the screen. It is possible to have a cluster of air bubbles that can raise the skin but these go away just as the others do.- The skin gives a little more "grip" to the screen, not enough to where you it changes the feel of the phone.  I actually kind of like it for tap functions but it will feel less smooth on slide functions.- I don't notice any difference in display quality.  Without the skin, the display has a very smooth, glass surface.  With the skin, it reminds me of the surface of a glossy notebook screen.This is very different from the generic, trim-to-size, multi-pack screen protectors that just don't work.  If you are looking for a product similar to the Skinomi TechSkin, check out Zagg.

I didn't have a hands-free device for my car, so when I was offered the chance to try this product, I jumped at the offer.  It's a nice basic speaker for your car to make & receive phone calls.  It's simple to connect and get up and running (your phone must have Blue Tooth capabilities in order to use this product). It charges pretty quickly and holds it's charge for quite a while.I do have a few negatives or things I'd like to see them improve on:1.  Overall, the speaker transmits pretty clearly, but there are times the voices on the other end sound a little muffled and hard to understand.  If the person on the other end is speaking slowly and clearly, the reception is perfect, but if their voice is soft or their speech is rushed, it may be a little hard to understand what they're saying.  If you turn the volume up too loud, it makes it hard to understand the other person, and if you have the volume turned down, the other person needs to speak a little bit louder than normal in order to hear them accurately.2.  I wish there was a caller ID display on the speaker.  If you want to know who's calling, you still need to look at your phone.3.  The indicator light doesn't stay lit when the power is turned on so.  There have been quite a few times I turned the speaker on and off just to make sure it was working properly because I couldn't remember if I turned it on and there's no way to know if it's on or off (w/o looking at the on/off switch which you can't really do while you're driving!)4.  An additional charging source would be nice.  You have to plug it into your car lighter to charge the device, which means it can only charge when the car is on.  That means I have a cord hanging down from the visor to the lighter sometimes when I'm driving (which becomes a nuisance after a while).  Maybe there's an adapter I can buy or a separate charger, but it would be nice if the speaker came with a car charger and an electric charger upon purchase.My phone has voice dialing capabilities, but I have never used it.  I'll have to test it out with the speaker and report back the results once I've tried it.Overall, I like the product and it does what I wanted -- provides me with a way to answer my phone while I'm driving the car w/o having to use my actual phone.

I used to have an iPhone 3GS and used the Defender case with it. So when I upgraded to the 4S, it was no different. I had to have the Defender. A kind rep from ATT showed me how to do it even though I didn't buy it from the store. Once the phone was secure inside, everything fit smoothly. The product that I ordered doesn't quite live up to the description. It is a 3200mAh battery (at 3.7 volts) yet it can only provide 1 full charge + 1 25% charge of my 1432 mAh (@ 3.7 volts device). That represents 56% efficiency. While I wasn't expecting 100%, 70% seems to be the average. Although this is a nice looking and affordable external USB battery pack don't buy it thinking you're going to get much more than a single full charge on an iPhone 4s. I've gone through a couple of charge and discharge cycles and it hasn't made a difference.I am impressed with the Anker's responsiveness and follow-up on the issues and so I have updated my original review to reflect that and to give the product another couple of stars. Let me explain my reasoning for doing this: this kind of capacity issue is hard to track down and comparison with other battery packs is difficult. So although I am disappointed I also can't be certain that other products won't have this issue. I could dig spend a week trying different battery packs but I just don't have the time to do that and ultimately I would have wanted the product's capacity description to be a fair reflection of it's performance.

Ok, I've got a Galaxy S2, and figured I'd set it up for my sister.  Since the phone works on the ATT network, I figured I'd buy the Net10 SIM card and get it all ready to ship to her.I activated the phone, and it connected to the ATT network with about 3 bars of service.  My sister sent me a text and I replied.  I surfed the web using Firefox.  All was well.The following day after activation, I see no bars and a white X over where the bars should be. No browsing, no phone dialing or receive functions. Checking the available networks, I see that ATT and TMobile networks are visible.  No problem, the phone isn't provisioning on the ATT network. I called the toll free number, and waited... and waited...  And got through to first layer support.  After I explain the situation, he forwards me on to the level 2 support.The level 2 support lady is nice enough, but she keeps trying to tell me that I need at least 3 bars to surf the web.  Each time I tell her that the phone isn't connecting to the available network, her response is that I need at least 3 bars to surf the internet.  Not. Helpful.  She tells me that maybe I need to change my location, and to go outside.  I have a Galaxy S3 and so I put the phones side by side (one in each hand).  The S2 with a Net10 SIM shows not connected to network, while the S3 shows 4 bars when set to 3g mode.  Finally, she tells me to contact the manufacturer of my phone (Samsung) for troubleshooting assistance.  Again, Not. Helpful.I tried doing a factory reset, no change.  I went to the Net10 forum.  In the Samsung section of the forum are huge numbers of people with issues, each creating a thread.  In each case, a representative will post that they need to take the issue off line to resolve it.  And that's the end of the thread... no posting of what was done to fix the person's problem.  There are 62 pages of threads in the Samsung section alone.I decided to post a thread.  I created a user account on their forum, and posted.  But nothing shows, because it's a moderated forum.  The next day, my posting shows up on the site along with a representative's reply that I need to post *5 distinct posts* before I can chat with a customer service representative about my issue.  Ok, I can do this.  I post a dummy message.  I try to post another message, and get an error that I can't post messages that frequently.  You gotta be joking!My sister called this evening to ask how the phone activation was going... *that* was a fun conversation.Hopefully tomorrow I can chat with a customer service rep and get help.Overall, my experience with Net10 and the SIM card activation kit has been 1 star.  Because that's as low as the rating system goes.  I guess for the price, you could buy a bunch to use as coasters for your drinks...Update:I never did manage to communicate with an online support representative.  Based on how difficult it is to get access to them, I would write off the forum as a lost cause.  I finally cleared the APN entries in the phone, and re-entered the default one (again).  For some unknown reason, it's working now.  Funny note:  I sent the phone to my sister in a rural area, and she get's slow internet connectivity.  I asked her if she had any letters next to the signal bars (S, H, E, 3, etc) to check for what kind of connectivity she was getting.  She had no letters.  When I said that's probably why she had slow internet, and that she wasn't getting a 3G connection, she said "Oh, we don't have 3G here!"  Slow connectivity issue solved.  Maybe someday her hometown will get 3G.I'm not going to change from the 1 star rating, based on the lack of support both internet and phone.

***Update 23 days beyond "warranty"***One year and 23 days after purchase, the flip out stand ceased to remain closed. The magnet in the stand became detached. I stuck it back in but it won't stay in.  Not that Seidio would have done anything because they don't stand behind their products, even from normal use. It looks real nice being taped shut with a piece of masking tape. Why didn't I get the otterbox again?Well at least the belt clip hasn't broken yet. YET.The rubbery material is no longer slick. It's quite the opposite now. (That's a good thing)  The felt has peeled off the holster in large chunks (not good but expected)******Update 4 days after the update above***Because of the comment to my original review (by a Seidio rep), I decided to call Seidio and explain what happened. They sympathized with my problem, and even though it occurred from "normal use" of the product, and was just outside the warranty period, they told me to go ahead and fill out a warranty claim anyway so I did.Yesterday I got an email with an RMA number saying my warranty claim has been approved. It went on to say to ship in my broken case, and it will be processed within 1-2 weeks. I would have to send it in at my expense and they recommended using a carrier with real tracking and insurance because they're not responsible if it gets lost in the mail.Well Seidio, I really appreciate the gesture... that gesture being a middle finger... but I'm not going to spend any more money to send this back to you. The case only cost $31 to begin with and if you wanted to try to compete with your biggest competitor, otterbox, you could do what they do and just send out the replacement part and not make people send back in their broken case.This morning I managed to fix the kickstand myself, but only because I have access to industrial adhesives, special tools and magnetic material and a way to cut a precise new magnet, not to mention a boat load of patience and the skills to do so. So now the kick stand works once again. No thanks to Seidio and this will be my last Seidio product.At the end of the day, it's how you treat your customers and for the record, that remains my primary complaint about this product... Seidio does not stand behind their products.***Original review below.++++++++++++++++For the past two and a half years I have used a seidio case. 2 weeks ago, before I got my new SG4, I remembered why it is I used cases in the first place when the phone fell and the case absorbed the impact (and subsequently broke).  That was a nasty spill on a tile floor and that wouldn't have fared well for the phone without a case. Not to mention my old seidio case has lots of battle scars  on it from brushing up against stuff, knocking into doors and machines in an industrial setting.Seidio has made it very clear on their website that they do not stand behind their products, even if the product broke in the course of "normal use."  And I have found that to be true when over the past 2 and a half years I have tried to get them to send me a new holster because their rubbish holster clips break just from getting in and out of a car. Their response was to buy another one because they aren't responsible for any broken cases even if it broke from normal, everyday use.So why did I buy another seidio case?  After all, this case itself is nothing to write home about. It fits snugly but the rubbery material is very slick. I guess they did that because people complained it was hard to put in and take out of their pockets. But I specifically wanted rubber because this phone was wanting to slip out of my hands. The phone is slipperier than a water snake in a grease pit.Taking it in and out of the holster, is prying off the outer skeleton at the bottom and damaging the felt.And they're the same holster clips that are going to break.Why would I buy a Seidio when I could buy an Otterbox who will send you a free replacement on your word if you email them a picture of the problem if the thing breaks? (really, they even describe how to do it on their website!)There are two reasons. For starters I wanted a similar vertically oriented holster. The case is just okay. But holster is really the strong point here. I have looked at competing holsters and they just look flimsier and doesn't secure the phone in the right place if you want to wear it vertically.  The other reason is I was stupid. I got all hung up on this little kickstand but in reality, if you just put the phone in the holster, screen facing out, the holster actually displays the phone at a better viewing angle than the kickstand does. And the kickstand bulges out of the case.There are no coverings over any of the ports so they will get filled with lint.So there you have it.A good holster but a mediocre to poor case from a company who has a warranty policy that excludes normal use. (like the phone is going to break by sitting in the packaging or sitting in a desk drawer)Your clip is going to break so prepare yourself for that eventuality.The way I have remedied that once and for all was to replace the broken clip with a clip from a Motorola Jedi series two way radio.  Those clips are much thicker plastic and as long as you still have the spring and the pin, it fits perfectly in this holster. I guess I also figured that based on my research the clips on the otterbox cases break too and at least here I'll be able to replace the clip when it does break.  Of course otterbox would just send me a free replacement anyway so I wouldn't have to do my own repair.I may send this one back if the texture of the rubber doesn't get more grippy and I'm still concerned about taking the phone out of the case and the damage that is doing to the felt and the skeleton.I wish I could tell you to go with this case or another case but this case is probably about as good as they come for the price. The holster on the other hand, you're not going to find another holster like this at the present time. If you don't care about a vertically worn holster go with otterbox.

I have owned the original Droid, and the Droid X, making me a big fan of Motorola's products.All of that goodwill has been lost with the Bionic. The first area of concern is battery life. Even with the extended battery and pulling the phone off of the charger around 8:30 am, if I didn't plug it in during the day, it would be dead by 7pm.Then I noticed that my phone was rebooting itself 3-5 times a day (this is a generous underestimate, not an exaggeration). Within a week, the screen froze in the "off" state. The phone itself had no locked up because I could still hear notifications, alerts, and phone calls come in, but the screen refused to turn on. The usual tricks of pulling the battery and rebooting the phone did not resolve this issue. The only way to get around this to do a Factory reset. As a power user, this mean 1-2 days (upwards of 8 hours) of reinstalling all of my programs, then getting them all running how I needed them.Given all of the issues I was having, I had the local Verizon store swap out my device for another new one, figuring it had to be an aberration.I am now on my third Bionic in less than 2 months, have had the screen freeze (requiring a Factory Reset) 4 times now. The constant reboots continue, and the frequent data connection losses (mind you I NEVER had an issue with my original Droid or the Droid X) are maddening.Couple this with a MAJOR degradation in the quality of the accessories when compared to previous models (the Droid X is probably the pinnacle of Motorola's build quality it seems) and Motorola has lost a loyal customer.If you are considering this generation of smart phones, I would strongly suggest you take a serious look at the HTC Rezound or wait for the Samsung Galaxy Nexus to compare these devices instead of the Bionic. I haven't had this level of frustration with a device since the Windows Mobile phones going back 4-5 years.
