If you are already familiar with Windows Mobile and or love to tinker with the workings of applications and the Windows environment, you'll really like this phone.We ordered both an iPhone and the Fuze at the same time.  I use the Fuze and my wife uses the iPhone.  Both have allot of terrific features and work very well, but they each have their niche of optimal user.  It is very much like comparing a Mac to a PC.  Whereas the iPhone is a relatively seamless package that just works, the Fuze provides customization options and access to countless outside applications that can keep one tinkering endlessly.  Sure, you could just stick to the AT&T; provided interface and applications and get a very decently working smartphone, but that's like asking Michael Phelps to break world records while wearing a business suit.I'm not going to go down the list of pros and cons outside of that because there are plenty of other reviews here and elsewhere to do that for you, but to sum it up:  If you just want something that works well and never want to tinker with how it works, there are better options, like the iPhone.  But if you want something that can do virtually everything the iPhone can do (the most noteable exception being no multi-touch on the Fuze) with the option to expand and modify it, the Fuze is a great platform to do that.Other points worth noting if you are the type to keep your mobile devices for several years are that you can simply replace a dying battery yourself with the Fuze, vs. sending the iPhone back to Apple, and the microSD card slot allows slick and easy memory swaps and upgrades.  Plus you are not tied to using iTunes to manage your applications and data when connected to a computer, with the option to use Microsoft ActiveSync or simple drag and drop style file transfer.  But don't expect it to be as easy either.  ;)The bottom line is that while my wife loves her iPhone and would never think to edit a registry setting, I love the versatility to tinker with the Fuze in ways that you simply cannot with the iPhone.  Similarly, I would never give my non-tech savvy mother a Fuze, whereas the iPhone would keep the "Can you fix my computer?" type of calls to a minimum.I only took away one star for two reasons.  The first of which was all of the useless demos and applicatons cluttering up the menus that AT&T; has included to sell you more stuff, and for the bulkiness of the package.  Sure it's allot to ask to stuff that much technology into something so small, but the similarly powerful iPhone manages it in a package half the thickness, although without the physical keyboard which I have found to be indispensable.

The OtterBox Defender is the best case for the Galaxy S4 in my opinion. It is bulky, but makes it easier for me to hold the phone. I never worry about dropping my phone anymore because it's easier to hold, but if I do drop it (or my daughter) I know it will be protected.It is very hard to get the case off if you need to clean it. When i first bought this, I gave it a 4 star review. I was being foolishly optimistic at the time.Straight away, I had two concerns: one cosmetic, and one functional. Cosmetically, I like where the USB out ports are, but the micro USB in port sucks. It's located on the side, so trying to run this with a solar panel means dealing with a mess of tangled wires. It also puts the charge cable in a position where it is likely to get banged around & damaged. My initial intent was to pair this with an Instapark 10 watt solar system, but quickly realized this wasn't much of an option due to the second concern I had: charging times. This battery takes forever to charge. The first charge I put on this was via a 1 amp wall charger, and it took nearly 18 hours to charge. To get the same charge from the Instapark panels would take 2 really good sunny days minimum. Couple that with the overnight break in charging, and suddenly you are injuring this battery's expected lifespan (manufacturer suggests 500 recharges).Those were my initial issues. I've now had this battery about 8 weeks, and I fully regret wasting money on it. That initial 18 hour charge was by far the fastest one yet, despite using various other chargers ranging up to 5volts with 2000mAh output. As it sits right now, the battery was plugged in shortly before noon yesterday, and it is now after 4PM. In the past 28+ hours, this battery shows it has charged between half & 3/4 of the way. I tested the cable to make sure it wasn't defective, but it is working great. It is still putting enough juice out to charge my Kindle Fire, Android phone, and the few other items I've tested it on. So, I check the battery to see if it is working. It's not.Update: Shortly after posting this review, i was contacted by Anker. Their customer care dept has been very polite, professional, and have taken the initiative to help resolve the issues I've had with their product. They recently shipped a replacement product, which I received last night. Between now and the holidays, I should have more than ample time to run this thing through its paces again, and I'll update this review again, either good or bad, in a few weeks.Thank you Anker support team for all your help. Sonnie has been very professional and polite throughout our communications. Cheers!

For the most part, I am comparing this to official Motorola car dock for my Droid 1.  This car dock for the MAXX is a GREAT improvement and shows Motorola can actually incorporate some good ideas.First, I made sure to research docks before getting one.  Which can be hard since this one didn't have a ton of user reviews right away.  Quickly I did learn that what Verizon was pushing in their stores was not the same as could be ordered from Motorola and elsewhere.  My biggest draw was the integrated audio.  This is a great idea and, in my opinion, worth the premium over the Verizon (which doesn't have the integrated audio cable).  My old Droid dock was just a holder for the phone.  I had to supply the charging cord and the aux cable (which went to the 3.5mm jack in the phone).  It got cluttered, but hey, that's the way is was 2 years ago.This dock for the MAXX has the connector that slides in to lock the phone in place, charge it, and place audio.  Only one cable that goes into the back of the dock.  Very clean.  2nd USB port is also a plus.  The "horn" that comes with this dock has the same size ball as my old Droid dock, as well as my old Garmin, so use which ever you like.  I like my old Garmin because it is more compact and uses a smaller disk.  I haven't had any issue with this dock losing suction, so I'm happy there.  Also, just pressing the phone into the dock without inserting the USB/audio connector is easy and seems to keep the phone in there pretty well.  It isn't to hard to pull it out, just watch for where the volume buttons are so you don't change them accidentally.I have one gripe.  I use a bluetooth device to [sometimes] talk when I'm driving.  This device is also capable of playing music from my phone.  With my old setup (Droid,dock), I would have BT broadcast enabled on the device, the music to BT option enabled, and a cable in the headphone jack going to my car stereo.  I'm listening to something from my phone and it plays on the car stereo.  When I get a call, it pauses the music on the phone, answers the call via BT device, I talk, hang up, music resumes over car stereo.  Perfect. With the MAXX and its dock, I no longer need to plug anything into the 3.5mm jack while I'm in the car.  The problem is when I have BT function enabled, music to BT option enable, and the integrated USB/audio connect inserted everything is pushed to the BT device.  If I turn off the BT device or disable the BT function on the phone, then music plays through the car stereo.  If I forego the dock and just use an aux cable going to the 3.5mm jack on the phone, then everything works great (as it did w/ the Droid).  This seems like a major oversight and I would think that it is just a software issue (but hey, I didn't design this thing).  Now, this is probably more a flaw of the phone than of the dock, but since Motorola makes both the phone and the dock and both of the items work exclusively with each other, I think people need to know about it. There is no reason the integrated USB/audio connector should affect the transfer of functions to the BT any differently than the 3.5mm jack.I'll gladly edit if I find out anything new on the BT issue or if an update fixes it.  Overal, great product at an acceptable price, with one oversight.==================================================================================================================Edit 6/8/12I've noticed that over the past month the audio crackles when going over bumps and rough roads.  It is the micro USB connection of the cable to the dock that is failing.  When I hold the connection rigid or switch to using the 3.5mm jack then there are no issues.  After looking at the micro USB side of the cable under magnification I can see that several of the metal prongs are flatter than others.Now, I first have to admit that I do not drive on the smoothest roads, and even for Wisconsin I think these are some of the worst roads around.  I would still expect better performance than lasting 3 months.  I have taken the review down from 4 stars to 2 because of this.  And to think that I was disappointed with getting only 1 year out of the bad retaining tab on the original Droid....  I expected better from Motorola.  I don't think that expecting this to last more than 3 months was too high.
Only lasted a few weeks before it burnt out.  Not worth the time or money.  I'd pass and spend a little more on something that lasts a while longer.

I've owned several Nokias- four generations in fact and the E72 is the first one I've been dissatisfied with- enough so that I'm considering just returning it and getting another E63 to replace the one I gave to my daughter. This phone seems nicer- the flashlight is still there, it's easier to turn bluetooth on and off, ovi maps is included, it's shiney, hefty, faster, has a front facing camera but....Hardware:There is an extra row of buttons from previous bar phones and that both impinges upon the QWERTY and makes it more difficult to use the extra row of buttons. The buttons add no new functionality- they've just made it more confusing and difficult to use function you want. In particular, separating the accept/reject and call/hangup/power buttons from two into five buttons is well, silly and frustrating. If you haven't used a Nokia before you may just find it annoying, if you've used a Nokia before you'll find yourself hitting the wrong thing and having to poke around with your thumb only to end up hitting the wrong thing over and over. Volume buttons on the side of the phone are nice though. Optical pad is a wash- really what's the point the mechanical one worked great, this one not so much esp if you have liquid on your fingertips. It doesn't work as well as the old mechanical button (often doesn't catch your finger) and it's optional vibrations is just annoying bling. The earphone plug now jaunts out a cocky new angle- great except you know what happens to cocky individuals- they get broken off. I'd don't get it.Software:Here's the REAL downside- Nokia have taken the reliable but not flashy Symbian in the E63i/E63 etc and turned it into a confusing, non-functional morass. The only upside I found was the "hold the home and active applications pop up" feature. Otherwise- bluetooth seems nonfunctional with all handsfree radio features I've tried with with- didn't work with my TomTom, my car radio or my wifes. Sometimes it connects, sometimes not, when it does connect sound is crackly and callers claim my voice is garbled. My daughter's E63 and my wife's G1 function perfectly with all these units so it's the E72 not the units. The messaging seems to longer support Conversations (the threaded SMS software) so it's back to 2003 and non-sortable, no threading SMS which is a real pain for active texters- you'll spend a lot of time deleting messages, scrolling up and down to find things and so on. Both fring and Skype periodically just stop working- you have to remove and reinstall to get call out functionality back. On the E63 both just worked flawlessly. The ordering of the installed applications and menus for configuration have been reorganized and not for the better- it's very difficult to configure a SIP account and by the way- out of the box the phone doesn't support SIP. Very lame.... You literally will spend hours and hours on forums begging the very patient and understanding Nokia employees who volunteer their time there to help you get even the most basic stuff working. Specifically....Nothing is where you think it isNokia has rearranged the menu structure to bring all things you don't use and don't care about up to the top and bury all the useful functionality deeply into a non-obvious and seemingly random location. Once you get there, what you're trying to do (see SIP and synching for instance) won't work unless you get something else from somewhere or set it up in a non-intuitive way not covered in the manual but only in the tenth description someone else on the net has spent hours of their life trying to figure out.Syncing with Google Calendar doesn't workFor instance, if you've used the very functional and easy MFe before to sync with Google calendar and contacts before- forget it. The E72 turns this single step process into 23 steps to sort of sometimes get it working with no feedback. MFE is now somehow integrated into the phone only in such a way that it doesn't work and won't provide any feedback. You literally have to set it up using fake info, cause it to fail and then reset it up to make it work. How Nokia think a phone that requires this sort of nonsense to work is viable is beyond me.SIP doesn't workAgain, you have to search pages of forums to find out the top of the line phone doesn't support SIP as delivered. It pretends it does, there's a SIP setting but it does absolutely nothing. You can't connect to SIP from from the WAN scan like you used to either. What? Instead you have to register in yet another forum (which you can't do from the phone) to download an unofficial piece of software to enable an "advanced setting" only to discover that's yet another can of worms to get going. I still don't have SIP working...Multitasking is just confusingInstead of having a straighforward- what you see is what you are doing, Nokia has turned Symbian into some sort of web page where things are filling up your memory but you don't know they are running and getting to them takes page after page of "back" or "exit" or something. You will wear your fingers out with this crap. Inconveniently, the menu options never use will be the top default and what you do all the time (exit) requires three or four finger presses.Despite the fast processor, actual use is MUCH slowerEvery press is accompanied by a flashing "fade" which just wastes your time without providing useful feedback- it just makes everything slower. By slower, I mean a LOT slower. Your WAN settings aren't remembered, you have to reconnect EVERY time,OVI is just more of the sameI don't get OVI- it's a dumb name, if it's meant to include all the Symbian software it doesn't seem to come close.Not one piece of the software needed to get the basic stuff (internet radio, SIP, sync) was there and seemingly everytime I foolishly try to see if something is there it needs to update itself before it works. They should either ditch this stupid "service" ASAP and put things back on web pages which DON'T require a different registration (Nokia forums, my nokia, nokia beta labs, Ovi) or put everything in Ovi. Seriously. For instance, try and download Nokia maps from Ovi for the E72- it doesn't work. Isn't this one of the only reasons someone would buy this phone?Threaded conversations are no moreIt used to be that Nokia Converations allowed you to thread your conversations by topic, sender, etc. Conversations still can be downloaded but it no longer works- your one and only option now is just a straight list. Great maybe for a teenager who just texts for fun but try and find a conversation when you're chatting with someone- it's no fun to have to look through 100 SMS to find the one you want when with conversations you could have found it in three thumb clicks. I've ended up abandoning SMS on the phone and just use google voice SMS because it's an order of magnitude better.Nokia has REALLY screwed the pooch on this one. The E72 is a terrible downgrade in functionality from any Nokia phone I've owned before. I was always excited and pleased by the new functionality of Nokia phones- no more. Nokia's were always functional, reliable, straightforward, MADE IN EUROPE NOT CHINA, and had all the features other phone/operating systems crowed about. No more..... If this is the direction Nokia is ended I'll leave them behind.

Knowing the wide range of reviews of this product, I ordered it anyway and would return it if it really didn't work out.Here's the summary: I spent much of two days trying every possible combination of signal antenna location: inside, outside, 10 feet high, 20 feet high, near a window, in the attic.  I followed the instructions and kept the base unit as far away as possible, with as much vertical separation as possible.  I oriented the base station antenna vertically and horizontally.Nothing worked.  I called Wi-Ex technical support and their recommendation was to buy the additional directional antenna and aim it at one specific tower.  I decided I didn't want to spend more and risk still not having the repeater work, so I returned it instead.Now the detail.  I did quite a bit of background work.  I live in the middle of a nice flat part of Silicon Valley and there is no lack of towers around.  My house is a single-story wood frame; no big buildings around and no hills.  There are 3 AT&T; cell towers within 0.85 miles (I found them and measured distance).  The problem is that I happen to be located almost exactly in between and equidistant from these three towers, and apparently their power is adjusted so that they don't overlap a great deal.  In other words, I get three sets of signals, none particularly strong, and depending on specific conditions at any moment in time the strongest signal comes from any of these three towers at random.  So the phone is constantly hunting for a signal, and rarely gets one very strong for very long.The YX510 repeater uses an omnidirectional signal antenna, in other words it will see all three towers the same way my phone does -- varying in relative signal strength constantly.  It will simply repeat that mixed up signal.  Furthermore, the YX510 will ratchet its boost down if it detects a strong signal, which happens every once in a while.  So it is easy to see what is happening.  The repeater cannot help this situation and in fact makes it worse by occasionally ratcheting  boost down when it happens to get a short burst of a strong signal.  The end result was that my cell reception was no better with the repeater than without -- not better, just "different" -- I was stuck more often in Edge than without, but I did often have 2-3 bars as long as I was in line of sight of the base station and no more than 15 feet away.  Beyond that, I lost signal.The solution would appear to be to use a directional antenna and aim it at one tower.  This would potentially solve these issues: the phone would not hunt between towers and the YX510 would not get a wildly varying signal strength (I think).  However, I'm not ready to make that jump yet and will need to do some more research.I was also not very impressed with the construction or the instructions for the YX510.  I will want to look at other options (all of which, BTW, are substantially more expensive, but generally the companies offering them have longer track records).The YX510 with omnidirectional signal antenna is probably a good product when there is a single, dominant cell tower nearby.  In my case, with multiple equal-strength towers (but at marginal strength), it did not help at all.  Since Wi-Ex tech support knew the problem immediately when I described my situation, it appears they are aware of it -- which IMHO means they should make it clearer what the limitations are.  The marketing material and the installation guide are written to promote the idea that you just throw the stuff up and it should work.  RF just isn't like that.Thanks to Amazon, returning it was easy.

Let the games marvelous headset games.  If your anything like me, you've had just about every make headset.  I've had them all, we all have.  Some better than others but I've never found one that I was in love with.  Since then I've tried dozens upon dozens of different Bluetooth headsets; they have ranged from great to mediocre to downright bad. I have found that the "great" Bluetooth headsets I've used have consistently come from one of two companies - Jabra or Plantronics.  So here comes the Jabra motion.  The Jabra Motion looked pretty in its clear plastic shell; so pretty that I felt a little bit bad about freeing it from its box. Once completely unboxed however, I was pleased to see that the device not only looked good but seemed to be thoughtfully designed as well.  It returns to the "curve around the ear design" which works for many people.  The design of the Jabra Motion headset is important, since the best Bluetooth headsets are ones you can comfortably wear throughout the day. You know, the Bluetooth headsets you don't feel the need to take on and off, and that don't cause "ear fatigue" because of the pressure they put on some portion of your ear or head. This Headset is amazingly comfortable and stands out in that respect. This surprised me since it is rather large, and it literally wraps around your ear. This headset is bigger than many of the headsets I've owned in recent years -- along with the excellent design -- allows for the proper distribution of weight. The result is a headset that sits comfortably on your ear without placing too much pressure on any one spot.  Add in the fact that the earpiece not only swivels and allows you to use the headset on either the left or the right ear, but it also has a movable post so that you can adjust the height of the portion that sits in your ear, and you now have a headset that offers as close to a custom fit as you're going to find without it being a custom fit.  I have been wearing this headset as much as possible since it arrived last week, so I could test its comfort over extended periods of use; I have to say that it is one of the most comfortable headsets I have ever used.SETTING UP THE JABRA MOTIONJabra recommends charging the device fully before using it for the first time.  After an hour and a half the battery icon glowed green and the Motion was ready for pairing. A fully charged battery should yield six hours of uninterrupted use and a depleted battery will only take two hours to fully recharge.Pairing the headset to my phone was effortless. Upon folding down the descriptively named "boom-arm," the headset came to life. It was just a moment before the reason behind the user's manual thinness was revealed: all of the instructions are presented in audio form through the earbud when the Motion is turned on for the first time. I disregarded the instructions on how to pair the headset via Bluetooth because I wanted to utilize Jabra's newly implemented NFC (near-field communication) technology support. All I had to do was physically touch the Motion to the back of my NFC enabled smart phone (the Samsung Galaxy S3) and a moment later I received confirmation that the two had paired. It was perhaps the most painless pairing process I've ever encountered. Pairing the Motion to my computer required the usual authentication process, yet was made easier by the audio instructions.So how is the audio quality on the Jabra Motion Headset? In a word it's fantastic. Yes, I said it FFFAAANNNTTTAAASSSTIC.  I'm not using the word lightly. I just spent some time on the phone while driving to and walking around New York City and I've asked people who I'm talking to how the headset sounds.  They have used words like "amazing" and "fantastic". Yes, while driving with the windows closed the connection was perfect and it didn't sound like I was using a headset at all. That was true even when I was speaking in a whisper. Things changed a bit when I opened the window and let the wind rush by my head while driving at about 65 miles an hour. But the worst it got was, "Now it sounds like you are on any headset." That means that with the window opens at 60 miles an hour the Jabra Motion Bluetooth Headset was as good or better than most Bluetooth headsets are in a quiet room. That's rather impressive.All this held true on my end of the conversation as well, and while I know I'm sounding a bit too enthusiastic I honestly can't help it - this headset sounds better than any headset I have ever used. I also used the headset to access Siri and to do voice recognition on my iPhone.  The convenience of activating Siri by simply touching the headset was great, and the accuracy of the voice-to-text transcription was as good as I have seen. That tells me that the audio signal the phone is receiving from the headset is clean and crisp, and it vastly improves the ability to text by voice on the fly.This is the best headset I've ever used.  I'm planning on purchasing 1 for my wife and one for the house phone.  This is a must have for anyone using a headset.

I know that paying less than $10 for a battery replacement for a smartphone like the iPhone 4S is risky; but since I've had great luck with Amazon in the past, and Amazon sellers are usually very good about refunds/returns, I took the plunge with this battery.To replace the battery in your 4S, you need a pentalobe screwdriver, which can be purchased separately. I'd also recommend getting a 'liberation kit' from iFixIt, so you can change the screws to standard phillips-head screws. Here's how to replace the battery:1. Remove the two screws on either side of the dock connector (bottom of iPhone).2. Push/slide back cover up towards top of phone (it will go up about 5mm).3. Pull back cover off the phone (this exposes the battery).4. Using a spudger tool, pry up the battery from the side of the iPhone, and carefully pry off the connector, being careful not to lose the little post that goes around one of the screws! (This is the most difficult part).Replacing the battery is annoying, but if the battery lasts twice as long as an old, worn-out battery, it's well worth it.Unfortunately, in my case, the replacement battery charged only if the phone was turned off completely, and wouldn't report back to the iPhone the correct charge level as I was using it throughout the day (it would stay at, say, 80% until I turned the phone off and on after a few hours--then it would say 50% until another on/off cycle). If I tried charging the phone while it was on, it would reboot itself every 3 to 5 minutes for no reason whatsoever. Very strange, and very annoying. I even reseated the power connector and that didn't fix it.In the end, I put the original battery back in, and even though it only lasts 3/4 of a day before needing a top off, it charges fine and doesn't exhibit any weird behavior.I contacted the seller, and they quickly allowed me to return the battery, so no sweat off my back. I'm guessing there are some duds, and some good ones--the cheaper batteries you buy, the greater chance you get a lemon. YMMV; good luck!

As far as Bluetooths go (Blueteeth?), sound quality has to be the most important factor, and I've now tested this one in a car with the radio on, in a crowded K-Mart with announcements blasting every minute or so, and at home. In each case, I asked the person on the other end how I sounded and if they could tell I was on a Bluetooth. No one could tell, and each person said I sounded perfectly clear. I could also hear just fine, and it was easy to adjust the volume when I needed to make it a little louder.Pairing it with my phone was also very simple, and the earpiece and hook felt fine. The earpiece sticks out at a slightly weird angle, I think, but it felt right.I'm deducting a star mainly because of the lack of a charging indicator. When you're charging the device, it blinks a few times, and then simply turns off the light. So for the duration of the time it's plugged into the charger, you don't know if the darn thing is still charging or if it's done. I had to keep turning it back on to find out if it still showed a low battery. How silly. And the cord for recharging is extremely short. What a strange way to save money... "I know... let's cut six inches off the cord!"It also answered a call BY ITSELF when I was in a different room. I heard my cell ring once, and then nothing. When I got to the room, I heard a woman saying, "Hello?" into my earpiece. So either I have a haunted Bluetooth or there's a bit of an error there.But anyway. I'm glad I have it and I expect it to be my main Bluetooth device now.

Let me start by saying that I wanted to love this product.  The plastic housing is top notch and features a very soft-to-the-touch rubber overmold which makes this feel like a very high quality product.  When plugged in, a cool blue LED on the side illuminates to let you know that the unit is receiving power and is ready to charge.When it worked, this was one of the few car chargers that I've found which produced enough current to be able to charge my Galaxy S III while using GPS and streaming music to my car receiver.  I was ecstatic!  Most car charger units don't deliver the amount of current advertised, and even though my phone was plugged in, I would still lose battery life - having the phone charging just slowed the inevitable dead battery.  This unit seemed to charge at a rate of approx 20% per hour with multiple apps running.Sadly, this unit only lasted me three days.  I was on the commute home, listening to a podcast with the GPS telling me the optimal route to avoid traffic when I noticed the "low battery notification" pop up on my phone.  It was then that I realized that the blue light on the side of the product was no longer illuminated and my phone was no longer receiving a charge.  Thinking that maybe I had blown a fuse in my car, I tried the unit with the cigarette lighter in my girlfriend's car but had no success there either.  I'm guessing that the unit itself blew a fuse, however the product is not designed to be serviceable and any failed circuitry is not user replaceable.I ended up not returning the unit, since the cost of shipping to send the item back would have been almost as much as buying a new one, so I've ended up going with a different brand which has been serving me very well for two months now.This is the product I ended up with, and it has the added benefit of an additional port for another device as well.http://www.amazon.com/o/ASIN/B0088U4YAG?tag=adapas02-20

This is a compact, solidly built lithium battery pack charger. It is on the higher end of power chargers in both usage and durability. However, the positive and negative aspects are clear.POSITIVE:It is the size of a pack of cigarettes and is sleek in design with four blue LED lights that show the level of power.The LED flashlight is remarkably powerful and turns on/off with a double click of the button.The double USB ports allow you to charge two items at once (2-Amp USB output and a 0.6 Amp Output). One is specifically for iPhone/iPod/iPad charges. Other devices can be charged but at a slower rate. The other port is for small-battery devices.There is an embedded Output Cable that slips into a groove on the outside of the charger and is for Micro-USB charging (2 amps.There are five connectors including a 4mm PlayStation Portable, a Nokia 2mm, a Mini-USB and two Micro-USBs.There is a Micro-USB in-port on the side of the PowerGen.Although the charger comes almost fully charged, it takes about 6-7 hours (overnight) to fully charge. This will fully recharge your iPhone approximately 3.5-4.5 times! With six different Micro-USB attachments, you can charge anything, except an ASUS Transformer or Laptop computer.You can leave the PowerGen plugged into a USB port for extended periods of time with no worries.The charging function lights turn off in 20 seconds to save power.The USB ports charges with a 1-Amp USB output. This will charge an iPhone/iPod/iPad, most tablets, smartphones, cell phones, MP3 music and MP4 players, game players and GPS units, including Apple, Blackberry, HTC, Kindle, LG, Motorola, Nexus, Nokia, Nook, Samsung and Sony phones. It will not charge Asus transformers or laptop computers or a Samsung Galaxy Tablet unless you have a TAB-USB adapter.This will NOT CHARGE an iPad or mini!NEGATIVE:The Output Cable for Micro-USB charges has the potential of getting snagged or pulled off.Although the charger comes with two USB cables (7 inches and 27 inches), you have to attach the correct Micro-USB connector. There are simply too many loose parts. It would be a good idea to get a small clear zip-lock baggie to store them in.An iPhone will get a 100% charge in about 45 minutes or less. The iPad mini charges about 95% of a full charge. That's good news for iPhones and not so great for iPad mini's. An iPad will get less, but I don't own one of these.At a full charge the PowerGen takes 9-10 hours charge from a 1 amp USB supply port and 18-20 hours with a 0.5 amp supply (Computer USB port).CONCLUSION: The positives outweigh the negative and I have to admit that after using many other models of chargers, this is one that you can feel you've invested you money in wisely.This product was generously supplied by the manufacturer for an honest review.

A screen protector with over 700+ ratings and reviews you would think is just going to be amazing. Possibly my expectations were set a little high because so? I don't think so.For $10 before shipping, lifetime warranty, videos and pictures of pen puncturing, power tools taken to it, purpose fingernail poking, etc. My expectations I feel were spot on. To expect something of great protection, long lasting, and all of these resources available to you to back this up, what more could you ask for.Well let me tell you... I purchased (2) of these things. One for my phone, and one for my wife's phone. I did everything to the "T" as far as installation instructions. I even took it another step and watched a couple videos of others doing it as well.Step 1: Remove your battery.Step 2: Ensure you are in a dust controlled area.Step 3: Clean the screen with the included cloth thoroughly.Step 4: Spray your finger tips with the included solution.Step 5: Spray the first side of the screen protector with the included solution.Step 6: Peel the screen protector from its backing and spray the opposite side.Step 7: Apply the screen protector to your phone, positioning as needed.Step 8: Use the provided rubber squeegee in working motion starting from the middle of the phone working outward towards the edges.Step 9: Remove and access liquid with the included dust free cloth.Step 10: Let sit for 24hours prior to apply any case or using the phone to ensure the internal electronic parts are dried and the protector has settled.Let me mention none of this was a big deal to me, nor was it real hard to do. Very simple to be quite honest. HOWEVER after installation, on both my wife's and my phone there are what appears to be molded ripples on the actual protector itself. It in no way provides a crisp clear look to your screen as a cheap standard screen protector would that you may have to replace every 6 months. Honestly to me this looks like FLAWS in the actual molding of the protectors. They stand out very much when your screen if off, and not so much when your viewing web pages or emails.However if you are like me and purchased a Samsung Galaxy S3 because of all the modifications and customizations you can do with your phone, this becomes a bit of a hindrance to me as I've got a complete custom layout of my main screen, text messaging screens, banners, clock modifications, etc. Being a computer graphic designer I am into darker backgrounds with bright vibrant colors, lines, and designs flashing on my phone that really sets this sucker off! So these flaws tend to stick out a little more for me, and I find this extremely annoying.Also keep in mind, their "Lifetime-Warranty" that is included DOES NOT include the RETURN SHIPPING that dings YOUR pocket every time you want to replace the current. Which on an average is roughly another $5. So you do the math. $10 for a screen protector that offer insane scratch/ding protection that you could potentially stab with a pen, or take a power tool to it (because we do that all the time right? lol) - (nothing like chilling in class and out of board-em getting the urge to poke the heck out of your phone with your pen) and forfeit visibility -OR- buy the .99 cent 3-packs of crystal clear screen protectors available to you and change them heck 3 times a year if you wanted to for precise visuals.Keep in mind, MOST cases out there today already offer a lift to the face of all phones. So laying your phone flat on it's face is rarely an issue. I just don't feel this is worth the buy at $10+$5(return shipping) every time you need a replacement.

Well, it seemed clever, and I gave it the benefit of the doubt that it filled a need.  The problem is that in filling the need for a quick physical shutter button for the iPhone 4/4S, it makes the iPhone a completely clunky device.For starters, the device must be used in conjunction with a very basic camera app provided through the App Store by Belkin.  It basically replicates the built-in iPhone camera app's functions, but disables the on-screen button.  Belkin could have gone a step further and added some special filters, something, anything to make this stand out a bit.  But that's beside the point - it works just fine as it is, but you're stuck with that app and ONLY that app.  No using Camera+ or Instagram or, well, *anything*.Plug the grip into the iPhone via the dock charger and you find that it cradles that end of the iPhone perfectly, with self-sizing grips holding either side of the iPhone that will stretch out to accommodate a bumper or other similar-sized case.  Open the app and press the camera button, located closest to the speaker (that's the "right" grill opening on the bottom when holding the iPhone in portrait mode, or the "upper" grill in landscape.) It does indeed make taking a photo very quick and easy, with a confident little "snip" feel to the button.There are two routes you can go with the LiveAction Camera Grip - you either leave it on so you can take full advantage of it or mount it as needed.  Neither of these works all that well, however.  The grip is big and, as we know, the iPhone is long and slim.  Leaving it on makes it awkward to keep in a pocket, but the alternative is putting it on for every picture, then removing it, and keeping the grip somewhere.  It's just awkward, period.  A solution, it turns out, for what is really a very minor problem.And making things worse, Apple included shutter-button capability in iOS 5, so depressing the "up" volume button while the Camera app is open snaps a picture.  So, basically, this device becomes pointless, other than the plus of the built-in tripod mount - but then there are solutions to that out there, too (like thisStand with Mini Tripod Mount Adapter for iPhone 4G (Black).)

The Apple 5-series (5S) with which I tested my review sample has less than spectacular battery performance, so I have more than a casual interest in this case. Let me say up front that there is a trade-off, but one I am glad to make: this takes the sleek 5S and adds a little extra size to it. Width wise the increase is nominal as is the weight. It does make it about a half inch taller, so if you love the phone's original size this will detract slightly from the aesthetics.Looks aren't everything, though, because use time is more important to me and I am not always around a charger. The 2300mAh capacity of the integrated battery is almost 1.5 times that of the 5S' internal 1560mAh battery. Even accounting for loss from charging that is still twice the use time of a 5S without this case, which is why I love it.Another feature I love is the way the case protects the phone. While Apple builds their phones to withstand a lot of abuse, this case adds another layer of protection. It is shock resistant, and the way the outer lip is slightly raised gives the screen more protection if you drop the phone in a carpeted office.Others have described how this case works and fit and form, so I am not going to repeat that information. I will say that the final selling point for me is this case earned Apple's MFi (Made for iPhone) certification, which gives me peace of mind. There are too many products on the market that are of shoddy quality or from fly-by-night outfits, so the certification is not only meaningful, but worth a premium price to me. Considering that this case is competitively priced is yet another value point and one of the reasons why I awarded it five stars.

I bought the S9 a few years ago and they were perfect in terms of comfort and performance and then they died after about a year from the usual sweat problems. They could still be used if they were dried out for a few days but being only able to use them every few days wasn't a solution. I went wired for about 18 months and then found the S10s.They arrived last week and I've used them a few times while working out though not for running. They feel heavier and tighter than the S9s. I have to work the top of my ears carefully under the top loop part of the S10 so that I don't feel pinched. The feeling that I get is that the back part is heavier than on the S9 and the added pressure amplifies the feeling back there. One other downside is that the buttons are harder to press. On the S9, the buttons were bigger and easier to find and press. On the S10, they are small are require more pressure to press.It is possible that it loosens up over time so I will give it a chance.I also get the feeling that the newer unit is much, much better at keeping sweat out. The seals seem to be better and some of the negatives in UI that I wrote about may have been due to compromises to improve durability.The S9s are still for sale and I'm tempted to buy another pair of those and alternate between the two - I'm hoping that the S9 would last longer if it were left to completely dry out after each use. I will write an update on the S10 in a few months. The earlier versions of these were terrible WRT durability but they work reasonably well if you're willing to replace them from time to time. The S10 could be a lot better at this - time will tell.

I wanted to love this.  I backed a Pebble Watch on Kickstarter a long time ago.  I got impatient and ordered this Sony to see what I MIGHT expect from my Pebble.  For a minute I thought wow after using the Sony the Pebble can't compare.Why?  For starters it's touchscreen.  It's color and has an established app eco system with some pretty useful apps.  After 48 hours of using it, I couldn't get past how cheap it felt, how ugly it looked, and far it stuck off of my wrist.  It caught EVERYTHING because it sits so high up off of your wrist.I remember one reviewer here stating something about the watch garnering attention, and once he/she saw people looking and pointing at the watch.  I don't doubt they were pointing, but they probably weren't saying "wow look at that".  Most likely they were saying "what the hell is that ugly contraption protruding from his wrist, what a dork".  Because essentially that's what you look like wearing this thing.What I liked:- Color screen- Touch screen- App eco-system- Responsive- Good battery life- Didn't impact my LG Optimus G (Quad core, 2GB ram, phone).- Bluetooth range was good.What I didn't like:- Ugly as all hell.- Bulky (in that it stuck so far off your wrist you couldn't get it to hide under a dress shirt, and it caught everything).- Feels like it's going to break at any moment.  Cheap plastic, light.- Not waterproof!  WTF?  That's ridiculous in 2013.  Splash resistent?  I wouldn't trust it.  One good storm and it could be ruined.- WEAK vibration, to the point I barely noticed it half the time.- Way too many taps to get to the information you need.- Poor resolution- Uses a clip to attach to the wristband.  Because it sticks so far off you wrist it catches everything I feel like that clip would break eventually.- Nearly unreadable in sunlight.- Cannot tell time without being synced to a phone.- Time doesn't display on face at all times (you can enable that option, but it dramatically reduces battery life).It was at this moment I realized something.  Sony is trying to TOTALLY replicate my phone on my wrist.  That's not really a good idea.  Pebble got it right.  I have a Pebble on my wrist right now.  I need something flat not boxy and not protruding from my wrist.  I need something that gives me at a glance information, I really don't need to swipe, tap, pinch-to-zoom on a watch do I?  I also think for a watch, physical buttons are the way to go.  I think Pebble got it right, don't overload the watch with too much extra nonsense.  Make it useful, not cumbersome.I also think this whole EVERYTHING has to be touchscreen is ridiculous.  I had a Logitech Harmony remote that was FULLY touchscreen.  It sucked.  Somethings deserve buttons.  A remote, a watch, and even car radio/headunits deserve some sort of physical button/knob etc.All in all, the BIGGEST flaw for this watch is it's UGLY, BULKY, CHEAP, CHEESY, and is trying TOO hard.  Just display the text of an email, and text message, GTalk, GVoice, control music, etc.  Don't overload with touch screen navigation.  And PLEASE make it look sexier.  This thing looks like total crap when I'm wearing a Movado on my other wrist.  On a good note you can change the band and it comes with an adapter to do so.If you're thinking about a smartwatch, for now the Pebble is the way to go though even then you might want to wait until they work out the kinks if you're an iPhone user.  Android users should definitely check out the Pebble.  I plan to do a comparison on XDA forums under Smartwatches if you're interested in seeing my comparison of Sony vs the Pebble.  By the way, Pebble isn't perfect and I have a few complaints about that watch too.  But those are minor and pale in comparison.  I also think those issues will be resolved in a firmware update.The Sony watch can do more than a Pebble (at least for now), but I think that's the problem, it's doing too much and not enough of what you want/need/expect a smart watch to do.

I bought this adapter because my computer was running out of USB ports and I only use them to charge my devices (i.e. phone, ipad, ipod, tablets, ect). I have both the three-port and the six-port chargers.  I’ve had the 6-port charger for several months now, and I use it daily.  I’ve found only one short-coming with the 6-port:  Too few 2A slots.  So, I still needed a few more chargers.  What I end up with, then, are enough 2A slots and too many 1A and lower slots.Enter this new-for-2014 model with 3 slots.  Perfect!  In my mind, the best “family” set-up is two or more of the 3-slot models, set around the house in convenient locations.On both the 3- and 6-port chargers, I really love the fact that they have thick, pliable, 6 foot cords. I had been looking for chargers like this: I can plug these chargers into a low wall outlet and still sit it on the corner of my desk--any corner of my desk. You can set it on top of a four-drawer file cabinet and still have some cord distance with which to work. You can set it on a hall table or other convenient-to-you locations (as opposed to a convenient-to-the-charger location).  And when I say pliable, I mean pliable: The rubber is tough, but gives up its memory rather quickly.Does it work? It works for our AT&T; Unite cards, our phones, our old phone, blue tooth speakers, and our tablets. My Kindle Fire HDX 8.9 can take more than this charger can give it, so I give it exactly what it wants and plug it into the surge protector separately.The 3-port charger has a nice neat look to it:  It is definitely compact at 4” (including the unbending part of the cord) x 2 5/8” x 1” (including the on/off toggle switch).  I like the classy look of a panel of shiny black set into matte black sides and bottom.  My USB cables fit in nice and tight—no wiggle.The 6-port charger has a orderly look to it, too: It is a fairly compact 2 3/8" x 7", and at its highest point it is only 1 1/4" tall and is a nice compatible matte black with some curved corners. My USB cables do wiggle a bit in the ports and the orientation is opposite for the top three and bottom three ports. The blue LED light is bright enough so you can work with it in the dark.*I received a sample product of both the 3-port and the 6-port charger from the seller.

I have a couple of other bluetooth headsets.  I have an older Logitec Mobile Freedom which was fairly inexpensive yet works great.  I also have a Plantronics Voyager Pro.  My requirements are the basics: good sound quality, simple use, comfort, and cutting down wind/background noise.At a little over $50, the Jabra Extreme falls right in between the aforementioned Logitec and Plantronics products (about $30 and $75, respectively).While I really like both of the other products, the Jabra would be my preference.  It cancels background noise out somewhat better than the Logitec and at least as well as the more expensive Plantronics Voyager Pro. I found it had great sound quality on both ends, and there was no background noise at all.  I tested this in my car with the window down at highway speeds, at a soccer game, and in a crowded pub.  Now, please keep in mind, YOU will still hear all that noise as you are there in person, but the person on the other end of your call will have a very easy time hearing you.  And you can hear them fine in noisy situation.What's in the box?  Quite a bit actually!  Usually, with this type of product you get the headset, a charger and maybe a spare earpeice cover.  Jabra gives you:* The Jabra Extreme headset* One spare ear rest* Two spare earpiece covers* * AC Charger* Car charger adapter* USB to min-USB connector* User GuideSetup of the Jabra is what you'd expect.  It charges quickly, usable within a half hour or so and fully charged in about 2 hours.  Displays are simple.  Green is fully charged, yellow 70% or more, and red less than 70% charged.  The charge holds nicely.  I used about 4 hours of talk time in a 12 hour period and still left the device on all night and it was fine the next morning, and I used it for another hour the next day.The unit is small, smaller than many others, which is nice.  I love my Plantronics but its a pretty big product.  The Extreme is very comfortable and lightweight.Controls are simple to use.  Slide the on/off button to 'on'.  Green light means it is on, and it will flash blue when connected.  Again, very simple and somewhat typical of a headset.To answer a call, press the Answer/End button.  Simple.I paired this device with my Motorola Razr V3, also a Razr V3xx, as well as a Blackberry Bold 9000 and a Pantech Matrix C740 too.  No issues pairing and functioning.Now, lots of headsets have similar features.  Here are the three things I love about the Extreme. I've seen a few portable Bluetooth speakers that were advertised as being water-resistant, but the SoundDew is the first one that appears to actually encourage its use around water, based on its splashy (pun intended) product graphic. I accepted the challenge and rigged the speaker to hang from the shower head support while I took a shower last night. It didn't get any direct spray, but it got plenty of splatter and was thoroughly soaked after a couple of minutes. It worked fine, and since it was hanging vertically, the water drained freely from the openings in the grille. Note that the built-in hook isn't very big, and it's a closed loop. To hang it from an object, you may need a wire tie as I used, or a carabiner or something similar.The speaker paired easily with my Android cell phone and Kindle Fire HD 7" tablet with the device name "SoundDew." The audio quality playing MP3 music was pretty good for such a small device. The specs don't list the output power, but based on my experience with other small portable speakers, my guess is about four watts. The volume can be controlled from either the phone/tablet or the controls on the SoundDew, but pausing and advance/return functions can only be done from the mobile phone or tablet. I also hard-wired the speaker to my tablet with an audio cable, and it worked fine. (A 40" audio cable with standard 3.5mm plugs is included.) With the Bluetooth connection, the advertised range of 10 meters appeared to be accurate. I was seeing a good connection at 25 feet or so even through a couple of walls without any break-ups.As a hands-free device for phone calls, the SoundDew is easy to use and worked well. I used it to answer a call on my cell phone, and the clarity of the sound was very good. I had the SoundDew sitting flat on a table during the call. I liked the rubberized base that kept it in place and prevented marring the finish of the wood table.The user guide indicates that the SoundDew's internal battery can be recharged through a USB port on a computer (a 10" USB cable with a Micro-USB plug is included). I contacted the seller's tech support and asked if an AC 5V power source could be used instead, and the answer was "Yes," so I recharged the speaker's battery overnight with an AC wall charger with a 5V~1.0A output. Note that the charging port and audio cable port are covered by a water-resistant flap that opens to allow access.If you're looking for a small portable Bluetooth speaker, the SoundDew is definitely worth a look, especially if you need a speaker that will operate in a damp environment like the bathroom or kitchen, or outdoors near a lake or pool, or in rainy weather.A product sample was provided by the seller for review purposes.

This NT Carbon Pak is one of my biggest power banks at 12,000 mAh.  It  has two USB slots that can charge two devices simultaneously.  One at 2.1A for iPads and tablets and 1A to charge smaller gadgets like smartphones.It came almost fully charged, I had to plug it only for a few minutes to top off  showing all blue LEDs lit.  One of the features I like the most in a battery pack is the use of a regular microUSB for charging the battery. It is  annoying to look and scramble for a round tip wall adapter from the many cables and adapters that I have in my drawer.  Currently I have battery packs that use three sizes of round tip wall adapters.  It is like trial and error which adapter will fit my battery pack.  With a microUSB, I know exactly which cable to get.As for packaging, this battery pack is housed in a handsome presentable box, making it perfect as a gift. It includes a microUSB cable for charging either to your own wall wart or a computer USB slot.  The battery is shiny black sleek.  You can check the battery status by pressing the big button once to display the number of LEDs representing the remaining battery level status.The only thing I wish it has is an LED flashlight.  The flashlight feature which is available on some is very useful to me.  Otherwise, this battery pack is one of the biggest in capacity and looks great aesthetically.Sample item provided by the distributor for evaluation review.

This is by far the best of the nearly dozen phone mounts I have tried: vent mounts, windshield mounts, dash mounts, CD slot mounts; sticky, beanbag, suction; arms, no arms.  Some have been pretty good, some just horrendous, but the iOttie One Touch stands far and away above all of them.  (At least after 2 weeks; let's see how it goes after a year.)Here are the reasons it is better:* The suction combined with the sticky surface (but not an adhesive!) is just unreal.  Wow this thing is not going anywhere.* The arm is sturdy and fairly short, so the phone end of the mount barely moves, even in my car with a stiff suspension* It has a small profile, so blocks a minimal amount of view, in fact it can easily be mounted on a windshield so that the whole mount is invisible behind the phone* Two easily reached adjustment knobs are easy to adjust, give a wide range of adjustment, and are sufficiently tightened by hand (most other models' adjustments loosen, don't give enough adjustment range, and/or require tools to tighten enough)* Able to fit even a high-rake windshield (like mine)* The one-handed operation for both insertion and removal of the phone is ingenious and, so far at least, works without any problem* Phone is held tightly on the sides and bottom.  The bottom even slides left-right so you can pick the position that works with cords coming into the bottom of the phoneHere are the reasons it doesn't measure up:[crickets]I haven't had it long enough to have needed to clean the suction cup so I don't know if just rinsing it and drying it will work.  Note that the instructions say to let it air dry, which makes sense as other reviewers appear to have had problems if they dried it with a towel which may have left lint behind.

I wanted so much to love this headset. Previously I was using a sort of first generation headset from another manufacturer and it was kinda cheap so now that I know that I would use it a lot, I wanted to get the best headset I could find. Well lets start with the pros.The sound quality is very good. Noise canceling was important to me and so was not sounding like I got a pair of socks in my mouth when I'm talking. This unit definitely is much better in those areas. In fact I was speaking to someone just yesterday and I was driving, flooring it to pass cars on the highway, at highway speeds, and the person on the other end couldn't hear any road noise at all. They also sounded very good. So good in fact that I kept asking if there were there because it sounded so quiet, I thought the call might have dropped. So no more having to pull over on the side of the road to be able to have a halfway-decent conversation. The standby battery life is better than expected. The talktime battery life is about what I expected. The microphone is about a half inch from the face. I don't have a beard but I know that's a common concern so I think you're safe with this one.OK, here's what I don't like. The description gives the shipping weight but not the actual weight of the headset. I was disappointed that it weighs in at about 1 ounce, the same as my last bulky 1st generation headset. That doesn't seem like a lot, but I definitely notice it.The unit takes some practice putting it on blindly. My last headset, you can instantly hook it on your ear and it's there. With this one, for about a week, I had to check in the mirror in order to put it on. After a month of use, I no longer need the mirror, but it still is tricky.At first I was satisfied with the comfort of the unit but as time went on, I find this unit downright painful to wear. I guess it doesn't really fit my ear. The part that loops around needs to be a little larger diameter. The earbud gets mashed into my ear over the course of the day. I think it's because of pressing the button on it, every time you do that, the ear bud gets crammed in your ear.The power button is hard to locate and press because it's too small and if you are wearing it, the unit becomes unhinged from your ear. Also, the volume keeps resetting down to a lower level so every time I use it, I have to raise the volume (thereby putting more pressure on my ear).So, all in all, I will continue to use it but I would not buy this style of headset again. The kind where the bulk of the unit sits outside the ear as opposed to behind the ear is much more comfortable in my view. This is one purchase where seeing a "floor model" would have helped.This unit does make you look like the ShamWow / Slap Chop pitchman, and I get sort of strange looks when I'm in public but the sound quality is totally worth it.

The Francois et Mimi LD-9210 (I will refer to it as F&M; in this post) was a promising budget bluetooth headphone for big heads that is ruined by reliability issues. Let's start with the deal killers:1) there is a constant buzz/hiss in the right ear (where the buttons are). This is on while music is playing. It is there always, but if you listen to busy music, it fades in the background. But if you listen to audiobooks, podcasts, soft music, classical, etc, it is annoying. It happens while music is playing. If you connect to a device but you don't play music, it's not there. You can test this by pressing the pause button while listening to music. For a fraction of a second after the music stops playing, you will hear the buzz and then it will stop. This happened with multiple devices (iPod Touch 4, Nexus 4, Note 8, etc) that don't buzz/hiss like that with other headphones. The hiss is also audible when listening to music at coffee shops which are a lot noisier than a quiet home.2) the battery died after about half a dozen recharges. It refuses to charge with its own charger or any other charger I tried. It got its best range with the Monoprice Bluetooth Transmitter.Why it's promising:1) great for big heads (13 position slider, I am halfway and have big head; I barely fit Monoprice 8323)2) bluetooth buttons and color codes make most sense among all bluetooth devices I used3) battery lasts longer than most other bluetooth headphones I used4) gives you multiple warnings before it runs out of battery5) good job of reducing outside sounds and does not leak sound to the outside world (eg at coffee shops)6) bluetooth pairing and connection works as well as other devices, worked with iPod Touch 4, Nexus 4, Note 8, laptop with Bluetooth USB dongle, Monoprice Bluetooth Transmitter (to listen from TV/cable box), etc7) fairly comfortable for extended periods of timeOther PointsThere are bluetooth only, no 3.5mm audio input... While playing music, the blue light flashes every 2 seconds on the right earcup (many Bluetooth headphones do this type of a thing)...As fate would have it, I reviewed theTASCAM TH-2000-R Professional Grade Headphones (Red/Black)last month, and surprise surprise, these two headphones are very very similar above the earcups. The very first thing that is impossible to ignore about this and other recent Jabra products is how elaborate the packaging is. They are obviously a company with little social responsibility for the environment. Thirty seconds after the elaborate retail box was opened it was in the trash; however it took a lot longer than 30 seconds to figure out the needlessly complicated packaging. What are they thinking of? Not to mention the obvious that every dollar they spend on wasteful packaging is a dollar they DON'T spend on research and engineering. The simple math is that it's one or the other.This is another Jabra headset that doesn't seem to have been designed by someone who has ears. Its design closely mimics the Plantronics Voyager series which has a clean, smooth look and a fit that permits placing it on your ear and immediately using it, which should be the goal of a good headset design. The Motion, to the contrary, is downright fussy with parts that lift and turn and swivel and fold. It takes patience and precision to get it just right in your ear and it's impossible to forget it's there because it is heavy and never feels quite comfortable. Note that the manufacturer does include 3 ear gels but that really doesn't solve the problem of an awkward design.Per Jabra "Simply pick up the headset and put it on your ear to automatically answer the call". The concept is that they assume you don't have it in your ear until the phone rings (not sure where they expect it would be until then). At that point you pick it up and the motion of doing so answers the call. Meanwhile the person at the other end is yelling "hello, HELLO" while you fumble with the headset trying to get it into your ear during which it is impossible not to slide your finger across the motion-sensing volume control. Once you do get it into your ear the natural thing to do is to press it into the ear canal. Guess what? Doing so presses on the "call" button which hangs up the call. Nice.A "benefit" of the motion sensing is supposed to be how fast it answers the phone. Someone else here mentioned something like 2 seconds. Well what if someone is calling that you do not want to talk to and you jiggle the headset slightly and it automatically answers? Do you hang up on them?On a similar note, Jabra has a process to pair with your phone that involves holding the call button while simultaneously moving the mic boom at which point a voice will start talking in the earpiece with critical instructions on pairing the headset with your phone. Unfortunately the voice starts immediately and you have no idea it's happening until you hear little sounds in the distance and have to try to fit the headset to your ear, only to hear the last couple of seconds of the valuable instructions. There is no possible physical way to have the headset already mounted to your ear to begin the pairing process. Hello?!?! Did anyone at Jabra actually try this before releasing it to the public?Another design oddity is having the mute button right on the microphone pod where, in order to press it, you have to squeeze the microphone, a noise that is obvious to the person(s) you're talking to. It's nearly impossible to do a quick cough or take a sip of water without it being obvious to the other person. On the subject of the microphone, it sits flat against my face with the mic grill facing my cheek. The slightest facial hair makes it sounds to the caller like I am rubbing it with a wire brush. It would probably be unusable for someone with a full beard.Adding to the suspicion that no one at Jabra has actually tried to use one of their own products is this instruction from them "when you're done using it, simply fold the flip-boom arm". But that's impossible because the boom arm goes UNDER the lobe of humans who actually have ears. There is no possible way to fold it up when not in use without cutting your ear lobe off first.An engineer is more qualified than me to address the matter of one side of the mic pressing against the face. The theory of noise-cancelling mics is that both sides pick up sound and together they cancel out unwanted noise. It seems logical that if one side is blocked that there can be no noise cancelling. Is that just a design goof by Jabra or do they not really understand the theory?Because it is motion-activated, every time you move it, it turns itself on, decreasing battery life. In an average scenario of wearing it on your ear, carrying it in your pocket or car or anything other than sitting on a desk top potentially the power could be on 50% of the time or more. The only time it will turn itself completely off is if you don't touch it for 24 hours straight.How does it sound? That certainly is a make or break feature of any headset. Of the 4 Bluetooth headsets I own ranging in price from about $50 to this one at the high end at $200 list price, it sounds no better nor worse.Until Jabra understands how the products they make will be used in real life it's hard to take them seriously as a manufacturer of cell phone accessories. This one has too many negatives to make it worth considering.

The Jabra Motion Bluetooth headset with intelligent motion sensor technology, made in China, is designed to work with iOS and android Bluetooth enabled devices. Built in technologies include NFC pairing, an energy saving nap mode, noise blackout, and A2DP multimedia streaming.  The battery lasts for up to seven hours of talk time or fifteen hours of standby time.  multiple eargels and adjustable parts allow a personalized fit for every user.  The CONNECT iOS and android app customizes your headset to provide the best experience.The package includes the Jabra motion headset, an ultimate comfort eargel, an AC charger, a car charger, and the quick start manual.  Pairing was simple, and then required downloading apps for customizing.  From then on, the headset connected effortlessly when turned on.Voice controls cover basic commands.  For audio play back, I had to say "phone controls" to the Motion headset, then tell Siri to "play music."  Other accepted phrases include redial, call back, battery, pair new device, and cancel.  Pressing the call button during periods of inactivity will indicate if connected and the battery time left.  Pressing the voice button and saying battery will only provide the latter information.I climb stairs in a building to train for a charity hike to Yosemite in June 2013, and decided to use the headset to listen to podcasts while going up and down the numerous flights.  There were no issues to listening to the podcasts until my hair became sweaty.  Any moisture falling from my hair onto the volume pad caused the volume to quickly decrease to mute.  This isn't a situation I would consider to be normal, but it did point out a limitation if you plan on using the headset outdoors on a hot summer day.Then after two weeks of use, the Jabra Motion Bluetooth headset started to randomly and repeatedly turn off and on, typically several times a minute, for no apparent reason rendering the headset completely useless.  I contacted Jabra by email and was told the problem was likely due to a Bluetooth communications error, and I needed to reset the Motion headset and reconnect it to the phone.To reset the Jabra Motion, I was told to make sure the unit was powered off. Then press and hold the CALL and VOICE buttons simultaneously, and extend the microphone arm without releasing the buttons. Keep pressing the buttons for a few seconds until the battery light powers on white / purple. Release the buttons and the headset will get into pairing mode automatically, ready to connect.After doing this several times with no resolution, I contacted Jabra again.  This time I received no response.  Since I am required to submit Amazon Vine reviews within a short time period of receiving an item, I'm unable to pursue resolution any further to remain in compliance.  The product has failed, and Jabra has not responded to a request for assistance at any time in the last week.  If you're looking for a quality Bluetooth headset backed by a company ready to offer support, look elsewhere.PROS:Listen to music with A2DP multimedia streamingNoise blackout, dual microphones, and app aid in clearer communications1 year warrantyCONS:No direct voice commands to control audioFailed after two weeksJabra support unresponsive

If the Access Case were sturdy and protective and could easily be attached to a belt clip the whole system might be very practical. Unfortunately, the Access Case is flimsy, not protective and can't be clipped to my belt, at least using my existing holster.If I had lots of other devices I could throw down on the power brick I would then find it more useful... but all I've got is my iPhone 5. I'm not going to carry the phone around without a protective case and the Access Case is no good for that. Therefore, if I want to use this system to charge my phone I need to take off its case, put on the Access Case and put it on the brick (sold separatelyDuracell Powermat PM1PB1 TravelMat Portable Charger for USB Smartphones - Retail Packaging - Black). It is easier to just plug it in.More useful is just charging the SnapBattery, which comes with the Access Case, and slipping that onto to the Access Case. The Access Case isn't flimsy with the SnapBattery attached to it and your iPhone will last twice as long. The system (Access Case, phone and SnapBattery) seems to be a little slimmer that others, such as myCharge Freedom 2000 Power Case for iPhone 5, RFAM-0266 (Metal) or Belkin Grip Power Battery Case for iPhone 5 (Black). This might be good option if I'm out all day using heavily and have an available jacket pocket.I like the idea in concept. If the Access Case were sturdy and protective and could clip to my belt (with or without the SnapBattery) it would be a great system. As it is, assembling all the parts seems more trouble for everyday use than simply plugging in my phone for recharging.
It is not worth to spend $19 for this cheap simple stand. Better look for a better product than this.

Product exterior - White power cable is 31" long with a white Velcro for some traveling portability.  The unit itself is slightly larger than a deck of cards.  It is just heavy enough to stand upright even when several plugs are used.  It's a white glossy plastic finish that while it can get fingerprints, it's completely unnoticeable due to being white.One side has the power cable port and the other side has the 5 USB ports.Description of use - I simply plug in the power cable (which is not grounded) to wall outlet and the other end into the unit.  I take one glance at the port labels so that I can appropriately put my most powerful cables (Stock Samsung and Apple) into the iPad and Samsung Tab ports and plug in the other end to my iPads, Tablets, Galaxy S4, Android phones, Bluetooth headsets, Anker External Battery, and just about anything that is USB powered.  I will say that the PS3 Wireless controller does not charge with this unit and I notice those need specifically a computer USB or suitable chargers to properly charge.  It does simply work great.  The only thing is if you don't have enough microusb cables or USB cables you might want to pick up a few to take as much advantage as you can and charge many phones and accessories at once.  Also there is no buzzing sound to worry about and this is great overall quality.  Notable feature is the Serial number is stamped right on the white glossy side (opposite of Anker logo).Ideal users - everyone with a phone, accessories, or USB-seeking friends that need to charge their phones.Overall outstanding unit and I'm very impressed.If I had to change anything on this unit I would make it all black along with a black cable, have an optional fold out wall plug to go with the power cable option.  Also it's possible to cram in 8 USB ports if used on the long edge of the unit, it is a little awkward that the ports are all on the front short side but it works out fine.  Maybe in the future also a QI Wireless charging mat built in could work too.I was supplied a sample for test and evaluation, and I promised that my review is fair and honest

I've never heard of this brand before but I randomly found it while searching for a case for my Droid X.I have tried Otterbox for my Droid 2 and for my Xoom. I would say they offer the best protection of any case I've ever used. However, they add major bulk to the phone and the deal breaker for me is how the silicone part of the case is a magnet for hair and dust. It drives me crazy to have to wipe the case down every single time I take the phone out of my pocket.This is not as high quality as an Otterbox but it is perfect for me. I take very good care of my phones so I don't feel that it's necessary to spend $35 on a huge bulky case.This is very simple to apply. I suggest to not even take the silicone part out of the hard case while installing it. Just put one side in and move the silicone over that side, then do the other sides. It makes the fit all the better to not take the lining out like most people do.The silicone lining does NOT attract dust or hair. It stays clean. Another huge plus is that it is very easy to remove this case to access the battery if the phone freezes or to take the SD card out. Very simple and removing doesn't damage the case. With all other hard cases I've owned, you almost always break a clip off the case when you take the case off.Very close fit that adds hardly no bulk or weight to the phone. I love it and I may buy another color besides the black. I wish the case was black instead of grey but that's a petty complaint.So, yes, I totally recommend this case. Very fairly priced at around $10. Not as high quality as some cases but not generic, either. Has a smooth feel yet you can get a comfy grip on it and doesn't slide from your hands.If you are super active and jog or cycle or mountain climb or are generally hard on your phones and drop them often, get one of the huge bulky Otterbox cases. But this case is just perfect for me.Update: My original one came with a defect at the bottom of the case where the silicone insert meets the plastic case. It's like a little chip in the plastic. I ordered a replacement and the replacement had the exact same defect in the exact same spot and the silicone part snags on the little chip in the plastic.I was going to deduct a star but it's still such a good case for the price and so easy to take on and off that I left it at 5 stars. I do think the defect is something that other customers will notice, too. I kept the replacement because I figured the same thing would happen again. I used nail clippers to cut the little snag with and that helped--just be careful to not cut the silicone while you're at it.Also, the silicone is black but the plastic case is gray. I do wish the plastic was black, too, but that's just a personal preference of mine--not a big deal but it's hard to notice that in the pic so I thought I'd let you all know.

*****UPDATE*****After using my iPad for a week, I am downgrading my rating to 1 star.The way home button sticks up is proving to be really annoying in use.As I said below, it sits above the surface of the screen. Other complaints are that the home button mount and and front camera mount were both crooked, one small pc of the per-installed adhesive was crooked (and unusable).I had a nervous moment at the end of the install before the home button assembly finally clicked into place, but in the end it did, so I am happy to have a functional iPad again.I would have been happy to pay more for a better quality replacement screen, but couldn't find any clear winners judging by the reviews here.
